# Rare-word review: AI change suggestions (KEEP / REVISE)

*Generated by `scripts/28_split_proposals_for_review.py` from the batch proposal files.* Each entry is the agent's suggested replacement with rationale and full NEW verse text.

## accuser → adversary — Revelation 12:10
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Per the 2026-07-16 owner ruling, merely re-inflecting the flagged word ("accuser → accusers" or reverting to "accuser") is invalid — a genuinely different word is required, kept singular to preserve subject–verb agreement with "is cast down." "Adversary" is the KJV's own standing epithet for Satan in this same accusing role (1 Peter 5:8, "your adversary the devil... as a roaring lion, walketh about, seeking whom he may devour"), so it is period-authentic and theologically apt; the following "which accused them" still reads naturally with "adversary" as its antecedent.
- NEW: And I heard a loud voice saying in heaven, Now is come salvation, and strength, and the kingdom of our God, and the power of his Christ: for the adversary of our brethren is cast down, which accused them before our God day and night.

## accustomed → have learned — Jeremiah 13:23
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Owner ruling 2026-07-16 — "learned" here is a past participle, so the auxiliary must change with it: "are accustomed" → "have learned." Wycliffe ("han lerned yuel") and DRC ("have learned evil") both attest the *have* construction.
- NEW: Can the Ethiopian change his skin, or the leopard his spots? then may ye also do good, that have learned to do evil.

## acknowledgement → knowledge — Colossians 2:2
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: DRC's "unto the knowledge of the mystery of God" directly attests "knowledge" as the sense, and Geneva/ASV's "to know the mystery" confirms the meaning; the substitution is sound and grammatical.
- NEW: That their hearts might be comforted, being knit together in love, and unto all riches of the full assurance of understanding, to the knowledge of the mystery of God, and of the Father, and of Christ;

## acquainted → knowest — Psalms 139:3
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "art perceived with all my ways" reverses the agency — it is God who knows the psalmist's ways, not the reverse — and "perceived with" is not idiomatic. BBE's "have knowledge of all my ways" confirms the sense of intimate knowing; "knowest," a common KJV verb form already used earlier in the same verse's construction pattern ("Thou compassest... and knowest"), restores both grammar and meaning.
- NEW: Thou compassest my path and my lying down, and knowest all my ways.

## acquainted → familiar with — Isaiah 53:3
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Perceived with grief" is not idiomatic English in any register. Geneva's "hath experience of infirmities" and the general sense (one who has known grief intimately) are best carried by "familiar with," a common word that keeps the same preposition pattern as the original "acquainted with."
- NEW: He is despised and rejected of men; a man of sorrows, and familiar with grief: and we hid as it were our faces from him; he was despised, and we esteemed him not.

## acquainting → guiding — Ecclesiastes 2:3
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Driving mine heart with wisdom" is obscure and ill-fitting; ASV, BBE, ACV, and BSB all render this "guiding," a common word that clearly conveys wisdom's governance over the seeker's pursuit of pleasure.
- NEW: I sought in mine heart to give myself unto wine, yet guiding mine heart with wisdom; and to lay hold on folly, till I might see what was that good for the sons of men, which they should do under the heaven all the days of their life.

## acquit → clear — Job 10:14
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Thou wilt not innocent me" misuses "innocent" as a verb, which is not standard usage even in Early Modern English. Geneva's "wilt not holde mee giltlesse" and BBE's "would not make me clear from sin" support "clear," a common verb (used elsewhere in the KJV corpus, including the parallel Nahum 1:3 entry below) that fits the grammar exactly.
- NEW: If I sin, then thou markest me, and thou wilt not clear me from mine iniquity.

## acquit → clear — Nahum 1:3
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Darby and ASV both render this "will/doth not at all clear [the guilty]," an exact structural match; "clear" is common, grammatical, and requires no other change to the verse.
- NEW: The Lord is slow to anger, and great in power, and will not at all clear the wicked: the Lord hath his way in the whirlwind and in the storm, and the clouds are the dust of his feet.

## acre → rood — I Samuel 14:14
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Per the 2026-07-16 owner ruling, "acre" is common in modern English but rare within the KJV corpus itself, so it must go rather than merely fix CUR's broken "an half furrows of land." "Rood" is a genuine period English land measure (a quarter-acre, in ordinary agricultural use in Early Modern England) naming the same kind of small parcel a yoke of oxen might plow, and it slots into the identical "half a ___ of land" frame without further rephrasing.
- NEW: And that first slaughter, which Jonathan and his armourbearer made, was about twenty men, within as it were an half rood of land, which a yoke of oxen might plow.

## acres → roods — Isaiah 5:10
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Per the 2026-07-16 owner ruling, "acres" must also be replaced, not kept, despite being common in modern English. "Roods" (see the parallel I Samuel 14:14 entry above) is the same genuine period land-measure word, distinct from "acre," and drops into the sentence without further rephrasing; CUR's "yokes" is rejected as confusing since a "yoke" properly names a team of oxen, not an area, and would clash with the yoke-of-oxen image already used elsewhere in this batch.
- NEW: Yea, ten roods of vineyard shall yield one bath, and the seed of an homer shall yield an ephah.

## actions → doings — I Samuel 2:3
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Doings" is a common, well-attested KJV word (e.g. "your doings" elsewhere in the corpus) and preserves the sense that all human deeds are weighed by God; grammar is unaffected.
- NEW: Talk no more so exceeding proudly; let not arrogancy come out of your mouth: for the Lord is a God of knowledge, and by him doings are weighed.

## activity → able men — Genesis 47:6
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Men of army" is nonsensical. RWebster and ASV both render this "able men," a clear, common phrase confirmed also by DRC's "industrious men" and BSB's "talented men."
- NEW: The land of Egypt is before thee; in the best of the land make thy father and brethren to dwell; in the land of Goshen let them dwell: and if thou knowest any able men among them, then make them rulers over my cattle.

## adamant → diamond — Ezekiel 3:9
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: The word choice is fine (BBE and BSB both render "diamond"), but CUR's article is wrong ("an diamond" before a consonant). Only the article needs correction.
- NEW: As a diamond harder than flint have I made thy forehead: fear them not, neither be dismayed at their looks, though they be a rebellious house.

## adamant → diamond — Zechariah 7:12
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same article error as Ezekiel 3:9 ("an diamond stone"); the word itself is acceptable (compare BBE's "hardest stone" and BSB's use of "flint" for the same image), but the indefinite article must be "a" before a consonant.
- NEW: Yea, they made their hearts as a diamond stone, lest they should hear the law, and the words which the Lord of hosts hath sent in his spirit by the former prophets: therefore came a great wrath from the Lord of hosts.

## addicted → devoted — I Corinthians 16:15
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Appointed themselves" implies external assignment, but the sense is self-giving wholehearted service. RWebster and Darby both render this "devoted themselves," a common word that captures the meaning of "addicted themselves to the ministry" precisely.
- NEW: I beseech you, brethren, (ye know the house of Stephanas, that it is the firstfruits of Achaia, and that they have devoted themselves to the ministry of the saints,)

## addition → wreath — I Kings 7:30
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "at the side of every certain" is nonsense — "certain" was left as a bare adjective with no noun. ASV and ACV both render this "wreaths... at the side of each," and Darby has "garland"; "wreath" is common and fits the architectural ornament being described.
- NEW: And every base had four brasen wheels, and plates of brass: and the four corners thereof had undersetters: under the laver were undersetters molten, at the side of every wreath.

## additions → ornaments — I Kings 7:29
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Ornaments" is common, grammatical, and matches the general sense confirmed by ASV's "wreaths" and BBE's "steps" for the same decorative feature.
- NEW: And on the borders that were between the ledges were lions, oxen, and cherubims: and upon the ledges there was a base above: and beneath the lions and oxen were certain ornaments made of thin work.

## additions → ornaments — I Kings 7:36
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Ornaments round about" is sound, matching DRC's "added round about" and ASV's "wreaths round about" for the same engraved decoration.
- NEW: For on the plates of the ledges thereof, and on the borders thereof, he graved cherubims, lions, and palm trees, according to the proportion of every one, and ornaments round about.

## administered → ministered — II Corinthians 8:19
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Wycliffe, Geneva, YLT, and Darby all render this "ministered," a common word already frequent in the KJV corpus, with no loss of meaning.
- NEW: And not that only, but who was also chosen of the churches to travel with us with this grace, which is ministered by us to the glory of the same Lord, and declaration of your ready mind:

## administered → ministered — II Corinthians 8:20
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Wycliffe, Geneva, and YLT support "ministered" as an equally valid, more common rendering alongside the witnesses that keep "administered."
- NEW: Avoiding this, that no man should blame us in this abundance which is ministered by us:

## administration → ministration — II Corinthians 9:12
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Geneva, YLT, Darby, and ASV all render this "ministration," an authentic KJV-family word (compare its use in 2 Corinthians 3) that is more familiar than "administration" in this liturgical sense.
- NEW: For the ministration of this service not only supplieth the want of the saints, but is abundant also by many thanksgivings unto God;

## administrations → ministries — I Corinthians 12:5
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "differences of ministers" wrongly shifts the sense from types of service to persons who serve. Wycliffe and DRC both render this "diversities of ministries," which preserves the original meaning (varieties of service, one Lord) with a common word.
- NEW: And there are differences of ministries, but the same Lord.

## admiration → flattering — Jude 1:16
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "having men's persons in marvel" is an awkward, unclear construction. BSB's "they flatter others for their own advantage" captures the sense precisely — showing favoritism toward persons for gain — with a common, unmistakable verb.
- NEW: These are murmurers, complainers, walking after their own lusts; and their mouth speaketh great swelling words, flattering men's persons because of advantage.

## admiration → marvelled greatly — Revelation 17:6
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Owner ruling 2026-07-17 — multi-word change aligning with MEV, ESV, RSV, and RSVCE; the agent's "wondered with great wonder" was duplicative. Spelled "marvelled" per KJV corpus convention (30+ uses; "marveled" has 0) — flip to single-l if modern spelling is preferred.
- NEW: And I saw the woman drunken with the blood of the saints, and with the blood of the martyrs of Jesus: and when I saw her, I marvelled greatly.

## admired → marvelled at — II Thessalonians 1:10
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR drops the required preposition ("to be marvelled in all them" should be "marvelled at"), as shown by YLT, Darby, and ASV, all of which use "wondered/marvelled AT." The single-word swap needs "at" restored for correct grammar.
- NEW: When he shall come to be glorified in his saints, and to be marvelled at in all them that believe (because our testimony among you was believed) in that day.

## ado → tumult — Mark 5:39
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Webster, RWebster, YLT, Darby, and ASV all render this "tumult," a common and well-attested word for the commotion of mourners.
- NEW: And when he was come in, he saith unto them, Why make ye this tumult, and weep? the damsel is not dead, but sleepeth.

## adventure → durst — Deuteronomy 28:56
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR ("would not gave to set") is ungrammatical. "Durst" (dared) is a well-attested KJV modal (Job 32:6, John 21:12 "durst not ask"), and the negative "durst not" construction is the corpus's own idiom for reluctance to act, matching the sense of Geneva's "would neuer venture." Rephrased per rule 1 to drop the broken "would" auxiliary.
- NEW: The tender and delicate woman among you, which durst not set the sole of her foot upon the ground for delicateness and tenderness, her eye shall be evil toward the husband of her bosom, and toward her son, and toward her daughter,

## adventure → durst — Acts 19:31
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR ("would not gave himself") is broken. "Durst not enter" carries the same reluctance-to-risk-oneself sense as DRC's "venture himself" and is attested KJV idiom (durst not — Job 32:6, John 21:12, Exodus 3:6 "durst not behold"). "Venture" itself occurs in the KJV corpus only as the noun phrase "at a venture" (2 Kings), never as a verb, so it was rejected under rule 4.
- NEW: And certain of the chief of Asia, which were his friends, sent unto him, desiring him that he durst not enter into the theatre.

## adventured → jeoparded — Judges 9:17
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR ("plucked his life far") is broken and nonsensical. "Jeoparded... life" is the KJV's own idiom for risking one's life in battle, attested verbatim at Judges 5:18 "jeoparded their lives unto the death" — an especially strong match since it is the same book.
- NEW: (For my father fought for you, and jeoparded his life far, and delivered you out of the hand of Midian:

## advertise → counsel — Numbers 24:14
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR ("I will away thee") is ungrammatical. "Counsel" as a transitive verb ("I counsel thee") is well attested in the KJV corpus (Revelation 3:18, 2 Chronicles 10:8) and matches Wycliffe's "yyue counsel to thee" and DRC's "give thee counsel."
- NEW: And now, behold, I go unto my people: come therefore, and I will counsel thee what this people shall do to thy people in the latter days.

## advertise → tell — Ruth 4:4
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR ("I thought to away thee") is ungrammatical. "Tell" is one of the most common verbs in the KJV corpus and fits the construction directly, matching RWebster's own resolution of this verse ("I thought to tell thee, saying...").
- NEW: And I thought to tell thee, saying, Buy it before the inhabitants, and before the elders of my people. If thou wilt redeem it, redeem it: but if thou wilt not redeem it, then tell me, that I may know: for there is none to redeem it beside thee; and I am after thee. And he said, I will redeem it.

## advisement → counsel — I Chronicles 12:19
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR already swaps in a genuinely different, very common KJV word ("counsel"); "upon counsel sent him away" is sound grammar and matches YLT's "by counsel."
- NEW: And there fell some of Manasseh to David, when he came with the Philistines against Saul to battle: but they helped them not: for the lords of the Philistines upon counsel sent him away, saying, He will fall to his master Saul to the jeopardy of our heads.

## advocate → comforter — I John 2:1
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR already swaps to a genuinely different, well-attested KJV word ("comforter," 8x — and notably the very word John himself uses for the same underlying Greek paraklētos at John 14:16, making it an authentically Johannine choice), but the article is wrong: "an comforter" must be "a comforter."
- NEW: My little children, these things write I unto you, that ye sin not. And if any man sin, we have a comforter with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous:

## affect → envy — Galatians 4:17
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR already swaps in a genuinely different, common KJV word ("envy," 20x) that substitutes cleanly into both clauses of the verse without breaking grammar, and is close in sense to Geneva's "ielous."
- NEW: They zealously envy you, but not well; yea, they would exclude you, that ye might envy them.

## affected → bitter — Acts 14:2
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "made their minds evil moved" is awkward — "moved" does not take the "evil" prefix naturally. Rephrased per rule 1 to "made their minds bitter against the brethren," using the common KJV word "bitter" (38x) and matching BBE's "made the minds... bitter."
- NEW: But the unbelieving Jews stirred up the Gentiles, and made their minds bitter against the brethren.

## affected → zealous — Galatians 4:18
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "zealously moved" is stilted. Dropping "affected" entirely and using the common KJV word "zealous" (8x, e.g. Titus 2:14) matches Darby's "right to be zealous... at all times" and reads as authentic KJV cadence.
- NEW: But it is good to be zealous always in a good thing, and not only when I am present with you.

## affecteth → breaketh — Lamentations 3:51
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "defileth" is semantically wrong (the eye does not defile the heart; it grieves it). Geneva reads "breaketh mine heart" for this very verse, and "breaketh" is common KJV usage for grief (Psalm 51:17, "a broken... heart").
- NEW: Mine eye breaketh mine heart because of all the daughters of my city.

## affectionately → earnestly — I Thessalonians 2:8
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR already swaps in a genuinely different, very common KJV word ("earnestly"); "earnestly desirous of you" reads naturally and needs no further change.
- NEW: So being affectionately desirous of you, we were willing to have imparted unto you, not the gospel of God only, but also our own souls, because ye were dear unto us.

## affording → yielding — Psalms 144:13
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "getting all manner of store" is semantically off (the garners do not "get" store, they hold and give it out). "Yielding" is common KJV usage for produce/store (Genesis 1:11-12, "yielding seed... yielding fruit") and preserves the participial construction.
- NEW: That our garners may be full, yielding all manner of store: that our sheep may bring forth thousands and ten thousands in our streets:

## affright → terrify — II Chronicles 32:18
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "to afraid them" is ungrammatical ("afraid" is not a verb). "Terrify" is attested in the KJV corpus (4x) and matches YLT/Darby/ACV/BSB directly for this verse, without repeating "trouble" which already appears later in the same clause.
- NEW: Then they cried with a loud voice in the Jews' speech unto the people of Jerusalem that were on the wall, to terrify them, and to trouble them; that they might take the city.

## afoot → on foot — Mark 6:33
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "ran foot thither" drops the needed preposition and is ungrammatical. "On foot" is the phrase used by Webster, RWebster, Darby, ASV, and UKJV for this very verse; a phrase substitution is permitted under rule 1.
- NEW: And the people saw them departing, and many knew him, and ran on foot thither out of all cities, and outwent them, and came together unto him.

## afoot → on foot — Acts 20:13
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "go foot" is ungrammatical. "On foot" is the phrase used by Webster, RWebster, YLT, and Darby for this verse.
- NEW: And we went before to ship, and sailed unto Assos, there intending to take in Paul: for so had he appointed, minding himself to go on foot.

## aforehand → beforehand — Mark 14:8
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR already swaps in a genuinely different word ("beforehand," attested 6x in the corpus, e.g. 1 Timothy 5:24) with grammar intact, matching Webster, DRC, and RWebster verbatim for this verse.
- NEW: She hath done what she could: she is come beforehand to anoint my body to the burying.

## afresh → again — Hebrews 6:6
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR repeats "crucify" twice, breaking the sentence. "Again" is extremely common in the KJV corpus and matches Geneva's and DRC's own rendering of this verse ("crucifie againe" / "crucifying again").
- NEW: If they shall fall away, to renew them again unto repentance; seeing they crucify to themselves the Son of God again, and put him to an open shame.

## afternoon → declined — Judges 19:8
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "tarried until day" is senseless in context (the day had already begun). "Declined" (of the day) is attested KJV usage in the sense of time passing, and this rendering follows the sense of ASV/ACV "until the day declineth."
- NEW: And he arose early in the morning on the fifth day to depart: and the damsel's father said, Comfort thine heart, I pray thee. And they tarried until the day declined, and they did eat both of them.

## agone → ago — I Samuel 30:13
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR already swaps in a genuinely different, extremely common word ("ago"), matching DRC, Webster, Darby, ASV, and UKJV for this verse.
- NEW: And David said unto him, To whom belongest thou? and whence art thou? And he said, I am a young man of Egypt, servant to an Amalekite; and my master left me, because three days ago I fell sick.

## agony → anguish — Luke 22:44
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR already swaps in a genuinely different, common KJV word ("anguish"), but "an anguish" is bad grammar — "anguish" is not used with the indefinite article elsewhere in the corpus. Corrected to "in anguish," matching BSB's "in His anguish."
- NEW: And being in anguish he prayed more earnestly: and his sweat was as it were great drops of blood falling down to the ground.

## aground → thrust in — Acts 27:41
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR repeats "ran" twice ("ran the ship ran"), breaking the sentence, and simply restoring "aground" would violate rule 3 (the flagged word must not return). "Thrust in the ship" is the construction used by both Tyndale and Geneva for this verse, and "thrust" is well attested in the corpus (50x).
- NEW: And falling into a place where two seas met, they thrust in the ship; and the forepart stuck fast, and remained unmoveable, but the hinder part was broken with the violence of the waves.

## ague → fever — Leviticus 26:16
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR already swaps in a genuinely different, attested KJV word ("fever," 9x, e.g. Matthew 8:14), and the grammar and construction are unchanged.
- NEW: I also will do this unto you; I will even appoint over you terror, consumption, and the burning fever, that shall consume the eyes, and cause sorrow of heart: and ye shall sow your seed in vain, for your enemies shall eat it.

## aided → helped — Judges 9:24
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "repaired him in the killing" is semantically wrong (repaired does not mean assisted). "Helped" is extremely common in the KJV corpus and matches the sense of Wycliffe's "hadde helpid hym" and DRC's "aided him."
- NEW: That the cruelty done to the threescore and ten sons of Jerubbaal might come, and their blood be laid upon Abimelech their brother, which slew them; and upon the men of Shechem, which helped him in the killing of his brethren.

## ailed → troubled — Psalms 114:5
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR already swaps in a genuinely different, common KJV word ("troubled") that reads naturally in the interrogative construction and preserves the parallelism with the following clause.
- NEW: What troubled thee, O thou sea, that thou fleddest? thou Jordan, that thou wast driven back?

## albeit → although — Ezekiel 13:7
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR already swaps in a genuinely different, extremely common KJV word ("although"), with grammar unchanged and sense intact.
- NEW: Have ye not seen a vain vision, and have ye not spoken a lying divination, whereas ye say, The Lord saith it; although I have not spoken?

## albeit → although — Philemon 1:19
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR already swaps in a genuinely different, extremely common KJV word ("although"), with grammar unchanged and sense intact.
- NEW: I Paul have written it with mine own hand, I will repay it: although I do not say to thee how thou owest unto me even thine own self besides.

## alienate → give away — Ezekiel 48:14
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Owner ruling 2026-07-17 — CUR's "nor through the firstfruits" is ungrammatical ("through" is not a verb). Giving away is very common in the KJV compared to convey.  A word that should also probably be replaced
- NEW: And they shall not sell of it, neither exchange, nor give away the firstfruits of the land: for it is holy unto the Lord.

## alleging → shewing — Acts 17:3
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "Opening and keeping" is semantically wrong (Paul was demonstrating from scripture, not keeping anything). "Shewing" is extremely common in the KJV corpus (17x) and fits the sense of "opening and shewing" — laying open the scriptures and proving his point, matching the sense of Darby's "laying down" and BSB's "proving."
- NEW: Opening and shewing, that Christ must needs have suffered, and risen again from the dead; and that this Jesus, whom I preach unto you, is Christ.

## allegory → figure — Galatians 4:24
*(from batch_0003_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR already swaps in a genuinely different, common KJV word ("figure," used elsewhere for typological meaning at Hebrews 9:9 and 1 Peter 3:21), but the article is wrong: "an figure" must be "a figure."
- NEW: Which things are a figure: for these are the two covenants; the one from the mount Sinai, which gendereth to bondage, which is Agar.

## allied → joined — Nehemiah 13:4
*(from batch_0003_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR already swaps in a genuinely different, common KJV word ("joined"); grammar and sense are sound and need no further change.
- NEW: And before this, Eliashib the priest, having the oversight of the chamber of the house of our God, was joined unto Tobiah:

## allowance → portion — II Kings 25:30
*(from batch_0003_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "his dinner was a continual dinner" is semantically narrow (allowance covers more than a meal) and awkward repeated. "Portion" is the word Geneva, Darby, and ASV all converge on for this verse, and it is common in the KJV corpus (114x, e.g. "portion of goods," Luke 15:12).
- NEW: And his portion was a continual portion given him of the king, a daily rate for every day, all the days of his life.

## allure → entice — Hosea 2:14
*(from batch_0003_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR already swaps in a genuinely different, attested KJV word ("entice," e.g. Proverbs 1:10), matching YLT's "enticing" for this verse.
- NEW: Therefore, behold, I will entice her, and bring her into the wilderness, and speak comfortably unto her.

## allure → entice — II Peter 2:18
*(from batch_0003_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "persuade" sits awkwardly with the "through the lusts" construction. "Entice" (attested, Proverbs 1:10) is the word ASV, YLT, and Darby converge on for this verse and reads more naturally with the same construction, also keeping consistency with the Hosea 2:14 entry above for the same English headword.
- NEW: For when they speak great swelling words of vanity, they entice through the lusts of the flesh, through much wantonness, those that were clean escaped from them who live in error.

## almsdeeds → alms — Acts 9:36
*(from batch_0003_proposals.md)*

## aloof → aside — Psalms 38:11
*(from batch_0003_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "stand stood" duplicates the verb and is ungrammatical. "Aside" is the word Geneva uses for this very verse ("stand aside from my plague") and is common in the KJV corpus (74x).
- NEW: My lovers and my friends stand aside from my sore; and my kinsmen stand afar off.

## amazement → astonishment — Acts 3:10
*(from batch_0003_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "trance" is semantically wrong (a trance is an altered state of consciousness, not astonishment) and reads oddly doubled with "wonder." "Astonishment" is common in the KJV corpus (21x) and matches the sense of Tyndale's "astonnyed" for this verse.
- NEW: And they knew that it was he which sat for alms at the Beautiful gate of the temple: and they were filled with wonder and astonishment at that which had happened unto him.

## amazement → terror — I Peter 3:6
*(from batch_0003_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR already swaps in a genuinely different, attested KJV word ("terror"), matching Geneva, YLT, and RWebster for this very verse.
- NEW: Even as Sara obeyed Abraham, calling him lord: whose daughters ye are, as long as ye do well, and are not afraid with any terror.

## ambassage → ambassadors — Luke 14:32
*(from batch_0003_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "sendeth an ambassador" narrows the sense to a single person where the original names a mission/legation. "Ambassadors" (attested, 8x, e.g. Joshua 9:4) matches the plural-legation sense found in Webster's "embassy" and UKJV's "embassy," using a word actually attested in the corpus (embassy itself has zero occurrences).
- NEW: Or else, while the other is yet a great way off, he sendeth ambassadors, and desireth conditions of peace.

## ambushment → liers in wait — II Chronicles 13:13
*(from batch_0003_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "ambush" is a mere re-inflection of the flagged word "ambushment" (same lemma), which rule 3 forbids. "Liers in wait" is the genuinely different, period-attested KJV phrase (Joshua 8:9,12) for the same concept, and keeps this entry consistent with the Jeremiah 51:12 and 2 Chronicles 20:22 entries in this batch.
- NEW: But Jeroboam caused liers in wait to come about behind them: so they were before Judah, and the liers in wait were behind them.

## ambushments → liers in wait — II Chronicles 20:22
*(from batch_0003_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's bare "liers" is an incomplete phrase. Adding "in wait" completes the attested KJV idiom exactly as rendered by ASV and Darby for this very verse.
- NEW: And when they began to sing and to praise, the Lord set liers in wait against the children of Ammon, Moab, and mount Seir, which were come against Judah; and they were smitten.

## amerce → charge — Deuteronomy 22:19
*(from batch_0003_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Owner Change 7/17/26 - Fine is not a good replacement as a Fee or Fine was not used in this manner.  Charge is more common for a fee rendered.  Fine is a word typically used to describe quality.  IE. Fine Gold, Fine Linen, or anything used by the wealthy.
- NEW: And they shall charge him in an hundred shekels of silver, and give them unto the father of the damsel, because he hath brought up an evil name upon a virgin of Israel: and she shall be his wife; he may not put her away all his days.

## amongst → midst — Genesis 3:8
*(from batch_0003_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "midst the trees" drops the required preposition and article. "In the midst of the trees" completes the construction, matching YLT and Darby exactly for this verse.
- NEW: And they heard the voice of the Lord God walking in the garden in the cool of the day: and Adam and his wife hid themselves from the presence of the Lord God in the midst of the trees of the garden.

## amongst → midst — Genesis 23:9
*(from batch_0003_proposals.md)*

*(from batch_0003_proposals.md)*

## antiquity → beginning — Isaiah 23:7
*(from batch_0004_proposals.md)*

- NEW: Is this your joyous city, whose beginning is of ancient days? her own feet shall carry her afar off to sojourn.

## apparently → plainly — Numbers 12:8
*(from batch_0004_proposals.md)*

## appeal → call upon — Acts 25:11
*(from batch_0004_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's bare "I call unto Cesar" reads as a literal shout rather than a legal invocation. "Call upon" is the KJV's own idiom for solemn invocation (e.g. "call upon the name of the LORD," Genesis 4:26 and throughout), and adding "upon" gives sound period grammar while keeping CUR's word choice.
- NEW: For if I be an offender, or have committed any thing worthy of death, I refuse not to die: but if there be none of these things whereof these accuse me, no man may deliver me unto them. I call upon Cesar.

## appeal → call upon — Acts 28:19
*(from batch_0004_proposals.md)*

## appease → pacify — Genesis 32:20
*(from batch_0004_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "I will atonement him" uses "atonement" as a verb, which the KJV never does. "Pacify" is the word YLT uses for this very verse ("I pacify his face") and is attested in the KJV corpus.
- NEW: And say ye moreover, Behold, thy servant Jacob is behind us. For he said, I will pacify him with the present that goeth before me, and afterward I will see his face; peradventure he will accept of me.

## appeased → pacified — Esther 2:1
*(from batch_0004_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "was ceased" is bad grammar ("ceased" is intransitive and does not pair naturally with "was" in this sense). "Pacified" is attested in the KJV corpus and matches ASV's own rendering of this verse ("was pacified").
- NEW: After these things, when the wrath of king Ahasuerus was pacified, he remembered Vashti, and what she had done, and what was decreed against her.

## appeased → quieted — Acts 19:35
*(from batch_0004_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "had ceased the people" is ungrammatical ("cease" cannot take a direct object — one cannot "cease" a person). "Quieted" is attested in the KJV corpus and matches RWebster's, YLT's, ASV's, and Darby's own convergent rendering of this verse.
- NEW: And when the townclerk had quieted the people, he said, Ye men of Ephesus, what man is there that knoweth not how that the city of the Ephesians is a worshipper of the great goddess Diana, and of the image which fell down from Jupiter?

## appeaseth → stilleth — Proverbs 15:18
*(from batch_0004_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "giveth strife" is semantically backwards — it says the patient man causes strife rather than ending it. "Stilleth" is attested in the KJV corpus in the same calming sense (Psalm 65:7, "which stilleth the noise of the seas") and matches BBE's "puts an end to fighting."
- NEW: A wrathful man stirreth up strife: but he that is slow to anger stilleth strife.

## appertain → belong — Numbers 16:30
*(from batch_0004_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "with all that thee unto them" is nonsense (uses "thee" as a verb). "Belong" is attested in the KJV corpus and matches DRC's rendering of this verse directly ("all things that belong to them").
- NEW: But if the Lord make a new thing, and the earth open her mouth, and swallow them up, with all that belong unto them, and they go down quick into the pit; then ye shall understand that these men have provoked the Lord.

## appertain → belong — Jeremiah 10:7
*(from batch_0004_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "for to thee doth it thee" is nonsense (repeats "thee" as a verb). "Belong" is attested in the KJV corpus and gives clean grammar matching the sense of DRC's "thine is the glory."
- NEW: Who would not fear thee, O King of nations? for to thee doth it belong: forasmuch as among all the wise men of the nations, and in all their kingdoms, there is none like unto thee.

## appertaineth → belongeth — Leviticus 6:5
*(from batch_0004_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "to whom it remaineth" is an imprecise fit (remaineth suggests persistence, not ownership). "Belongeth" is attested in the KJV corpus (23x) and matches Darby's own rendering of this verse ("to whom it belongeth").
- NEW: Or all that about which he hath sworn falsely; he shall even restore it in the principal, and shall add the fifth part more thereto, and give it unto him to whom it belongeth, in the day of his trespass offering.

## appertaineth → belongeth — II Chronicles 26:18
*(from batch_0004_proposals.md)*
