## asswage → relieve — Job 16:5
*(from batch_0005_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "should back your grief" is an ambiguous, unclear verb choice. "Relieve" is attested in the KJV corpus (7x) and matches RWebster's own rendering of this exact verse.
- NEW: But I would strengthen you with my mouth, and the moving of my lips should relieve your grief.

## asswaged → abated — Genesis 8:1
*(from batch_0005_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "the waters spared" is semantically wrong (spared does not mean subsided). "Abated" is attested in the KJV corpus (6x) and matches RWebster's own rendering of this exact verse.
- NEW: And God remembered Noah, and every living thing, and all the cattle that was with him in the ark: and God made a wind to pass over the earth, and the waters abated;

## asswaged → restrained — Job 16:6
*(from batch_0005_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR already swaps in a genuinely different, common KJV word ("restrained"), matching YLT verbatim for this verse.
- NEW: Though I speak, my grief is not restrained: and though I forbear, what am I eased?

## astrologer → enchanter — Daniel 2:10
*(from batch_0005_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "astrologers" is merely the plural of the flagged word "astrologer" (same lemma), which rule 3 forbids, and it also breaks number agreement with the surrounding singular list ("any magician, or astrologers, or Chaldean"). "Enchanter" is attested in the KJV corpus (Deuteronomy 18:11) and matches ASV's and ACV's own rendering of this exact verse, restoring singular agreement.
- NEW: The Chaldeans answered before the king, and said, There is not a man upon the earth that can shew the king's matter: therefore there is no king, lord, nor ruler, that asked such things at any magician, or enchanter, or Chaldean.

## attent → attentive — II Chronicles 6:40
*(from batch_0006_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR already swaps in a genuinely different, common KJV word ("attentive"), matching DRC, Webster, RWebster, YLT, Darby, UKJV, and BSB for this verse.
- NEW: Now, my God, let, I beseech thee, thine eyes be open, and let thine ears be attentive unto the prayer that is made in this place.

## attent → attentive — II Chronicles 7:15
*(from batch_0006_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same reasoning as 2 Chronicles 6:40 above — "attentive" is already a genuinely different, common, well-attested word.
- NEW: Now mine eyes shall be open, and mine ears attentive unto the prayer that is made in this place.

## attentively → diligently — Job 37:2
*(from batch_0006_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR already swaps in a genuinely different, common KJV word ("diligently"), matching YLT's "Hearken diligently" for this verse.
- NEW: Hear diligently the noise of his voice, and the sound that goeth out of his mouth.

## augment → add — Numbers 32:14
*(from batch_0006_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "to add yet the fierce anger" is missing a preposition ("add" needs "to"). Adding "to" completes the construction, matching YLT's own rendering of this exact verse ("to add yet to the fury").
- NEW: And, behold, ye are risen up in your fathers' stead, an increase of sinful men, to add yet to the fierce anger of the Lord toward Israel.

## aunt → kinswoman — Leviticus 18:14
*(from batch_0006_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "she is thine sister" is a serious content error — this verse concerns the father's brother's wife (an aunt by marriage), not a sister, a wholly different forbidden relation elsewhere in this same chapter. "Kinswoman" is attested in the KJV corpus (3x) and, while more general than "aunt," correctly preserves the sense of a close relative by marriage without introducing the wrong relation. Article corrected to "thy" before the consonant sound.
- NEW: Thou shalt not uncover the nakedness of thy father's brother, thou shalt not approach to his wife: she is thy kinswoman.

## austere → hard — Luke 19:21
*(from batch_0006_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR already swaps in a genuinely different, common word ("hard," matching BBE exactly), but the article is wrong: "an hard man" must be "a hard man."
- NEW: For I feared thee, because thou art a hard man: thou takest up that thou layedst not down, and reapest that thou didst not sow.

## austere → hard — Luke 19:22
*(from batch_0006_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same article error as Luke 19:21 above — "an hard man" corrected to "a hard man," keeping CUR's word choice.
- NEW: And he saith unto him, Out of thine own mouth will I judge thee, thou wicked servant. Thou knewest that I was a hard man, taking up that I laid not down, and reaping that I did not sow:

## averse → return — Micah 2:8
*(from batch_0006_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR already swaps in a genuinely different word ("return"); the sense (catching men off guard, as though just returned from war) matches Geneva's "as though they returned from the warre" and YLT's "turning back from war" for this exact verse.
- NEW: Even of late my people is risen up as an enemy: ye pull off the robe with the garment from them that pass by securely as men return from war.

## avouched → proclaimed — Deuteronomy 26:17
*(from batch_0006_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "answered the LORD" is semantically wrong (does not convey solemn declaration/choosing). "Proclaimed" is attested in the KJV corpus (16x) and matches RWebster's own rendering of this exact verse.
- NEW: Thou hast proclaimed the Lord this day to be thy God, and to walk in his ways, and to keep his statutes, and his commandments, and his judgments, and to hearken unto his voice:

## avouched → proclaimed — Deuteronomy 26:18
*(from batch_0006_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "hath caused thee... to be" is an odd fit for a solemn declaration. "Proclaimed" is attested in the KJV corpus and matches RWebster's own rendering of this exact verse, keeping the pair of consecutive verses (26:17-18) consistent.
- NEW: And the Lord hath proclaimed thee this day to be his peculiar people, as he hath promised thee, and that thou shouldest keep all his commandments;

## await → wait — Acts 9:24
*(from batch_0006_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "laying wait" is not a mere re-inflection but the KJV's own established idiom — "laying wait" and "lying in wait" both occur repeatedly in the corpus (3x and 5x respectively, e.g. Acts 20:3, 23:30), so this is genuine, attested period phrasing rather than a bare prefix-drop.
- NEW: But their laying wait was known of Saul. And they watched the gates day and night to kill him.

## axletrees → axles — I Kings 7:32
*(from batch_0006_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "the yourselves of the wheels" is complete nonsense. "Axles" is the modern-equivalent technical term for this exact part and matches RWebster's own rendering of this verse; note it has zero occurrences elsewhere in the KJV corpus (the flagged word "axletrees" itself occurs only twice, both in this pair of verses), so this is flagged for owner awareness even though no better-attested alternative exists.
- NEW: And under the borders were four wheels; and the axles of the wheels were joined to the base: and the height of a wheel was a cubit and half a cubit.

## axletrees → axles — I Kings 7:33
*(from batch_0006_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same reasoning as 1 Kings 7:32 above (the immediately following verse) — "axles" matches RWebster's own rendering, with the same zero-corpus caveat noted there.
- NEW: And the work of the wheels was like the work of a chariot wheel: their axles, and their naves, and their felloes, and their spokes, were all molten.

## backbone → chine — Leviticus 3:9
*(from batch_0006_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Chine" is a genuine period English butchery term for the backbone (still in use, "chine of beef/pork"), predating 1611 easily, and is genuinely different from "backbone." It has zero occurrences in the KJV corpus itself, so this is flagged for owner awareness, but no witness or corpus alternative exists for this specific anatomical/culinary term.
- NEW: And he shall offer of the sacrifice of the peace offering an offering made by fire unto the Lord; the fat thereof, and the whole rump, it shall he take off hard by the chine; and the fat that covereth the inwards, and all the fat that is upon the inwards,

## backslider → him that turneth away — Proverbs 14:14
*(from batch_0006_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "The back in heart" is nonsensical (a "back" is not a person). "Him that turneth away" is a genuinely different phrase, using the common KJV construction "turneth away" (7x), and matches the sense of BBE's "he whose heart is turned away" for this verse.
- NEW: Him that turneth away in heart shall be filled with his own ways: and a good man shall be satisfied from himself.

## badness → foul — Genesis 41:19
*(from batch_0006_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "wickedness" is a serious content error — this verse describes the poor physical condition of cattle in a dream, not moral wickedness. Rephrasing with the attested KJV adjective "foul" (5x) matches Wycliffe's own rendering of this exact verse ("so foule and leene").
- NEW: And, behold, seven other kine came up after them, poor and very ill favoured and leanfleshed, so foul as I never saw in all the land of Egypt:

## bakemeats → meats — Genesis 40:17
*(from batch_0006_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "all manner of works" is semantically wrong (works does not mean baked goods). "Meats" is common in the KJV corpus (8x, used broadly for food) and gives clean, period-authentic phrasing.
- NEW: And in the uppermost basket there was of all manner of meats for Pharaoh; and the birds did eat them out of the basket upon my head.

## balancings → spreadings — Job 37:16
*(from batch_0006_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "balance" merely drops the "-ings" suffix from the flagged word (same lemma), which rule 3 forbids. "Spreadings" is a genuinely different word and matches the real KJV's own idiom for the same concept at Job 36:29, two verses earlier in the same book ("the spreadings of the clouds").
- NEW: Dost thou know the spreadings of the clouds, the wondrous works of him which is perfect in knowledge?

## banished → exiled — II Samuel 14:13
*(from batch_0007_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Owner Choice 7/17/26 - Match other exile changes to banishment - CUR's "or to cause" destroys the sense of a legal penalty. Wycliffe's "exilyng" attests the same concept in period English; "exile" is a genuinely different word from "banishment," attested elsewhere in the KJV corpus as a noun for a banished state (2 Samuel 15:19, "thou art a stranger, and also an exile"), and fits this list of punishments.
- NEW: And the woman said, Wherefore then hast thou thought such a thing against the people of God? for the king doth speak this thing as one which is faulty, in that the king doth not fetch home again his exiled.

## banished → exiled — II Samuel 14:14
*(from batch_0007_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Owner Choice 7/17/26 - Match other exile changes to banishment - CUR's "or to cause" destroys the sense of a legal penalty. Wycliffe's "exilyng" attests the same concept in period English; "exile" is a genuinely different word from "banishment," attested elsewhere in the KJV corpus as a noun for a banished state (2 Samuel 15:19, "thou art a stranger, and also an exile"), and fits this list of punishments.
- NEW: For we must needs die, and are as water spilt on the ground, which cannot be gathered up again; neither doth God respect any person: yet doth he devise means, that his exiled be not expelled from him.

## banishment → exile — Ezra 7:26
*(from batch_0007_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "or to cause" destroys the sense of a legal penalty. Wycliffe's "exilyng" attests the same concept in period English; "exile" is a genuinely different word from "banishment," attested elsewhere in the KJV corpus as a noun for a banished state (2 Samuel 15:19, "thou art a stranger, and also an exile"), and fits this list of punishments.
- NEW: And whosoever will not do the law of thy God, and the law of the king, let judgment be executed speedily upon him, whether it be unto death, or to exile, or to confiscation of goods, or to imprisonment.

## banishment → exile — Lamentations 2:14
*(from batch_0007_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "causes of cause" is a broken duplication. Consistent with the Ezra 7:26 ruling above, "exile" replaces "banishment" with a genuinely different, KJV-attested word; YLT and Darby's "causes of expulsion" confirm the sense of a cause leading to exile/removal.
- NEW: Thy prophets have seen vain and foolish things for thee: and they have not discovered thine iniquity, to turn away thy captivity; but have seen for thee false burdens and causes of exile.

## banquetings → feasts — I Peter 4:3
*(from batch_0007_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "feastings" does not occur in the KJV corpus and would itself become a new rare word. BBE's "feasting" attests the same underlying sense; "feasts," a distinct and common KJV word (Jude 1:12, "these are spots in your feasts"), fills the same noun-list slot without further rephrasing.
- NEW: For the time past of our life may suffice us to have wrought the will of the Gentiles, when we walked in lasciviousness, lusts, excess of wine, revellings, feasts, and abominable idolatries:

## barbarous → foreigners — Acts 28:2
*(from batch_0007_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "the barbarians people" is doubled and ungrammatical. Darby's "the barbarians shewed us" and YLT's "the foreigners were shewing us" both attest dropping the redundant noun "people"; "foreigners" is used for consistency with the two Barbarian entries above.
- NEW: And the foreigners shewed us no little kindness: for they kindled a fire, and received us every one, because of the present rain, and because of the cold.

## barbed → darts — Job 41:7
*(from batch_0007_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "hooked irons" does not occur in the KJV corpus. Darby's "wilt thou fill his skin with darts" attests both the word and the construction; "darts" is common in the KJV corpus and the phrase is reshaped to drop the awkward leftover "irons" per the witness.
- NEW: Canst thou fill his skin with darts? or his head with fish spears?

## barber's → razor — Ezekiel 5:1
*(from batch_0007_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Barber's" is a hapax modifier in the KJV corpus; CUR left it untouched. DRC's "a sharp knife that shaveth the hair" shows the sense can stand on the razor alone without the "barber's" attribution; "razor" (already the head noun) is common in the KJV corpus (Numbers 6:5, Judges 13:5) on its own.
- NEW: And thou, son of man, take thee a sharp knife, take thee a razor, and cause it to pass upon thine head and upon thy beard: then take thee balances to weigh, and divide the hair.

## bark → cry — Isaiah 56:10
*(from batch_0007_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Bark" is a hapax in the KJV corpus despite being an ordinary modern word — its rarity in this corpus is what flags it. BBE's "unable to make a sound" shows the sense is the watchdogs' failure to raise an alarm; "cry," extremely common in the KJV corpus, carries that same alarm-raising sense (compare Isaiah's own frequent "cry aloud" idiom) without inventing new content.
- NEW: His watchmen are blind: they are all ignorant, they are all dumb dogs, they cannot cry; sleeping, lying down, loving to slumber.

## barked → stripped — Joel 1:7
*(from batch_0007_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Stripped" is common in the KJV corpus and matches DRC's and RWebster's own rendering of this verse exactly, preserving both sense and grammar.
- NEW: He hath laid my vine waste, and stripped my fig tree: he hath made it clean bare, and cast it away; the branches thereof are made white.

## barnfloor → threshingfloor — II Kings 6:27
*(from batch_0007_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Threshingfloor" is common in the KJV corpus and matches YLT's, Darby's, and ASV's own rendering of this verse.
- NEW: And he said, If the Lord do not help thee, whence shall I help thee? out of the threshingfloor, or out of the winepress?

## baser → low degree — Acts 17:5
*(from batch_0007_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR doubled "sort" ("the sort sort"). Simply swapping "baser" for another form of "base" would be a forbidden re-inflection of the same lemma (per the owner ruling on "abhorrest → abhor"). "Of low degree" is a distinct, common KJV idiom for the same low social standing (Luke 1:52, "them of low degree"; James 1:9), matching the sense of Darby's "lowest rabble" and ASV's "vile fellows of the rabble."
- NEW: But the Jews which believed not, moved with envy, took unto them certain lewd fellows of low degree, and gathered a company, and set all the city on an uproar, and assaulted the house of Jason, and sought to bring them out to the people.

## battlement → railing — Deuteronomy 22:8
*(from batch_0007_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR left "battlement" untouched. Darby's and YLT's "parapet" names the same feature but is unattested in the KJV corpus (0 occurrences); BBE's and BSB's "railing" is a genuinely different, common KJV word (Exodus 27:4-5 and elsewhere) naming the identical roof-edge safety feature.
- NEW: When thou buildest a new house, then thou shalt make a railing for thy roof, that thou bring not blood upon thine house, if any man fall from thence.

## battlements → branches — Jeremiah 5:10
*(from batch_0007_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Contrary to first appearance, "branches" is not an error here: the underlying image is of Judah as a vineyard whose walls/hedges are being torn down, and Wycliffe ("siouns," i.e. shoots), DRC, YLT, ASV, ACV, and BSB all independently render this word "branches," confirming CUR's choice as the better-attested reading, not merely an alternate one.
- NEW: Go ye up upon her walls, and destroy; but make not a full end: take away her branches; for they are not the Lord's.

## bdellium → spice — Genesis 2:12
*(from batch_0007_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Bdellium" is a transliterated Hebrew word with no true English name; Wycliffe glosses it directly as "delium, that is, a tree of spicerie," attesting "spice" as the period gloss for the same substance. "Spice" is very common in the KJV corpus and preserves the sense of a precious aromatic good found in that land, alongside gold and onyx.
- NEW: And the gold of that land is good: there is spice and the onyx stone.

## bdellium → crystal — Numbers 11:7
*(from batch_0007_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: This occurrence is a color-simile ("the colour thereof as the colour of bdellium"), where "spice" (used for the Genesis 2:12 occurrence above) would not supply a color image. Wycliffe glosses bdellium's color directly, "whijt and briyt as cristal," attesting "crystal" as the period comparison; "crystal" is common in the KJV corpus (Job 28:17, Ezekiel 1:22, Revelation 4:6) and preserves the color-likeness the verse requires.
- NEW: And the manna was as coriander seed, and the colour thereof as the colour of crystal.

## bedstead → bed — Deuteronomy 3:11
*(from batch_0008_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Bed" is a genuinely different lexeme from "bedstead" (not a re-inflection), common throughout the KJV corpus, and matches Geneva1599's and DRC's own rendering ("his bed was a bed of yron" / "his bed of iron") of this verse.
- NEW: For only Og king of Bashan remained of the remnant of giants; behold, his bed was a bed of iron; is it not in Rabbath of the children of Ammon? nine cubits was the length thereof, and four cubits the breadth of it, after the cubit of a man.

## beetle → cricket — Leviticus 11:22
*(from batch_0008_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Beetle" names a specific insect in a list of edible locust-kin; ASV and ACV both render this "cricket," a genuinely different English word naming a kindred insect and independently attested in Middle English (Chaucer's "criket") as predating 1611.
- NEW: Even these of them ye may eat; the locust after his kind, and the bald locust after his kind, and the cricket after his kind, and the grasshopper after his kind.

## beggarly → poor — Galatians 4:9
*(from batch_0008_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Poor" matches YLT's own rendering of this verse exactly and is common throughout the KJV corpus.
- NEW: But now, after that ye have known God, or rather are known of God, how turn ye again to the weak and poor elements, whereunto ye desire again to be in bondage?

## beguile → deceive — Colossians 2:4
*(from batch_0008_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Deceive" matches Webster's and RWebster's own rendering exactly and is common in the KJV corpus.
- NEW: And this I say, lest any man should deceive you with enticing words.

## beguile → rob — Colossians 2:18
*(from batch_0008_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "reward you of your reward" is a broken duplication. ASV's "rob you of your prize" attests a genuinely different, common KJV verb for the same wrongful deprivation, matched to the noun "reward" already standing in the OLD text.
- NEW: Let no man rob you of your reward in a voluntary humility and worshipping of angels, intruding into those things which he hath not seen, vainly puffed up by his fleshly mind,

## beguiling → deceiving — II Peter 2:14
*(from batch_0008_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Deceiving" is common in the KJV corpus and matches the sense every witness gives (Wycliffe's "disseyuynge," DRC's "alluring").
- NEW: Having eyes full of adultery, and that cannot cease from sin; deceiving unstable souls: an heart they have exercised with covetous practices; cursed children:

## behoved → was needful — Luke 24:46
*(from batch_0008_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "thus it owed Christ to suffer" is not idiomatic English. "Needful" is common in the KJV corpus (Philippians 1:24, Titus 3:14) and several witnesses (RWebster, UKJV, ACV) independently render this "was necessary" / "was essential," confirming the underlying sense of a fitting necessity.
- NEW: And said unto them, Thus it is written, and thus it was needful for Christ to suffer, and to rise from the dead the third day:

## behoved → was needful — Hebrews 2:17
*(from batch_0008_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Consistent with the Luke 24:46 ruling above; RWebster, UKJV, ACV, and BBE all render this "was necessary" / "was essential," and "needful" is the KJV-corpus-attested word for the same idea.
- NEW: Wherefore in all things it was needful for him to be made like unto his brethren, that he might be a merciful and faithful high priest in things pertaining to God, to make reconciliation for the sins of the people.

## bekah → portion — Exodus 38:26
*(from batch_0008_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "A half for every man" is confusing, immediately followed by "that is, half a shekel." Geneva1599 attests "A portion for a man, that is, halfe a shekel," supplying a genuinely different, common KJV word (Deuteronomy 18:8 and elsewhere) for the fixed share owed by each man, and resolving the awkward doubled "half."
- NEW: A portion for every man, that is, half a shekel, after the shekel of the sanctuary, for every one that went to be numbered, from twenty years old and upward, for six hundred thousand and three thousand and five hundred and fifty men.

## belch → utter — Psalms 59:7
*(from batch_0008_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Utter" is a genuinely different, common KJV word that preserves both the sense of a hostile outpouring of speech and the grammar of the surrounding clause.
- NEW: Behold, they utter out with their mouth: swords are in their lips: for who, say they, doth hear?

## belied → denied — Jeremiah 5:12
*(from batch_0008_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Denied" matches Wycliffe, Geneva1599, DRC, and ASV all exactly, and is extremely common in the KJV corpus.
- NEW: They have denied the Lord, and said, It is not he; neither shall evil come upon us; neither shall we see sword nor famine:

## belief → faith — II Thessalonians 2:13
*(from batch_0008_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Faith" is common throughout the KJV corpus and preserves the sense confirmed by BBE's "faith in what is true."
- NEW: But we are bound to give thanks alway to God for you, brethren beloved of the Lord, because God hath from the beginning chosen you to salvation through sanctification of the Spirit and faith of the truth:

## believers → those with faith — Acts 5:14
*(from batch_0008_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Owners choice - 7/17/26 - those with faith
- NEW: And those with faith were the more added to the Lord, multitudes both of men and women.)

## believers → those with faith — I Timothy 4:12
*(from batch_0008_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Owners choice - 7/17/26 - those with faith
- NEW: Let no man despise thy youth; but be thou an example of those with faith, in word, in conversation, in charity, in spirit, in faith, in purity.

## bellow → cry — Jeremiah 50:11
*(from batch_0008_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Cry" is common in the KJV corpus and matches YLT's own rendering ("cry aloud as bulls") closely enough to preserve the sense of a loud triumphant noise.
- NEW: Because ye were glad, because ye rejoiced, O ye destroyers of mine heritage, because ye are grown fat as the heifer at grass, and cry as bulls;

## bellows → blower — Jeremiah 6:29
*(from batch_0008_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Bellows" names a specific smithing tool, and every witness including pre-1611 Wycliffe retains it — but BBE independently renders this "the blower," a genuinely different English word for the same fire-tending device (compare the KJV's own "the smith that bloweth the coals," Isaiah 54:16), offering a workable substitute where the creature-name entries in this batch had none.
- NEW: The blower is burned, the lead is consumed of the fire; the founder melteth in vain: for the wicked are not plucked away.

## benches → seats — Ezekiel 27:6
*(from batch_0009_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Seats" is a genuinely different, common word that matches Wycliffe's own rendering ("seetis") of this verse directly.
- NEW: Of the oaks of Bashan have they made thine oars; the company of the Ashurites have made thy seats of ivory, brought out of the isles of Chittim.

## benefactors → bountiful — Luke 22:25
*(from batch_0009_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Geneva1599 attests "are called bountifull" directly; "bountiful" is a genuinely different word from "benefactors," and the same root stands elsewhere in the KJV corpus (Isaiah 32:5, "the churl said to be bountiful"), confirming it as a common, period-authentic term for one styled generous.
- NEW: And he said unto them, The kings of the Gentiles exercise lordship over them; and they that exercise authority upon them are called bountiful.

## benevolence → her due — I Corinthians 7:3
*(from batch_0009_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "due good" is vague and awkward. ASV's and Darby's "render unto the wife her due" attest a genuinely different, common construction, preserved with the parallel clause about the husband unchanged.
- NEW: Let the husband render unto the wife her due: and likewise also the wife unto the husband.

## berries → olives — Isaiah 17:6
*(from batch_0009_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Olives" is a genuinely different, extremely common word that preserves the sense of the small fruit remaining after an olive tree is shaken, consistent with the surrounding imagery.
- NEW: Yet gleaning grapes shall be left in it, as the shaking of an olive tree, two or three olives in the top of the uppermost bough, four or five in the outmost fruitful branches thereof, saith the Lord God of Israel.

## berries → olives — James 3:12
*(from batch_0009_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "olive olives" is a broken duplication. ASV's "yield olives" and Geneva1599's "bring forth oliues" attest dropping the redundant adjective "olive" entirely, leaving the common noun alone.
- NEW: Can the fig tree, my brethren, bear olives? either a vine, figs? so can no fountain both yield salt water and fresh.

## besom → broom — Isaiah 14:23
*(from batch_0009_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "mire of destruction" wrongly substitutes a swampy image for the sweeping image the verse requires. ACV's and BSB's "broom of destruction" attest a genuinely different, common word naming the same sweeping implement.
- NEW: I will also make it a possession for the bittern, and pools of water: and I will sweep it with the broom of destruction, saith the Lord of hosts.

## bestead → distressed — Isaiah 8:21
*(from batch_0009_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "hardly hard" is a broken duplication. Webster's, RWebster's, and ASV's "distressed" all attest a genuinely different, common word for the same affliction, and fits the "hardly ___ and hungry" frame directly.
- NEW: And they shall pass through it, hardly distressed and hungry: and it shall come to pass, that when they shall be hungry, they shall fret themselves, and curse their king and their God, and look upward.

## bestir → move — II Samuel 5:24
*(from batch_0009_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Move" is a genuinely different, common word attested independently by Geneva1599 ("remoue") and YLT ("move sharply") for the same call to action.
- NEW: And let it be, when thou hearest the sound of a going in the tops of the mulberry trees, that then thou shalt move thyself: for then shall the Lord go out before thee, to smite the host of the Philistines.

## bethink → take it to their hearts — I Kings 8:47
*(from batch_0009_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "return themselves in the land" is ambiguous and does not convey inward reflection. RWebster's and Darby's "take it to their hearts" attest a genuinely different, clearer construction for the same act of inward repentance.
- NEW: Yet if they shall take it to their hearts in the land whither they were carried captives, and repent, and make supplication unto thee in the land of them that carried them captives, saying, We have sinned, and have done perversely, we have committed wickedness;

## bethink → take it to their hearts — II Chronicles 6:37
*(from batch_0009_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Consistent with I Kings 8:47 above; RWebster and Darby both attest the same construction here.
- NEW: Yet if they take it to their hearts in the land whither they are carried captive, and turn and pray unto thee in the land of their captivity, saying, We have sinned, we have done amiss, and have dealt wickedly;

## bewray → discover — Isaiah 16:3
*(from batch_0009_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Discover" carries its period sense of "reveal, betray" (not the modern "find"), matches RWebster's, Webster's, and Darby's own rendering, and is common in the KJV corpus.
- NEW: Take counsel, execute judgment; make thy shadow as the night in the midst of the noonday; hide the outcasts; discover not him that wandereth.

## bier → coffin — II Samuel 3:31
*(from batch_0009_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "bed" wrongly substitutes ordinary sleeping furniture for a funeral litter, which is confusing in a mourning context. Tyndale's and Geneva1599's "coffin" (used at Luke 7:14 for the identical funeral-bier structure) is a genuinely different, period-authentic word for the object carried in a funeral procession.
- NEW: And David said to Joab, and to all the people that were with him, Rend your clothes, and gird you with sackcloth, and mourn before Abner. And king David himself followed the coffin.

## bier → coffin — Luke 7:14
*(from batch_0009_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Consistent with II Samuel 3:31 above; Tyndale, Geneva1599, and ACV all render this "coffin" directly.
- NEW: And he came and touched the coffin: and they that bare him stood still. And he said, Young man, I say unto thee, Arise.

## billows → floods — Psalms 42:7
*(from batch_0009_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "heaps" does not fit the imagery of waves passing over the psalmist. Geneva1599's "all thy waues and thy floods are gone ouer me" attests a genuinely different, very common KJV word (Exodus 15:8's own "floods stood upright as an heap" shows the same watery sense) for the same overwhelming imagery.
- NEW: Deep calleth unto deep at the noise of thy waterspouts: all thy waves and thy floods are gone over me.

## billows → floods — Jonah 2:3
*(from batch_0009_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Consistent with Psalms 42:7 above; "floods" is used here for the same reason, preserving the parallel with "waves."
- NEW: For thou hadst cast me into the deep, in the midst of the seas; and the floods compassed me about: all thy floods and thy waves passed over me.

## bishoprick → oversight — Acts 1:20
*(from batch_0009_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Oversight" is a genuinely different, common word that matches YLT's own rendering of this verse exactly.
- NEW: For it is written in the book of Psalms, Let his habitation be desolate, and let no man dwell therein: and his oversight let another take.

## bitten → stung — Numbers 21:8
*(from batch_0009_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "every one that is bite" is ungrammatical. "Stung" is a genuinely different, common word for the same venomous serpent-attack described throughout this passage, and preserves correct past-participle grammar.
- NEW: And the Lord said unto Moses, Make thee a fiery serpent, and set it upon a pole: and it shall come to pass, that every one that is stung, when he looketh upon it, shall live.

## bitten → stung — Numbers 21:9
*(from batch_0009_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Consistent with Numbers 21:8 above; CUR's "had bite any man" is likewise ungrammatical, and "stung" resolves it correctly.
- NEW: And Moses made a serpent of brass, and put it upon a pole, and it came to pass, that if a serpent had stung any man, when he beheld the serpent of brass, he lived.

## blacker → darker — Lamentations 4:8
*(from batch_0009_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "dark than a coal" is ungrammatical, dropping the required comparative. YLT's and Darby's "darker than blackness"/"darker than a coal" attest "darker," a genuinely different lemma (dark, not black) that correctly carries the comparative form.
- NEW: Their visage is darker than a coal; they are not known in the streets: their skin cleaveth to their bones; it is withered, it is become like a stick.

## blackish → turbid — Job 6:16
*(from batch_0009_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "black" is a forbidden mere re-inflection of the same lemma (dropping the "-ish" qualifier). Darby attests "turbid," a genuinely different word describing water clouded and murky with melting ice, fitting the sense of streams darkened by ice-melt.
- NEW: Which are turbid by reason of the ice, and wherein the snow is hid:

## blains → sores — Exodus 9:9
*(from batch_0009_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: RWebster and ACV both attest "sores," a genuinely different, extremely common KJV word (Job 2:7, Luke 16:20-21) for the same skin affliction named by the specialized medical term "blains."
- NEW: And it shall become small dust in all the land of Egypt, and shall be a boil breaking forth with sores upon man, and upon beast, throughout all the land of Egypt.

## blains → sores — Exodus 9:10
*(from batch_0009_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Consistent with Exodus 9:9 above; RWebster and ACV both attest "sores" here as well.
- NEW: And they took ashes of the furnace, and stood before Pharaoh; and Moses sprinkled it up toward heaven; and it became a boil breaking forth with sores upon man, and upon beast.

## blasphemer → railer — I Timothy 1:13
*(from batch_0009_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's bare "a railing" is not a valid noun for a person and breaks the grammar of "was before a railing." "Railer" is the correctly formed noun for the same root CUR reached for, is a genuinely different word from "blasphemer," and is independently attested as a KJV-family term for one who reviles (compare "or a railer, or a drunkard," 1 Corinthians 5:11).
- NEW: Who was before a railer, and a persecutor, and injurious: but I obtained mercy, because I did it ignorantly in unbelief.

## blaze → spread — Mark 1:45
*(from batch_0010_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Spread" is a genuinely different, common word that matches YLT's, Darby's, and ASV's own rendering of this verse.
- NEW: But he went out, and began to publish it much, and to spread abroad the matter, insomuch that Jesus could no more openly enter into the city, but was without in desert places: and they came to him from every quarter.

## bleating → crying — I Samuel 15:14
*(from batch_0010_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "speaking of the sheep" wrongly implies sheep speak. "Crying" is a genuinely different, common KJV word for an animal's distress-noise, and parallels "the lowing of the oxen" that follows in the same clause.
- NEW: And Samuel said, What meaneth then this crying of the sheep in mine ears, and the lowing of the oxen which I hear?

## bleatings → pipings — Judges 5:16
*(from batch_0010_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "hiss of the flocks" is wrong (a hiss is not a sheep's sound). ASV and ACV both attest "pipings for the flocks" — a different but genuine translation tradition referring to shepherds' pipe-music rather than the animals' own noise — supplying a genuinely different, coherent word.
- NEW: Why abodest thou among the sheepfolds, to hear the pipings of the flocks? For the divisions of Reuben there were great searchings of heart.

## blindfolded → covered — Luke 22:64
*(from batch_0010_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Covered" is a genuinely different, common word that matches Darby's and ACV's own rendering ("covering him up" / "having covered him") of this verse.
- NEW: And when they had covered him, they struck him on the face, and asked him, saying, Prophesy, who is it that smote thee?

## bloodguiltiness → blood — Psalms 51:14
*(from batch_0010_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Blood" is a genuinely different, common word that matches Geneva1599's and YLT's own rendering of this verse exactly.
- NEW: Deliver me from blood, O God, thou God of my salvation: and my tongue shall sing aloud of thy righteousness.

## bloodthirsty → men of blood — Proverbs 29:10
*(from batch_0010_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "The man hate the upright" has a broken subject-verb agreement and drops the essential descriptor. YLT's "Men of blood hate the perfect" attests a genuinely different, grammatical construction naming the same violent character.
- NEW: Men of blood hate the upright: but the just seek his soul.

## bloomed → produced — Numbers 17:8
*(from batch_0010_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "shewed blossoms" is an unusual and unclear phrasing. ASV's and ACV's "produced blossoms" attest a genuinely different, common word that reads naturally alongside "brought forth buds."
- NEW: And it came to pass, that on the morrow Moses went into the tabernacle of witness; and, behold, the rod of Aaron for the house of Levi was budded, and brought forth buds, and produced blossoms, and yielded almonds.

## blueness → stripes that wound — Proverbs 20:30
*(from batch_0010_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "The wounds of a wound" is a broken duplication. ASV's "Stripes that wound cleanse away evil" attests a genuinely different construction that resolves the nonsense while preserving the sense of corrective punishment.
- NEW: Stripes that wound cleanse away evil: so do stripes the inward parts of the belly.

## blunt → dull — Ecclesiastes 10:10
*(from batch_0010_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "the iron be set" loses the sense entirely (a dull edge requiring more strength). BSB independently attests "dull," a genuinely different, common English word for the same lack of a sharp edge.
- NEW: If the iron be dull, and he do not whet the edge, then must he put to more strength: but wisdom is profitable to direct.

## bolled → in bloom — Exodus 9:31
*(from batch_0010_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Bolled" is a specialized botanical term for flax in seed-pod stage; ASV and ACV both attest "in bloom," a genuinely different, common phrase for the same growth stage.
- NEW: And the flax and the barley was smitten: for the barley was in the ear, and the flax was in bloom.

## bolt → lock — II Samuel 13:17
*(from batch_0010_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "shod the door" is nonsensical (to shoe, not to fasten). Geneva1599 attests "locke the doore," a genuinely different, common word for securing a door.
- NEW: Then he called his servant that ministered unto him, and said, Put now this woman out from me, and lock the door after her.

## bolted → locked — II Samuel 13:18
*(from batch_0010_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "inclosed the door" is an unusual, unclear use of "inclosed" for shutting a door. Consistent with the II Samuel 13:17 ruling above, Geneva1599's "locked the doore" supplies the same genuinely different word.
- NEW: And she had a garment of divers colours upon her: for with such robes were the king's daughters that were virgins apparelled. Then his servant brought her out, and locked the door after her.

## bondmaid → bondwoman — Leviticus 19:20
*(from batch_0010_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "handmaid" weakens the legal point of the verse, which turns on the woman's unfree status ("because she was not free") — a handmaid in KJV usage need not be unfree. Darby attests "bondwoman," a genuinely different word that keeps the essential bond/unfree sense the verse's ruling depends on.
- NEW: And whosoever lieth carnally with a woman, that is a bondwoman, betrothed to an husband, and not at all redeemed, nor freedom given her; she shall be scourged; they shall not be put to death, because she was not free.

## bondmaid → bondwoman — Galatians 4:22
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "handmaid" weakens the free/bond contrast the verse itself makes explicit ("the other by a freewoman") — a handmaid need not be unfree. Darby, RWebster, and DRC all attest "bondwoman," a genuinely different word that preserves the bond/free legal distinction the passage's argument depends on, consistent with the Leviticus 19:20 ruling in the previous batch.
- NEW: For it is written, that Abraham had two sons, the one by a bondwoman, the other by a freewoman.

## bondmaids → bondwomen — Leviticus 25:44
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR wrongly reduced the plural "bondmaids" to singular "handmaid" in both places, breaking both number and sense. DRC attests "bondwomen," a genuinely different plural word that keeps the parallel with "bondmen" and is consistent with the Galatians 4:22 ruling above.
- NEW: Both thy bondmen, and thy bondwomen, which thou shalt have, shall be of the heathen that are round about you; of them shall ye buy bondmen and bondwomen.

## bondservant → slave — Leviticus 25:39
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's plain "servant" loses the specific point of the verse, which is to forbid reducing this brother to slave status specifically (an ordinary servant relationship would otherwise be unremarkable). RWebster attests "slave," a genuinely different word that preserves the legal force of the prohibition.
- NEW: And if thy brother that dwelleth by thee be waxen poor, and be sold unto thee; thou shalt not compel him to serve as a slave:

## bondservice → forced service — I Kings 9:21
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's bare "service" loses the compelled, unfree character of the labor. RWebster's "forced labour" and BBE's "forced work" attest the same sense; "forced service" is a genuinely different phrase preserving that sense while fitting "a tribute of ___" from the OLD text.
- NEW: Their children that were left after them in the land, whom the children of Israel also were not able utterly to destroy, upon those did Solomon levy a tribute of forced service unto this day.

## booth → lodge — Job 27:18
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "tabernacle" carries the wrong connotation — a sacred dwelling rather than the flimsy, temporary hut central to this simile about an unstable house. Geneva1599 attests "a lodge that the watchman maketh," a genuinely different word preserving the sense of a makeshift shelter.
- NEW: He buildeth his house as a moth, and as a lodge that the keeper maketh.

## booth → shelter — Jonah 4:5
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "tabernacle" is likewise too grand a word for Jonah's makeshift sunshade. BSB attests "shelter," a genuinely different, common word for the same casual structure.
- NEW: So Jonah went out of the city, and sat on the east side of the city, and there made him a shelter, and sat under it in the shadow, till he might see what would become of the city.

## bore → pierce — Exodus 21:6
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "curse his ear through with an aul" is nonsensical. BSB attests "pierce his ear with an awl," a genuinely different, common word for the same act.
- NEW: Then his master shall bring him unto the judges; he shall also bring him to the door, or unto the door post; and his master shall pierce his ear through with an aul; and he shall serve him for ever.

## bore → pierce — Job 41:2
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "curse his jaw through with a thorn" is likewise nonsensical. YLT, ASV, ACV, and BSB all attest "pierce," consistent with the Exodus 21:6 ruling above.
- NEW: Canst thou put an hook into his nose? or pierce his jaw through with a thorn?

## bored → pierced — II Kings 12:9
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "expressed a hole" is nonsensical. YLT attests "pierceth a hole," consistent with the "bore → pierce" rulings above and fitting the same past-tense grammar.
- NEW: But Jehoiada the priest took a chest, and pierced a hole in the lid of it, and set it beside the altar, on the right side as one cometh into the house of the Lord: and the priests that kept the door put therein all the money that was brought into the house of the Lord.

## borrower → debtor — Proverbs 22:7
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's bare "lend" is a verb, not a valid subject noun, breaking the grammar of "the lend is servant." "Debtor" is a genuinely different, common word for the same party, and is independently attested as the alternate rendering for this identical Hebrew concept in the parallel Isaiah 24:2 entry below (ASV, ACV).
- NEW: The rich ruleth over the poor, and the debtor is servant to the lender.

## borrower → debtor — Isaiah 24:2
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "as with the lender, so with the lend" is ungrammatical. ASV and ACV both attest "as with the creditor, so with the debtor," confirming "debtor" as a genuinely different, common word, consistent with Proverbs 22:7 above.
- NEW: And it shall be, as with the people, so with the priest; as with the servant, so with his master; as with the maid, so with her mistress; as with the buyer, so with the seller; as with the lender, so with the debtor; as with the taker of usury, so with the giver of usury to him.

## bosses → studs — Job 15:26
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "the thick bodies of his bucklers" is unclear. ACV attests "the thick studs of his bucklers," a genuinely different word naming the same raised metal knobs on a shield.
- NEW: He runneth upon him, even on his neck, upon the thick studs of his bucklers:

## botch → boil — Deuteronomy 28:27
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Boil" is a genuinely different word from "botch" (not a re-inflection), independently attested by ASV, ACV, and RWebster for this verse.
- NEW: The Lord will smite thee with the boil of Egypt, and with the emerods, and with the scab, and with the itch, whereof thou canst not be healed.

## botch → boil — Deuteronomy 28:35
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Consistent with Deuteronomy 28:27 above; ASV and ACV both attest "boil" here as well.
- NEW: The Lord shall smite thee in the knees, and in the legs, with a sore boil that cannot be healed, from the sole of thy foot unto the top of thy head.

## bowmen → archers — Jeremiah 4:29
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's bare "bow" cannot serve as a plural agent-noun alongside "horsemen," breaking the grammar. DRC attests "the archers," a genuinely different, common word for the same class of soldier.
- NEW: The whole city shall flee for the noise of the horsemen and archers; they shall go into thickets, and climb up upon the rocks: every city shall be forsaken, and not a man dwell therein.

## bowshot → an arrow's flight — Genesis 21:16
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Bowshot" is a compound period distance-measure retained by every witness including pre-1611 Wycliffe's paraphrase. BBE attests "about an arrow flight," a genuinely different construction naming the same distance.
- NEW: And she went, and sat her down over against him a good way off, as it were an arrow's flight: for she said, Let me not see the death of the child. And she sat over against him, and lift up her voice, and wept.

## boy → lad — Joel 3:3
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "a child" loses the male/female parallel with "sold a girl for wine" that the verse's structure depends on. "Lad," a genuinely different, common KJV word for a young male (Genesis 44:22, "the lad cannot leave his father"), restores the parallel.
- NEW: And they have cast lots for my people; and have given a lad for an harlot, and sold a girl for wine, that they might drink.

## boys → youths — Genesis 25:27
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "the servants grew" wrongly changes the subject from Esau and Jacob themselves to household servants, a serious meaning break. YLT attests "the youths grew," a genuinely different, common word correctly naming the twin sons.
- NEW: And the youths grew: and Esau was a cunning hunter, a man of the field; and Jacob was a plain man, dwelling in tents.

## boys → lads — Zechariah 8:5
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "servants" wrongly turns playing children into household servants. "Lads," a genuinely different, common KJV word (consistent with the Joel 3:3 ruling above), restores the sense of children at play alongside "girls."
- NEW: And the streets of the city shall be full of lads and girls playing in the streets thereof.

## bracelet → band — II Samuel 1:10
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "the chains that was on his arm" has a number mismatch (plural "chains" with singular "was") and loses the precision of a single armband. BSB attests "the band that was on his arm," a genuinely different, singular word that also resolves the grammar.
- NEW: So I stood upon him, and slew him, because I was sure that he could not live after that he was fallen: and I took the crown that was upon his head, and the band that was on his arm, and have brought them hither unto my lord.

## brand → firebrand — Zechariah 3:2
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "a firebrands" has an article/number mismatch (indefinite "a" with plural "firebrands"). BSB attests singular "firebrand," a genuinely different compound word that both fixes the grammar and matches the sense.
- NEW: And the Lord said unto Satan, The Lord rebuke thee, O Satan; even the Lord that hath chosen Jerusalem rebuke thee: is not this a firebrand plucked out of the fire?

## brandish → bare — Ezekiel 32:10
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Owner's choice 7/17/26 - bare appears hundreds of time
- NEW: Yea, I will make many people amazed at thee, and their kings shall be horribly afraid for thee, when I shall bare my sword before them; and they shall tremble at every moment, every man for his own life, in the day of thy fall.

## brands → torches — Judges 15:5
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "the lamps on fire" does not fit the context of firebrands tied to foxes' tails. RWebster, Darby, and YLT all attest "torches," a genuinely different, common word for the same burning implement.
- NEW: And when he had set the torches on fire, he let them go into the standing corn of the Philistines, and burnt up both the shocks, and also the standing corn, with the vineyards and olives.

## bravery → beauty — Isaiah 3:18
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Beauty" is a genuinely different, common word that matches YLT's and ASV's own rendering of this verse.
- NEW: In that day the Lord will take away the beauty of their tinkling ornaments about their feet, and their cauls, and their round tires like the moon,

## brawler → railer — I Timothy 3:3
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Railer" is a genuinely different, common KJV-family word for a quarrelsome person (compare "a railer," 1 Corinthians 5:11) and fits the grammar without further change.
- NEW: Not given to wine, no striker, not greedy of filthy lucre; but patient, not a railer, not covetous;

## brawlers → railers — Titus 3:2
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Consistent with I Timothy 3:3 above; "railers" is a genuinely different, common word for the same fault.
- NEW: To speak evil of no man, to be no railers, but gentle, shewing all meekness unto all men.

## brawling → contentious — Proverbs 21:9
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "a causing woman" is unclear (causing what?). Geneva1599, Darby, ASV, and ACV all attest "a contentious woman," a genuinely different, common word for the same quarrelsome character.
- NEW: It is better to dwell in a corner of the housetop, than with a contentious woman in a wide house.

## brawling → contentious — Proverbs 25:24
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Consistent with Proverbs 21:9 above; the same witnesses attest "contentious" here.
- NEW: It is better to dwell in the corner of the housetop, than with a contentious woman and in a wide house.

## bray → cry — Job 6:5
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "should" is a bare modal verb with no following infinitive, meaningless. "Bray" is the specific donkey-sound verb, flagged as a hapax despite being ordinary; "cry," used consistently in this project for animal-sound words that must be replaced (compare "bark → cry," Isaiah 56:10, and "bleating → crying," I Samuel 15:14), preserves the sense.
- NEW: Doth the wild ass cry when he hath grass? or loweth the ox over his fodder?

## bray → pound — Proverbs 27:22
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "shouldest should a fool" is a broken duplication. This occurrence of "bray" means to pound or crush in a mortar (a different sense from the donkey-sound "bray" in Job 6:5); ACV attests "pound," a genuinely different, common word for the same mortar-and-pestle action, and RWebster and BSB confirm the sense with "crush"/"grind."
- NEW: Though thou shouldest pound a fool in a mortar among wheat with a pestle, yet will not his foolishness depart from him.

## brayed → cried — Job 30:7
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: This is the donkey-sound sense of "bray," consistent with the Job 6:5 ruling above; Geneva1599's "roared" and BSB's "cried out" both attest a genuinely different word for the same animal-like wailing of outcasts.
- NEW: Among the bushes they cried; under the nettles they were gathered together.

## breaker → transgressor — Micah 2:13
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Owner's Choiuce 7/17/26 - CUR's "a broken of the law" is not a valid noun phrase. Geneva1599, DRC, Darby, and ASV all attest "transgressor," a genuinely different, very common KJV word for the same violation.
- NEW: The transgressor is come up before them: they have broken up, and have passed through the gate, and are gone out by it: and their king shall pass before them, and the Lord on the head of them.

## breaker → transgressor — Romans 2:25
*(from batch_0011_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "a broken of the law" is not a valid noun phrase. Geneva1599, DRC, Darby, and ASV all attest "transgressor," a genuinely different, very common KJV word for the same violation.
- NEW: For circumcision verily profiteth, if thou keep the law: but if thou be a transgressor of the law, thy circumcision is made uncircumcision.

## bred → brought forth — Exodus 16:20
*(from batch_0012_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "it high worms" is ungrammatical nonsense. "brought forth" (511 occurrences) is extremely well attested and matches the sense of YLT "bringeth up worms".
- NEW: Notwithstanding they hearkened not unto Moses; but some of them left of it until the morning, and it brought forth worms, and stank: and Moses was wroth with them.

## breed → swarm — Genesis 8:17
*(from batch_0012_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "may appointed abundantly" is ungrammatical. "swarm" (42 occurrences) is well attested and matches Darby "swarm on the earth".
- NEW: Bring forth with thee every living thing that is with thee, of all flesh, both of fowl, and of cattle, and of every creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth; that they may swarm abundantly in the earth, and be fruitful, and multiply upon the earth.

## breed → fed in — Deuteronomy 32:14
*(from batch_0012_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "rams of the appointed of Bashan" is nonsense. Copying Geneva1599's construction "rammes fed in Bashan" ("fed in" attested 56 times) gives a natural, well-attested reading.
- NEW: Butter of kine, and milk of sheep, with fat of lambs, and rams fed in Bashan, and goats, with the fat of kidneys of wheat; and thou didst drink the pure blood of the grape.

## breeding → possession — Zephaniah 2:9
*(from batch_0012_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "growth" is a genuinely different word but rare in the KJV corpus (7 occurrences). "possession" (161 occurrences) is far better attested and matches Darby/ASV/ACV "possession of nettles" exactly.
- NEW: Therefore as I live, saith the Lord of hosts, the God of Israel, Surely Moab shall be as Sodom, and the children of Ammon as Gomorrah, even the possession of nettles, and saltpits, and a perpetual desolation: the residue of my people shall spoil them, and the remnant of my people shall possess them.

## bribe → ransom — I Samuel 12:3
*(from batch_0012_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "ransom" is a genuinely different word, well attested in KJV (112 occurrences), and matches YLT "ransom".
- NEW: Behold, here I am: witness against me before the Lord, and before his anointed: whose ox have I taken? or whose ass have I taken? or whom have I defrauded? whom have I oppressed? or of whose hand have I received any ransom to blind mine eyes therewith? and I will restore it you.

## bribe → ransom — Amos 5:12
*(from batch_0012_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same as I Samuel 12:3 — "ransom" is well attested (112) and matches YLT "ransoms".

## briefly → wholly — Romans 13:9
*(from batch_0012_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "throughly comprehended" reads as "thoroughly" — a poor sense-fit for "briefly." "wholly" (203 occurrences) is well attested and fits "wholly comprehended in this saying" (all commandments contained entirely in one), matching the sense behind Darby/ASV/YLT/BSB's "summed up."
- NEW: For this, Thou shalt not commit adultery, Thou shalt not kill, Thou shalt not steal, Thou shalt not bear false witness, Thou shalt not covet; and if there be any other commandment, it is wholly comprehended in this saying, namely, Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself.

## briefly → shortly — I Peter 5:12
*(from batch_0012_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "written throughly" contradicts the verse's sense of a short letter. "shortly" (105 occurrences) is well attested and matches Wycliffe's "wroot schortli" for this very verse.
- NEW: By Silvanus, a faithful brother unto you, as I suppose, I have written shortly, exhorting, and testifying that this is the true grace of God wherein ye stand.

## brigandine → harness — Jeremiah 51:3
*(from batch_0012_proposals.md)*

*(from batch_0012_proposals.md)*

*(from batch_0012_proposals.md)*

- NEW: The measure thereof is longer than the earth, and greater than the sea.

"lame" (1039 occurrences) is well attested for the foot, and "maimed" (49 occurrences) is used for the companion word to keep both entries consistent and non-duplicative.

## carriest → destroyest — Psalms 90:5
