## circumspectly → carefully — Ephesians 5:15
*(from batch_0016_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "walk perfectly" significantly changes the sense (perfection versus watchfulness). "carefully" (28 occurrences) is well attested and matches Darby/ASV/ACV/BSB's rendering of this exact verse.  Owner 7/17/26.  Wise should also be wise men to clarify the subject.
- NEW: See then that ye walk carefully, not as fools, but as wise men,

## citizen → inhabitant — Luke 15:15
*(from batch_0017_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: All period witnesses (Wycliffe "citeseyns", Tyndale "citesyn", Geneva "citizen", DRC "citizens") offer no alternate word for this sense, so a genuine replacement outside that family is needed; "inhabitant" (1512 occurrences in the KJV corpus, common inflected form) preserves the sense of one dwelling in that country.
- NEW: And he went and joined himself to an inhabitant of that country; and he sent him into his fields to feed swine.

## citizen → inhabitant — Acts 21:39
*(from batch_0017_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same difficulty as above — Wycliffe, Tyndale, Geneva, and DRC all retain "citizen/citeseyn" with no substitute. "Inhabitant" is well attested in the corpus and keeps Paul's point ("of no mean city") intact; the article shifts from "a" to "an" for the vowel-initial word.
- NEW: But Paul said, I am a man which am a Jew of Tarsus, a city in Cilicia, an inhabitant of no mean city: and, I beseech thee, suffer me to speak unto the people.

## citizens → inhabitants — Luke 19:14
*(from batch_0017_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "subjects" has zero occurrences anywhere in the KJV corpus (a brand-new rare word, violating the frequency rule) and no period witness supports it. "Inhabitants" (1302 occurrences) is amply common and fits the parable's sense of the people of the nobleman's own country who reject his rule.
- NEW: But his inhabitants hated him, and sent a message after him, saying, We will not have this man to reign over us.

## clamorous → tumultuous — Proverbs 9:13
*(from batch_0017_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Geneva's "troublesome" would be the closest witness match, but "troublesome" has zero occurrences elsewhere in the KJV corpus (a new rare word). "Tumultuous" is genuinely different from "clamorous," reasonably attested (21 occurrences), and fits the sense of the loud, unruly woman that Darby, ASV, and ACV also preserve as "clamorous."
- NEW: A foolish woman is tumultuous: she is simple, and knoweth nothing.

## clamour → cry — Ephesians 4:31
*(from batch_0017_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Wycliffe's period witness reads "cry" directly at this verse ("wraththe, and indignacioun, and cry"), and "cry" is extremely common in the corpus (1435 occurrences) and fits the list of nouns to be put away.
- NEW: Let all bitterness, and wrath, and anger, and cry, and evil speaking, be put away from you, with all malice:

## clearer → fairer — Job 11:17
*(from batch_0017_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "shall be rose than the noonday" is ungrammatical, and the seemingly obvious fix "brighter" has zero occurrences anywhere in the corpus (a new rare word). "Fairer" (21 occurrences) is a genuinely different comparative that preserves the sense (per DRC's "brightness... shall arise") that this renewed age will outshine noonday.
- NEW: And thine age shall be fairer than the noonday; thou shalt shine forth, thou shalt be as the morning.

## clearness → brightness — Exodus 24:10
*(from batch_0017_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "brightness" is a genuinely different, common word (154 occurrences) that captures the same sense as Wycliffe/Geneva's "cleer" and DRC's "clear," describing the sapphire pavement's radiance.
- NEW: And they saw the God of Israel: and there was under his feet as it were a paved work of a sapphire stone, and as it were the body of heaven in his brightness.

## cleft → (clause rewrite) — Deuteronomy 14:6
*(from batch_0017_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "cleaveth the rent into two claws" is ungrammatical, since "rent" cannot stand as the object noun there. DRC's construction ("divideth the hoof in two parts") models a clean rewrite; "parteth" reuses the verse's own vocabulary (v.6 already has "parteth the hoof") and is common (77 occurrences).
- NEW: And every beast that parteth the hoof into two claws, and cheweth the cud among the beasts, that ye shall eat.

## cleft → rent — Micah 1:4
*(from batch_0017_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Rent" (616 occurrences) is genuinely different from "cleft," common, and reads correctly here ("the valleys shall be rent... as wax before the fire"), consistent with the violent imagery YLT renders as "the valleys do rend themselves."
- NEW: And the mountains shall be molten under him, and the valleys shall be rent, as wax before the fire, and as the waters that are poured down a steep place.

## clemency → gentleness — Acts 24:4
*(from batch_0017_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: YLT's witness reads "in thy gentleness" directly at this verse; "gentleness" (28 occurrences, a fruit of the Spirit at Galatians 5:23) is common and preserves Tertullus's flattering appeal, matching Tyndale/Geneva's sense of "courtesy."
- NEW: Notwithstanding, that I be not further tedious unto thee, I pray thee that thou wouldest hear us of thy gentleness a few words.

## clift → hole — Exodus 33:22
*(from batch_0017_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "a thee of the rock" is a garbled self-reference. DRC and BBE both render this "a hole of the rock"; "hole" is extremely common (1862 occurrences) and preserves the image of Moses sheltered in the rock.
- NEW: And it shall come to pass, while my glory passeth by, that I will put thee in a hole of the rock, and will cover thee with my hand while I pass by:

## clifts → hollow places — Job 30:6
*(from batch_0017_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "clefts" is merely a respelling of the flagged word "clifts," not a genuine replacement. BBE's "hollows of the valleys" points to the right sense, but the bare plural "hollows" has zero occurrences in the corpus; "hollow" (singular, 56 occurrences, e.g. Genesis 32:25 "hollow of his thigh") is common, so the phrase is rendered as "hollow places" to keep the plural sense with an attested word.
- NEW: To dwell in the hollow places of the valleys, in caves of the earth, and in the rocks.

## clifts → tops — Isaiah 57:5
*(from batch_0017_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "clefts" again merely respells the flagged word. Geneva's period witness reads "vnder the tops of the rocks," and "tops" is common in the corpus (126 occurrences); adopted as a genuine alternative per the witness construction.
- NEW: Enflaming yourselves with idols under every green tree, slaying the children in the valleys under the tops of the rocks?

## clipped → plucked — Jeremiah 48:37
*(from batch_0017_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "diminished" matches only YLT, a post-1611 translation, with no period-witness support. Geneva's period witness reads "euery beard plucked" directly; "plucked" is common (161 occurrences, e.g. Ezra 9:3, Nehemiah 13:25).
- NEW: For every head shall be bald, and every beard plucked: upon all the hands shall be cuttings, and upon the loins sackcloth.

## closer → nearer — Proverbs 18:24
*(from batch_0017_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "sticketh cleave than a brother" is ungrammatical — a verb was substituted where a comparative adjective belongs. Geneva's period witness reads "a friend is neerer then a brother"; "nearer" restores correct grammar and is attested (14 occurrences) though modest, it is the only period-witness option for this sense.
- NEW: A man that hath friends must shew himself friendly: and there is a friend that sticketh nearer than a brother.

## closet → chamber — Joel 2:16
*(from batch_0017_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "the bride out of her secret" is nonsensical, using an adjective as a noun. Geneva and DRC both render the bride's room "bride chamber"/"bridal chamber"; repeating "chamber" (already used for the bridegroom in the same verse) mirrors the Hebrew's own parallel structure and is common (910 occurrences).
- NEW: Gather the people, sanctify the congregation, assemble the elders, gather the children, and those that suck the breasts: let the bridegroom go forth of his chamber, and the bride out of her chamber.

## closet → chamber — Matthew 6:6
*(from batch_0017_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "chamber" is directly attested by three period witnesses at this verse — Tyndale, Geneva, and DRC all read "chamber" — and is common (910 occurrences).
- NEW: But thou, when thou prayest, enter into thy chamber, and when thou hast shut thy door, pray to thy Father which is in secret; and thy Father which seeth in secret shall reward thee openly.

## closets → chambers — Luke 12:3
*(from batch_0017_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "chambers" is directly attested by the period witness DRC ("spoken in the ear in the chambers") and is common (413 occurrences).
- NEW: Therefore whatsoever ye have spoken in darkness shall be heard in the light; and that which ye have spoken in the ear in chambers shall be proclaimed upon the housetops.

## clouted → mended — Joshua 9:5
*(from batch_0017_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "spotted" wrongly changes the meaning (spotted ≠ patched); "patched," suggested by Webster's modernized edition, has zero occurrences in the corpus. "Mended" (56 occurrences) is common, genuinely different from "clouted," and preserves the sense of old, repaired footwear (per DRC "clouted with patches").
- NEW: And old shoes and mended upon their feet, and old garments upon them; and all the bread of their provision was dry and mouldy.

## clouts → clothes — Jeremiah 38:11
*(from batch_0017_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "cloths" duplicates the meaning of the later "rags" too closely and is only weakly attested. Wycliffe's period witness reads "elde clothis" (old clothes); "clothes" is extremely common (707 occurrences) and stays distinct from "rags" later in the verse.
- NEW: So Ebed–melech took the men with him, and went into the house of the king under the treasury, and took thence old cast clothes and old rotten rags, and let them down by cords into the dungeon to Jeremiah.

## clouts → clothes — Jeremiah 38:12
*(from batch_0017_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same fix as the parallel verse above, for consistency — Wycliffe's "elde clothis" supports "clothes," common (707 occurrences) and distinct from "rags."
- NEW: And Ebed–melech the Ethiopian said unto Jeremiah, Put now these old cast clothes and rotten rags under thine armholes under the cords. And Jeremiah did so.

## cloven → (clause rewrite with parted) — Deuteronomy 14:7
*(from batch_0017_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "divide the rent hoof" is ungrammatical. Since "cloven" is itself the flagged word (and separately flagged at Acts 2:3), "parted" is used instead — it reuses the chapter's own vocabulary (v.6 "parteth the hoof") and is extremely common (1585 occurrences).
- NEW: Nevertheless these ye shall not eat of them that chew the cud, or of them that divide the parted hoof; as the camel, and the hare, and the coney: for they chew the cud, but divide not the hoof; therefore they are unclean unto you.

## cloven → parted — Acts 2:3
*(from batch_0017_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "divided tongues" matches only YLT, a post-1611 translation. DRC, a period witness (Douay-Rheims NT, 1582/1610), reads "parted tongues" directly, as does Darby; "parted" is extremely common (1585 occurrences).
- NEW: And there appeared unto them parted tongues like as of fire, and it sat upon each of them.

## cockle → tares — Job 31:40
*(from batch_0017_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "tares" matches Darby exactly, is genuinely different from "cockle," and is common and period-authentic (56 occurrences, cf. the parable of the tares in Matthew 13).
- NEW: Let thistles grow instead of wheat, and tares instead of barley. The words of Job are ended.

## cogitations → thoughts — Daniel 7:28
*(from batch_0018_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "thoughts" is a genuinely different word from "cogitations," is grammatically clean, and matches Wycliffe "thouytis" and DRC "thoughts" directly.
- NEW: Hitherto is the end of the matter. As for me Daniel, my thoughts much troubled me, and my countenance changed in me: but I kept the matter in my heart.

## collar → neck — Job 30:18
*(from batch_0018_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "mouth of my coat" is a modern-witness borrowing (YLT, not period) that reads as a mixed metaphor. "Neck" is a genuinely different, common word naming the same part of a garment, and matches the sense of Geneva/Wycliffe/DRC's "collar" (the coat's neck-opening) as well as BBE's modern gloss "neck of my coat."
- NEW: By the great force of my disease is my garment changed: it bindeth me about as the neck of my coat.

## collars → jewels — Judges 8:26
*(from batch_0018_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR duplicates "chains" later in the same verse ("...chains, and purple raiment... beside the chains..."), which is clumsy. Geneva1599 ("collers and iewels") and DRC ("ornaments, and jewels") both independently attest "jewels" for this item, giving a period-supported word that avoids the repetition.
- NEW: And the weight of the golden earrings that he requested was a thousand and seven hundred shekels of gold; beside ornaments, and jewels, and purple raiment that was on the kings of Midian, and beside the chains that were about their camels' necks.

## college → second quarter — II Kings 22:14
*(from batch_0018_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's bare "the second" is grammatically incomplete. Wycliffe's "the secounde dwellyng" and DRC's "the Second" attest a place-reference sense for the word "college" (a district of Jerusalem); "quarter" is added for clarity per the not-one-for-one rule, matching the sense-completing pattern also seen in Darby/ASV.
- NEW: So Hilkiah the priest, and Ahikam, and Achbor, and Shaphan, and Asahiah, went unto Huldah the prophetess, the wife of Shallum the son of Tikvah, the son of Harhas, keeper of the wardrobe; (now she dwelt in Jerusalem in the second quarter;) and they communed with her.

## college → second quarter — II Chronicles 34:22
*(from batch_0018_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same construction and evidence as II Kings 22:14 — Wycliffe "the secounde warde," DRC "the Second part" — supplemented with "quarter" for clarity.
- NEW: And Hilkiah, and they that the king had appointed, went to Huldah the prophetess, the wife of Shallum the son of Tikvath, the son of Hasrah, keeper of the wardrobe; (now she dwelt in Jerusalem in the second quarter:) and they spake to her to that effect.

## collops → fat (rephrased) — Job 15:27
*(from batch_0018_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "maketh fat of fat" is nonsensical, doubling the same word without meaning. DRC's rendering of this verse ("Fatness hath covered his face...and the fat hangeth down on his sides") supplies a period-attested clause construction that conveys the same image without keeping "collops."
- NEW: Because he covereth his face with his fatness, and the fat hangeth down upon his flanks.

## colony → free city — Acts 16:12
*(from batch_0018_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR left "colony" untouched, which violates the rule that the flagged word must always be replaced. Tyndale's period rendering, "a fre cite," supplies a direct, genuinely different, period-attested alternative.
- NEW: And from thence to Philippi, which is the chief city of that part of Macedonia, and a free city: and we were in that city abiding certain days.

## comers → them that draw nigh — Hebrews 10:1
*(from batch_0018_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "the worshippers thereunto" is grammatically awkward ("worshippers thereunto" doesn't parse naturally). Wycliffe's "men neiyinge" (men drawing nigh) attests the underlying sense of "comers," and this clause-level rewrite mirrors that construction while replacing "comers" outright, consistent with rule 1 (not one-for-one).
- NEW: For the law having a shadow of good things to come, and not the very image of the things, can never with those sacrifices which they offered year by year continually make them that draw nigh thereunto perfect.

## comfortable → pleasant — II Samuel 14:17
*(from batch_0018_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "shall now be rest" is ungrammatical (a noun standing where an adjective is needed). "Pleasant" is a genuinely different, common adjective conveying the same sense of an agreeable word, matching the "pleasant/agreeable" register of Early Modern usage without the grammar break.
- NEW: Then thine handmaid said, The word of my lord the king shall now be pleasant: for as an angel of God, so is my lord the king to discern good and bad: therefore the Lord thy God will be with thee.

## comfortable → pleasant — Zechariah 1:13
*(from batch_0018_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same defect as II Samuel 14:17 — CUR's "rest words" is not idiomatic English. "Pleasant words" is a genuinely different adjective, grammatically sound, and consistent with the companion entry above.
- NEW: And the Lord answered the angel that talked with me with good words and pleasant words.

## comfortedst → hast eased — Isaiah 12:1
*(from batch_0018_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR merely stripped the -edst ending from the same word ("thou comfort me"), which both breaks thou/verb agreement and violates the rule against re-inflecting rather than replacing the flagged word. DRC's "hast comforted me" and Darby's "hast comforted me" model the have + past-participle construction; substituting a genuinely different verb, "eased" (a plain, common Early Modern word for relief from distress), keeps the grammar correct.
- NEW: And in that day thou shalt say, O Lord, I will praise thee: though thou wast angry with me, thine anger is turned away, and thou hast eased me.

## comfortless → fatherless — John 14:18
*(from batch_0018_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Geneva1599 renders this verse almost verbatim as "I will not leave you fatherless," giving strong, near-exact period attestation for a genuinely different word conveying the same sense as DRC's "orphans."
- NEW: I will not leave you fatherless: I will come to you.

## commander → master — Isaiah 55:4
*(from batch_0018_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "command" changes the noun's sense entirely (an abstract command, not a person), making "a leader and command to the people" nonsensical. Geneva1599 and DRC both render this "a prince and a master," supplying a period-attested noun for a person that preserves the parallel with "leader."
- NEW: Behold, I have given him for a witness to the people, a leader and master to the people.

## commendation → recommendation — II Corinthians 3:1
*(from batch_0018_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Geneva1599 attests this word choice almost verbatim ("epistles of recommendation vnto you, or letters of recommendation from you"), making CUR's swap both genuinely different and strongly period-supported.
- NEW: Do we begin again to commend ourselves? or need we, as some others, epistles of recommendation to you, or letters of recommendation from you?

## commission → license — Acts 26:12
*(from batch_0018_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "charge" is ambiguous (could mean accusation or task) and not witness-supported. Tyndale's period rendering, "auctorite and licence," gives a genuinely different, precise, period-attested alternative naming the same grant of authority.
- NEW: Whereupon as I went to Damascus with authority and license from the chief priests,

## commissions → decrees — Ezra 8:36
*(from batch_0018_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "laws" is generic and flattens the sense of a specific royal order. DRC's "the king's edicts" and Wycliffe's "the comaundementis of the kyng" both attest a decree/command sense; "decrees" captures this precisely and is the common Early Modern word for a royal order (cf. its heavy use for royal orders in Daniel/Esther-style contexts).
- NEW: And they delivered the king's decrees unto the king's lieutenants, and to the governors on this side the river: and they furthered the people, and the house of God.

## commodious → convenient — Acts 27:12
*(from batch_0018_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "convenient" is a genuinely different word from "commodious," grammatically sound, and carries the older Early Modern sense of "fitting/suitable" needed here.
- NEW: And because the haven was not convenient to winter in, the more part advised to depart thence also, if by any means they might attain to Phenice, and there to winter; which is an haven of Crete, and lieth toward the south west and north west.

## commonly → spread abroad — Matthew 28:15
*(from batch_0018_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Owner Choice - changed to spread abroad 7/17/26 - CUR's "utterly reported" changes the meaning entirely (utterly = completely, not commonly/widely). Tyndale ("is noysed amoge the Iewes") and DRC ("was spread abroad") both attest "noised abroad" for this exact sense, giving strong period support for a genuinely different phrase.
- NEW: So they took the money, and did as they were taught: and this saying is spread abroad among the Jews until this day.

## commonly → certainly — I Corinthians 5:1
*(from batch_0018_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same defect as Matthew 28:15 — "utterly" misstates the sense. Geneva1599 attests "It is heard certainely," supplying a genuinely different, period-attested word.
- NEW: It is reported certainly that there is fornication among you, and such fornication as is not so much as named among the Gentiles, that one should have his father's wife.

## commonwealth → birthright — Ephesians 2:12
*(from batch_0018_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Owner choice - 7/17/26 - commonwealth and alternatives do not make grammatical sense in context of non jews inheriting the promise god gave the jews.  Being part of the conversations and commonwealth of israel bears little relevance.
- NEW: That at that time ye were without Christ, being aliens from the birthright of Israel, and strangers from the covenants of promise, having no hope, and without God in the world:

## commotion → tumult — Jeremiah 10:22
*(from batch_0018_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "earthquake" mistakes the sense — this is a tumult/uproar of invading armies from the north, not a seismic event. "Tumult" is a genuinely different, common word conveying the correct sense, consistent with the companion entry (commotions → seditions) below.
- NEW: Behold, the noise of the bruit is come, and a great tumult out of the north country, to make the cities of Judah desolate, and a den of dragons.

## commotions → seditions — Luke 21:9
*(from batch_0018_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "tumults" is plausible but unattested locally. Geneva1599 and DRC both independently render this "wars and seditions" (Tyndale: "dissencion"), giving stronger period support for a genuinely different word.
- NEW: But when ye shall hear of wars and seditions, be not terrified: for these things must first come to pass; but the end is not by and by.

## compact → knit together — Psalms 122:3
*(from batch_0018_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "together together" duplicates the word already present in the verse, which is clearly broken. Geneva1599's own translators use "knit together" for this same sense of firm joining elsewhere (Ephesians 4:16, "coupled and knit together"), giving period-consistent vocabulary for a genuinely different word.
- NEW: Jerusalem is builded as a city that is knit together:

## compacted → knit — Ephesians 4:16
*(from batch_0018_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "gathered" loses the sense of fitted joining. Geneva1599 attests "coupled and knit together by euery ioynt" directly in this verse, supplying a genuinely different, period-attested word.
- NEW: From whom the whole body fitly joined together and knit by that which every joint supplieth, according to the effectual working in the measure of every part, maketh increase of the body unto the edifying of itself in love.

## comparable → like — Lamentations 4:2
*(from batch_0018_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "like to fine gold" is a genuinely different construction, grammatically sound, and uses the common Early Modern "like to" pattern independent of any single witness borrowing.
- NEW: The precious sons of Zion, like to fine gold, how are they esteemed as earthen pitchers, the work of the hands of the potter!

## complainers → backbiters — Jude 1:16
*(from batch_0019_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "backbiters" is a genuinely different word, correctly inflected, and attested as period vocabulary in Geneva1599 (14 hits), fitting Jude's catalogue of vices.
- NEW: These are murmurers, backbiters, walking after their own lusts; and their mouth speaketh great swelling words, having men's persons in admiration because of advantage.

## composition → making — Exodus 30:32
*(from batch_0019_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "tale" is nonsensical (a story, not a formula). Wycliffe reads "bi the makyng therof ye schulen not make another" — a period witness attesting "making" as the term for the recipe/formula, common in the corpus (182 hits).
- NEW: Upon man's flesh shall it not be poured, neither shall ye make any other like it, after the making of it: it is holy, and it shall be holy unto you.

## composition → making — Exodus 30:37
*(from batch_0019_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same fix as Exodus 30:32 — Wycliffe again attests "makyng" for this recipe/formula sense in the companion verse; "tale" is meaningless here.
- NEW: And as for the perfume which thou shalt make, ye shall not make to yourselves according to the making thereof: it shall be unto thee holy for the Lord.

## compound → tempered — Exodus 30:25
*(from batch_0019_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR duplicates "ointment ointment," a broken text. "Compounded" is merely a re-inflection of the flagged lemma (disallowed). "Tempered" is a genuinely different word for mixing/blending, well attested as period vocabulary in Geneva1599 and Wycliffe (112 hits each), and common in the KJV corpus (56 hits).
- NEW: And thou shalt make it an oil of holy ointment, an ointment tempered after the art of the apothecary: it shall be an holy anointing oil.

## compoundeth → maketh — Exodus 30:33
*(from batch_0019_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "maketh" is directly attested for this verse in both Geneva1599 ("make the like oyntment") and Wycliffe ("makith sich oile"), and is extremely common in the corpus (812 hits).
- NEW: Whosoever maketh any like it, or whosoever putteth any of it upon a stranger, shall even be cut off from his people.

## comprehend → know — Job 37:5
*(from batch_0019_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "know" is directly attested for this verse in Geneva1599 ("which we know not") and is extremely common in the corpus.
- NEW: God thundereth marvellously with his voice; great things doeth he, which we cannot know.

## comprehend → know — Ephesians 3:18
*(from batch_0019_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: No period witness diverges from "comprehend" here, so the nearest defensible genuinely-different word is the one already ruled sound at Job 37:5; "know" is extremely common in the corpus and the construction "able to know what is" is standard biblical idiom.
- NEW: May be able to know with all saints what is the breadth, and length, and depth, and height;

## concern → about — Acts 28:31
*(from batch_0019_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR is grammatically broken ("things which about the Lord"). ACV models the needed clause rewrite by dropping "which": "teaching the things about the Lord Jesus Christ" — genuinely different from "concern" and grammatically sound.
- NEW: Preaching the kingdom of God, and teaching those things about the Lord Jesus Christ, with all confidence, no man forbidding him.

## concern → about — II Corinthians 11:30
*(from batch_0019_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same grammatical break as Acts 28:31 ("things which about"). Apply the same clause fix, dropping "which."
- NEW: If I must needs glory, I will glory of the things about mine infirmities.

## concerneth → pertaineth — Psalms 138:8
*(from batch_0019_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "meaneth" changes the sense entirely (signifies vs. pertains to). "Pertaineth" is a genuinely different word carrying the same sense of "belongs to/concerns," common in the corpus (49 hits); its construction requires the preposition "unto," which is added per the grammar rule for swapped words.
- NEW: The Lord will perfect that which pertaineth unto me: thy mercy, O Lord, endureth for ever: forsake not the works of thine own hands.

## concerneth → pertaineth — Ezekiel 12:10
*(from batch_0019_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same fix as Psalms 138:8 — "meaneth" gives the wrong sense; "pertaineth unto" preserves the intended meaning with a genuinely different, well-attested word.
- NEW: Say thou unto them, Thus saith the Lord God; This burden pertaineth unto the prince in Jerusalem, and all the house of Israel that are among them.

## concision → false circumcision — Philippians 3:2
*(from batch_0019_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: The batch itself flagged this as having no safe one-word swap. RWebster's own period-adjacent rendering clarifies the sense as "the false circumcision," a phrase built from "circumcision," well attested as period vocabulary in Geneva1599 (231 hits) and Tyndale (112 hits), and theologically accurate to Paul's wordplay against mere physical "cutting."
- NEW: Beware of dogs, beware of evil workers, beware of the false circumcision.

## concord → agreement — II Corinthians 6:15
*(from batch_0019_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: No period witness diverges from "concord" here, but "agreement" is a genuinely different word, reasonably well attested in the corpus (42 hits), and captures the sense of harmony intended.
- NEW: And what agreement hath Christ with Belial? or what part hath he that believeth with an infidel?

## concourse → multitudes — Proverbs 1:21
*(from batch_0019_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "roar" is nonsensical ("chief place of roar"). DRC reads "At the head of multitudes she crieth out" and Wycliffe "heed of cumpenyes" (head of crowds) — both period witnesses attest "multitude(s)" for this crowded public place, and it is extremely common in the corpus (161 hits for the plural).
- NEW: She crieth in the chief place of multitudes, in the openings of the gates: in the city she uttereth her words, saying,

## concourse → tumult — Acts 19:40
*(from batch_0019_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "noise" is plausible but unsupported by any period witness (Tyndale, Geneva1599, and DRC all keep "concourse"). "Tumult" is a genuinely different, well-attested word (154 hits) for a disorderly gathering, and it echoes "uproar" earlier in the same verse, mirroring the verse's own near-repetition.
- NEW: For we are in danger to be called in question for this day's uproar, there being no cause whereby we may give an account of this tumult.

## condescend → make yourselves equal — Romans 12:16
*(from batch_0019_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "away" is grammatically broken ("but away to men of low estate"). Tyndale and Geneva1599 both attest the clause "make yourselves equal to them of the lower sort" — a direct period-witness construction replacing "condescend" entirely.
- NEW: Be of the same mind one toward another. Mind not high things, but make yourselves equal to men of low estate. Be not wise in your own eyes.

## coney → rock badger — Leviticus 11:5
*(from batch_0020_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "shaphan" is a bare transliteration, not English. Darby's witness rendering "rock-badger" gives an authentic period-flavored alternative; "badger"/"badgers" is well attested in KJV.db (98 hits, e.g. the tabernacle's "badgers' skins"), so "rock badger" is not itself a new rare coinage.
- NEW: And the rock badger, because he cheweth the cud, but divideth not the hoof; he is unclean unto you.

## coney → rock badger — Deuteronomy 14:7
*(from batch_0020_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same fix as Leviticus 11:5 for consistency — "shaphan" is not English; Darby's "rock-badger" is the period-witness alternative and "badger(s)" is common in KJV.db.
- NEW: Nevertheless these ye shall not eat of them that chew the cud, or of them that divide the cloven hoof; as the camel, and the hare, and the rock badger: for they chew the cud, but divide not the hoof; therefore they are unclean unto you.

## confection → composed — Exodus 30:35
*(from batch_0020_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "a ointment" is both wrong article (needs "an") and wrong sense — this incense is not applied like an ointment. Geneva1599's own rendering of this verse reads "a perfume composed after the arte of the apothecarie," which drops the noun for a verb and resolves the article problem outright; DRC's "compounded" confirms the sense.
- NEW: And thou shalt make it a perfume, composed after the art of the apothecary, tempered together, pure and holy:

## conference → imparted — Galatians 2:6
*(from batch_0020_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR reads "in add added nothing to me" — a broken duplicate. ASV's "imparted nothing to me" and Darby's "communicated nothing" both point to a clean verb replacing the noun clause; "impart" is attested in KJV (Romans 1:11, 1 Thessalonians 2:8).
- NEW: But of these who seemed to be somewhat, (whatsoever they were, it maketh no matter to me: God accepteth no man's person:) for they who seemed to be somewhat imparted nothing to me:

## confidently → boldly — Luke 22:59
*(from batch_0020_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "constantly affirmed" shifts the meaning from "with confidence/certainty" to "continually," which is not the sense (a single assertion, not a repeated one). "Boldly" is the KJV's own idiom for confident, assertive speech (Acts 4:31, 9:27, 9:29, 14:3, 19:8) and is well attested; it matches the assertive tone Darby ("stoutly maintained") and BSB ("insisted") both preserve.
- NEW: And about the space of one hour after another boldly affirmed, saying, Of a truth this fellow also was with him: for he is a Galilean.

## conflict → fight — Philippians 1:30
*(from batch_0020_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Geneva1599 renders this exact verse with "fight" ("Hauing the same fight"), a direct period-witness match, and "fight" is one of the most common nouns in the KJV corpus.
- NEW: Having the same fight which ye saw in me, and now hear to be in me.

## conflict → fighting — Colossians 2:1
*(from batch_0020_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Geneva1599's witness for this verse specifically uses the gerund "fighting" ("what great fighting I haue for your sakes"), not the bare noun "fight" CUR chose. "Fightings" (plural) is attested in KJV.db (James 4:1, 2 Corinthians 7:5), so the singular gerund form is a safe, witness-matched adjustment.
- NEW: For I would that ye knew what great fighting I have for you, and for them at Laodicea, and for as many as have not seen my face in the flesh;

## conformable → made like — Philippians 3:10
*(from batch_0020_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR reads "being made being unto his death" — a broken duplicated word. Wycliffe's witness "be maad lijk to his deeth" gives a clean, genuine alternative; "like unto" is an extremely common KJV construction.
- NEW: That I may know him, and the power of his resurrection, and the fellowship of his sufferings, being made like unto his death;

## conformed → made like to — Romans 8:29
*(from batch_0020_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "fashioned to the image" merely grafts a new word into the old "conformed to" frame, which rule 5 disfavors. Geneva1599's own construction, "made like to the image," is the more faithful witness-copy and avoids the awkward "fashioned to" collocation.
- NEW: For whom he did foreknow, he also did predestinate to be made like to the image of his Son, that he might be the firstborn among many brethren.

## conformed → fashioned according to — Romans 12:2
*(from batch_0020_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "fashioned to this world" keeps the old preposition from "conformed to," an ill fit for "fashioned." ASV's witness reading, "not fashioned according to this world," and Geneva's "fashion not your selues like vnto this worlde" both support "according to" as the natural preposition with "fashioned."
- NEW: And be not fashioned according to this world: but be ye transformed by the renewing of your mind, that ye may prove what is that good, and acceptable, and perfect, will of God.

## confused → tumultuous — Isaiah 9:5
*(from batch_0020_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "confounded noise" shifts the sense from disorderly battle-din to being put to shame/perplexed, which is a different concept. Darby's "tumult" and ASV/ACV's "in the tumult" point to the tumult/uproar sense; "tumultuous noise" preserves the original noun-adjective shape while genuinely replacing the flagged word with the KJV's own battle-din vocabulary (tumult is common in the corpus).
- NEW: For every battle of the warrior is with tumultuous noise, and garments rolled in blood; but this shall be with burning and fuel of fire.

## confused → out of order — Acts 19:32
*(from batch_0020_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "confounded" again shifts from "disorderly/in an uproar" to "ashamed/perplexed." Geneva1599's own witness for this verse reads "for the assemblie was out of order," a direct period-witness match for the correct sense.
- NEW: Some therefore cried one thing, and some another: for the assembly was out of order; and the more part knew not wherefore they were come together.

## congealed → curdled — Exodus 15:8
*(from batch_0020_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "settled" loses the sense of solidifying/thickening. "Curdled" preserves that exact sense and is already an attested KJV word in the same semantic register (Job 10:10, "curdled me like cheese"), making it a genuine, well-grounded replacement rather than a new coinage.
- NEW: And with the blast of thy nostrils the waters were gathered together, the floods stood upright as an heap, and the depths were curdled in the heart of the sea.

## conies → rock badgers — Psalms 104:18
*(from batch_0020_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Shaphanes" is a bare transliteration/plural mangling, not English. Consistent with the Leviticus/Deuteronomy conies entries, Darby's "rock-badgers" is the period-witness alternative, and "badgers" is attested in KJV.db.
- NEW: The high hills are a refuge for the wild goats; and the rocks for the rock badgers.

## conies → rock badgers — Proverbs 30:26
*(from batch_0020_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same fix as Psalms 104:18 for consistency — "shaphanes" is not English; "rock badgers" is the witness-supported, attested alternative.
- NEW: The rock badgers are but a feeble folk, yet make they their houses in the rocks;

## consentedst → wast well pleased — Psalms 50:18
*(from batch_0020_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "thou accept with him" breaks both tense and idiom. Darby's witness "thou didst take pleasure in him" points toward a "pleased/pleasure" construction; "well pleased" is one of the most common phrases in the KJV corpus (e.g. "in whom I am well pleased"), giving a strong, well-attested, genuinely different rendering in the correct past tense.
- NEW: When thou sawest a thief, then thou wast well pleased with him, and hast been partaker with adulterers.

## consist → stand — Colossians 1:17
*(from batch_0020_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "commend" is wrong in sense (commend = praise/entrust, not "hold together/exist by"). "Stand" is a common, well-attested KJV verb used for being upheld/continuing in being, giving a genuine and idiomatic replacement for the abstract Latinate "consist."
- NEW: And he is before all things, and by him all things stand.

## consisteth → standeth — Luke 12:15
*(from batch_0020_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "meaneth not in the abundance" is ungrammatical ("mean" does not take this construction). Tyndale and Geneva both render this exact clause with "standeth" ("his life stondeth not in the aboundaunce"/"his life standeth not in his riches"), a direct double period-witness match.
- NEW: And he said unto them, Take heed, and beware of covetousness: for a man's life standeth not in the abundance of the things which he possesseth.

## consorted → joined themselves to — Acts 17:4
*(from batch_0020_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "joined with" is close in sense but grafts the new word loosely onto the old frame. Darby's witness gives the fuller idiomatic construction, "joined themselves to Paul and Silas," matching the common KJV reflexive-joining idiom (e.g. Numbers 25:3).
- NEW: And some of them believed, and joined themselves to Paul and Silas; and of the devout Greeks a great multitude, and of the chief women not a few.

## constant → stedfast — I Chronicles 28:7
*(from batch_0020_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "be hold" is ungrammatical (hold is not an adjective here). Darby's witness "firm" points to steadfastness as the sense; "stedfast" is the KJV's own characteristic word for this exact idea (1 Corinthians 15:58, "be ye stedfast, unmoveable") and is well attested.
- NEW: Moreover I will establish his kingdom for ever, if he be stedfast to do my commandments and my judgments, as at this day.

## constellations → sky — Isaiah 13:10
*(from batch_0020_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Owner's choice. 7/17/26 -  Constellations and planets did not exist as concepts during biblical times of Jesus.
- NEW: For the stars of heaven and the sky thereof shall not give their light: the sun shall be darkened in his going forth, and the moon shall not cause her light to shine.

## constrain → compel — Galatians 6:12
*(from batch_0020_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Darby and ASV both render this exact verse with "compel," and "compel" is well attested throughout the KJV corpus (e.g. Matthew 5:41).
- NEW: As many as desire to make a fair shew in the flesh, they compel you to be circumcised; only lest they should suffer persecution for the cross of Christ.

## constraineth → compelleth — Job 32:18
*(from batch_0020_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "keepeth" misses the sense of inward pressure/urging to speak. Geneva1599's witness reads "the spirite within me compelleth me" exactly; "compel" is a common KJV lemma (compelled, compel, compellest all occur), so the -eth inflection is a safe grammatical extension even though this exact form is otherwise rare.
- NEW: For I am full of matter, the spirit within me compelleth me.

## constraineth → compelleth — II Corinthians 5:14
*(from batch_0020_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same correction as Job 32:18 for consistency — "keepeth" misses the sense; "compelleth" (BSB: "Christ's love compels us") gives a genuine, witness-supported replacement built on the common lemma "compel."
- NEW: For the love of Christ compelleth us; because we thus judge, that if one died for all, then were all dead:

## constraint → necessity — I Peter 5:2
*(from batch_0020_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Darby's witness renders this exact verse "not by necessity, but willingly," a direct match for CUR's word; "necessity" is well attested in the KJV corpus and closely parallels 2 Corinthians 9:7 ("not grudgingly, or of necessity"), a strong thematic match.
- NEW: Feed the flock of God which is among you, taking the oversight thereof, not by necessity, but willingly; not for filthy lucre, but of a ready mind;

## consult → devise — Psalms 62:4
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "counsel" ("They only counsel to cast him down") is grammatically strange — counsel wants a direct object, not "to cast." "Devise" is common in the Psalms for hostile scheming ("he deviseth mischief upon his bed," Ps 36:4) and takes an infinitive naturally in this idiom.
- NEW: They only devise to cast him down from his excellency: they delight in lies: they bless with their mouth, but they curse inwardly. Selah.

## consultation → counsel — Mark 15:1
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "held a counsel" genuinely replaces the rare word and mirrors Geneva1599's "helde a Councill" and Tyndale's "helde counsell" construction exactly.
- NEW: And straightway in the morning the chief priests held a counsel with the elders and scribes and the whole council, and bound Jesus, and carried him away, and delivered him to Pilate.

## consulter → counselleth with — Deuteronomy 18:11
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "a ask with familiar spirits" is broken (bare verb where a noun/clause is required). Geneva1599 supplies the exact construction: "that counselleth with spirits." Adapting it as a relative clause keeps the parallel list intact.
- NEW: Or a charmer, or one that counselleth with familiar spirits, or a wizard, or a necromancer.

## consulteth → taketh counsel — Luke 14:31
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "counselleth whether he be able" misreads counsel as advising others rather than deliberating with himself. Geneva1599's "taketh counsell, whether he be able" is the period construction for self-deliberation and fits the sentence cleanly.
- NEW: Or what king, going to make war against another king, sitteth not down first, and taketh counsel whether he be able with ten thousand to meet him that cometh against him with twenty thousand?

## consummation → end — Daniel 9:27
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "the end" is a genuine, common replacement, echoed by ASV's "the full end."
- NEW: And he shall confirm the covenant with many for one week: and in the midst of the week he shall cause the sacrifice and the oblation to cease, and for the overspreading of abominations he shall make it desolate, even until the end, and that determined shall be poured upon the desolate.

## contemn → scorn — Psalms 10:13
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "scorn" is a genuine, common KJV-register verb and the do-support ("doth...scorn") is grammatically correct as bare infinitive after "doth."
- NEW: Wherefore doth the wicked scorn God? he hath said in his heart, Thou wilt not require it.

## contemn → scorn — Ezekiel 21:13
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "scorn" preserves the subjunctive construction ("what if the sword scorn"), matching the mood of the original, and RWebster/YLT confirm the "despise" sense-family is the period reading.
- NEW: Because it is a trial, and what if the sword scorn even the rod? it shall be no more, saith the Lord God.

## contemneth → despiseth — Ezekiel 21:10
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "despiseth" is directly attested by RWebster ("despiseth"), YLT ("despising"), and BSB ("despises").
- NEW: It is sharpened to make a sore slaughter; it is furbished that it may glitter: should we then make mirth? it despiseth the rod of my son, as every tree.

## contemptuously → scornfully — Psalms 31:18
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "scornfully" is a genuine replacement in the same semantic family as Geneva1599's "spitefully" and the "contempt" family used by ASV/Darby/ACV.
- NEW: Let the lying lips be put to silence; which speak grievous things proudly and scornfully against the righteous.

## contentment → sufficiency — I Timothy 6:6
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "sufficiency" is directly attested by Wycliffe: "a greet wynnyng is pitee, with sufficience."
- NEW: But godliness with sufficiency is great gain.

## contradicting → gainsaying — Acts 13:45
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "gainsaying" is a well-attested KJV-family word (cf. "gainsayers," "gainsay" elsewhere in this batch) and fits Darby's bracketed "[contradicting and] speaking injuriously."
- NEW: But when the Jews saw the multitudes, they were filled with envy, and spake against those things which were spoken by Paul, gainsaying and blaspheming.

## contradiction → controversy — Hebrews 7:7
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "controversy" is a direct, exact match to YLT: "apart from all controversy."
- NEW: And without all controversy the less is blessed of the better.

## contradiction → gainsaying — Hebrews 12:3
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "strife" is awkward here ("such strife of sinners against himself" implies mutual conflict rather than one-sided hostility endured). ASV's "such gainsaying of sinners against himself" and YLT's "such gainsaying from the sinners" match the sentence exactly and use the same word-family already approved for Acts 13:45 above.
- NEW: For consider him that endured such gainsaying of sinners against himself, lest ye be wearied and faint in your minds.

## contribution → distribution — Romans 15:26
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "fellowship" makes for strange idiom ("make a certain fellowship for the poor saints" — fellowship is a state, not a thing "made for" someone). Tyndale and Geneva1599 both read "make a certaine distribution vnto/vpon the poore Saintes," a direct period-attested construction.
- NEW: For it hath pleased them of Macedonia and Achaia to make a certain distribution for the poor saints which are at Jerusalem.

## conveniently → occasion — Mark 14:11
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: The auto-system flagged this itself as having no safe one-word swap ("season" produces the nonsensical "he sought how he might season betray him"). Rephrasing with the KJV idiom "sought occasion" (cf. Judges 14:4 "he sought an occasion") restores sense and grammar without inventing new content, since the underlying idea — Judas looking for his moment — is preserved.
- NEW: And when they heard it, they were glad, and promised to give him money. And he sought occasion how he might betray him.

## conversant → dwelt — Joshua 8:35
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "were go among them" is ungrammatical. Wycliffe ("dwelliden among hem"), DRC ("dwelt among them"), and BSB ("lived among them") converge on this sense; "dwelt" is the common KJV-register choice (cf. Judges 1:21).
- NEW: There was not a word of all that Moses commanded, which Joshua read not before all the congregation of Israel, with the women, and the little ones, and the strangers that dwelt among them.

## conversant → went — I Samuel 25:15
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "were go with them" is ungrammatical. ASV reads "as long as we went with them" for this exact verse — a direct, exact-match witness.
- NEW: But the men were very good unto us, and we were not hurt, neither missed we any thing, as long as we went with them, when we were in the fields:

## conversion → turning — Acts 15:3
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "turning" is genuine and fits the wider Acts idiom of Gentiles "turning" to God (cf. Acts 26:18, 26:20); no witness offers a better single-word alternative since all period witnesses retain "conversion."
- NEW: And being brought on their way by the church, they passed through Phenice and Samaria, declaring the turning of the Gentiles: and they caused great joy unto all the brethren.

## convert → return — Isaiah 6:10
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "return" is attested in sense by ASV ("turn again") and BSB ("turn"); "return" is common and fits the verse's rhythm.
- NEW: Make the heart of this people fat, and make their ears heavy, and shut their eyes; lest they see with their eyes, and hear with their ears, and understand with their heart, and return, and be healed.

## convert → turn him back — James 5:19
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "one return him" is not idiomatic (return does not normally take a direct object this way). YLT's "turn him back" is the exact period-witness construction and is grammatical.
- NEW: Brethren, if any of you do err from the truth, and one turn him back;

## converteth → turneth back — James 5:20
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "goeth the sinner" is ungrammatical (goeth cannot take a direct object). YLT's "turn back a sinner" supplies the correct transitive construction; "turneth back" carries the required -eth inflection.
- NEW: Let him know, that he which turneth back the sinner from the error of his way shall save a soul from death, and shall hide a multitude of sins.

## converting → restoring — Psalms 19:7
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "bringing the soul" is incomplete (bringing it where?). Darby and ASV both read "restoring the soul" for this exact verse — a strong, direct match.
- NEW: The law of the Lord is perfect, restoring the soul: the testimony of the Lord is sure, making wise the simple.

## converts → they that return in her — Isaiah 1:27
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "her needs" abandons the sense entirely (converts are people who turn back, not "needs"). Geneva1599 ("they that returne in her") and DRC ("they shall bring her back") both supply this construction directly.
- NEW: Zion shall be redeemed with judgment, and they that return in her with righteousness.

## convey → carry — I Kings 5:9
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "I will through them by sea" uses "through" as a verb, which is not English. "Carry" is a common, plain KJV verb that fits the sense (cf. BSB "float them as rafts"); using "float" instead would awkwardly double the following noun "floats."
- NEW: My servants shall bring them down from Lebanon unto the sea: and I will carry them by sea in floats unto the place that thou shalt appoint me, and will cause them to be discharged there, and thou shalt receive them: and thou shalt accomplish my desire, in giving food for my household.

## convey → let me pass through — Nehemiah 2:7
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "may through me over" is ungrammatical for the same reason as above. RWebster, ASV, and ACV all independently render this exact verse as "let me pass through," a strong three-witness match.
- NEW: Moreover I said unto the king, If it please the king, let letters be given me to the governors beyond the river, that they may let me pass through till I come into Judah;

## conveyed → withdrawn — John 5:13
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "withdrawn himself away" is a genuine replacement and matches RWebster's "Jesus had withdrawn himself" for this exact verse.
- NEW: And he that was healed wist not who it was: for Jesus had withdrawn himself away, a multitude being in that place.

## convicted → accused — John 8:9
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "reproved by their own conscience" implies an external corrector, which does not fit an internal, reflexive prompting. Geneva1599 supplies the exact phrase for this verse: "being accused by their owne conscience."
- NEW: And they which heard it, being accused by their own conscience, went out one by one, beginning at the eldest, even unto the last: and Jesus was left alone, and the woman standing in the midst.

## convince → confound — Titus 1:9
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "rebuke the gainsayers" loses the sense of argumentative refutation that "convince" carried; the surrounding chapter (v. 11, "whose mouths must be stopped") shows the idea is silencing opponents by argument, not simple correction. "Confound" carries this sense and is grammatically parallel to 1 Corinthians 1:27's "to confound the wise" — a well-attested KJV construction.
- NEW: Holding fast the faithful word as he hath been taught, that he may be able by sound doctrine both to exhort and to confound the gainsayers.

## convince → rebuke — Jude 1:15
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "rebuke" is a genuine, very common KJV word and fits the judgment-day tone of the passage; RWebster/YLT/Darby/ASV confirm the "convict" sense-family as the period reading, which "rebuke" adequately carries in context.
- NEW: To execute judgment upon all, and to rebuke all that are ungodly among them of all their ungodly deeds which they have ungodly committed, and of all their hard speeches which ungodly sinners have spoken against him.

## convinceth → rebuketh — John 8:46
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "telleth me of sin" drastically changes the meaning (informing vs. proving guilty). Tyndale and Geneva1599 both read "can rebuke me of sinne" for this exact verse.
- NEW: Which of you rebuketh me of sin? And if I say the truth, why do ye not believe me?

## cool → wind — Genesis 3:8
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "spirit of the day" abandons the physical sense entirely (a breeze, not a metaphysical spirit). YLT and BSB both render this exact phrase "the breeze of the day," and BBE has "the evening wind." "Wind" is common throughout the KJV corpus and captures the same physical referent as "breeze" without introducing a word absent from the corpus.
- NEW: And they heard the voice of the Lord God walking in the garden in the wind of the day: and Adam and his wife hid themselves from the presence of the Lord God amongst the trees of the garden.

## cool → refresh — Luke 16:24
*(from batch_0021_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "spirit my tongue" is nonsensical (spirit cannot function as a verb here). No period witness truly supplies a different word (Wycliffe "kele" and Tyndale "cole" are simply older spellings of "cool," not distinct words). "Refresh" is common and period-attested for relief from distress (cf. Philemon 1:7, "the bowels of the saints are refreshed") and fits the torment context of this verse.
- NEW: And he cried and said, Father Abraham, have mercy on me, and send Lazarus, that he may dip the tip of his finger in water, and refresh my tongue; for I am tormented in this flame.

## coping → top — I Kings 7:9
*(from batch_0022_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "top" is a genuinely different, very common KJV word (637 occurrences) and matches DRC's "top of the walls" for this verse; the phrase reads as authentic 1611 construction.
- NEW: All these were of costly stones, according to the measures of hewed stones, sawed with saws, within and without, even from the foundation unto the top, and so on the outside toward the great court.

## copper → brass — Ezra 8:27
*(from batch_0022_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "brasen" is an adjective with no noun to modify ("vessels of fine brasen" is ungrammatical). Geneva1599 and DRC both read "vessels of shining brass"; "brass" is a common KJV noun (882 occurrences).
- NEW: Also twenty basons of gold, of a thousand drams; and two vessels of fine brass, precious as gold.

## coppersmith → smith — II Timothy 4:14
*(from batch_0022_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "founder" is ambiguous (implies "one who founds/establishes," not a metalworker). Darby renders this word "the smith"; "smith" occurs 21x in KJV.db, a common attested noun.
- NEW: Alexander the smith did me much evil: the Lord reward him according to his works:

## cornfloor → threshingfloor — Hosea 9:1
*(from batch_0022_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "every corn" is missing its noun and is ungrammatical. RWebster renders this same verse "threshingfloor," a well-attested compound in KJV.db (119 occurrences).
- NEW: Rejoice not, O Israel, for joy, as other people: for thou hast gone a whoring from thy God, thou hast loved a reward upon every threshingfloor.

## corpse → body — Mark 6:29
*(from batch_0022_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Wycliffe, Tyndale, Geneva1599, and DRC all render this verse "took up his body." "body" is extremely common in KJV.db (1225 occurrences) and avoids "carcase," which in KJV usage reads as undignified for a disciple's burial of John the Baptist.
- NEW: And when his disciples heard of it, they came and took up his body, and laid it in a tomb.

## corrupters → destroyers — Isaiah 1:4
*(from batch_0022_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "spoilers" shifts the sense toward military plunderers rather than moral corrupters. "corrupters" itself is being flagged elsewhere in this batch (Jeremiah 6:28) alongside "corruptly," so a replacement built on "corrupt-" risks curing one rare word with another; "destroyers" (28 occurrences in KJV.db) captures the same Hebrew root sense (mashchith, "one who ruins") and is used for the same word at Jeremiah 6:28 (see below), keeping the two parallel renderings consistent.
- NEW: Ah sinful nation, a people laden with iniquity, a seed of evildoers, children that are destroyers: they have forsaken the Lord, they have provoked the Holy One of Israel unto anger, they are gone away backward.

## corrupters → destroyers — Jeremiah 6:28
*(from batch_0022_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "spoilers" is not attested by any witness for this verse. Geneva1599 reads "they all are destroyers" directly; "destroyers" is attested in KJV.db (28 occurrences).
- NEW: They are all grievous revolters, walking with slanders: they are brass and iron; they are all destroyers.

## corruptly → transgress — II Chronicles 27:2
*(from batch_0022_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "did yet utterly" is ungrammatical (utterly is an adverb lacking a verb to modify). DRC reads "the people still transgressed" and BBE "the people still went on in their evil ways"; "transgress" is common in KJV.db (98 occurrences) and slots into the existing "did yet ___" construction.
- NEW: And he did that which was right in the sight of the Lord, according to all that his father Uzziah did: howbeit he entered not into the temple of the Lord. And the people did yet transgress.

## corruptly → perversely — Nehemiah 1:7
*(from batch_0022_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "very utterly" is ungrammatical (double adverb with no complement). Darby renders this verse "acted very perversely against thee"; "perversely" is attested in KJV.db (21 occurrences) and fits the existing "dealt very ___" construction exactly.
- NEW: We have dealt very perversely against thee, and have not kept the commandments, nor the statutes, nor the judgments, which thou commandedst thy servant Moses.

## costliness → riches — Revelation 18:19
*(from batch_0022_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "riches" is common in KJV.db (686 occurrences), genuinely different from "costliness," and generally consonant with RWebster's "by her wealth" and Webster's "by reason of her opulence" for this verse.
- NEW: And they cast dust on their heads, and cried, weeping and wailing, saying, Alas, alas, that great city, wherein were made rich all that had ships in the sea by reason of her riches! for in one hour is she made desolate.

## cotes → folds — II Chronicles 32:28
*(from batch_0022_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "herds for flocks" is nonsensical (herds does not pair with "for flocks"; YLT's "herds" was for a different slot, "for stalls"). DRC reads "folds for cattle" and ASV "flocks in folds"; "folds" is attested in KJV.db (35 occurrences) and gives sound sense: enclosures for flocks.
- NEW: Storehouses also for the increase of corn, and wine, and oil; and stalls for all manner of beasts, and folds for flocks.

## cottage → booth — Isaiah 1:8
*(from batch_0022_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "tabernacle" carries the heavily loaded sense of the sacred Tent of Meeting elsewhere in KJV, ill-suited to a garden hut. Five witnesses (Webster, RWebster, YLT, Darby, ASV, ACV) all render this word "booth"; "booth" is attested in KJV.db (14 occurrences, e.g. the Feast of Booths context) and is a genuinely different word.
- NEW: And the daughter of Zion is left as a booth in a vineyard, as a lodge in a garden of cucumbers, as a besieged city.

## cottage → lodge — Isaiah 24:20
*(from batch_0022_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Matches YLT exactly ("moved as a lodge"); "lodge" is common in KJV.db (189 occurrences) and genuinely different from "cottage."
- NEW: The earth shall reel to and fro like a drunkard, and shall be removed like a lodge; and the transgression thereof shall be heavy upon it; and it shall fall, and not rise again.

## cottages → tents — Zephaniah 2:6
*(from batch_0022_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: No witness supplies a direct alternative (Darby uniquely reads "cave-dwellings"; most keep "cottages"), but "tents" is a common, plausible dwelling-word for shepherds (very frequent in KJV.db) and is genuinely different from "cottages."
- NEW: And the sea coast shall be dwellings and tents for shepherds, and folds for flocks.

## couchingplace → sheepcote — Ezekiel 25:5
*(from batch_0022_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "a lie for flocks" is nonsensical (reads as falsehood, not a resting-place). Geneva1599 reads "a dwelling place for camels...and the Ammonites a sheepecote"; "sheepcote" is attested in KJV.db (14 occurrences, e.g. I Samuel 24:3) and gives a concrete, sound sense.
- NEW: And I will make Rabbah a stable for camels, and the Ammonites a sheepcote for flocks: and ye shall know that I am the Lord.

## coulter → hook — I Samuel 13:20
*(from batch_0022_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "plowshares" duplicates the tool already named as "share" earlier in the same verse (share and plowshare being the same implement). Geneva1599's tool-list for this verse includes "weeding hooke"; "hook" is attested in KJV.db (35 occurrences) and names a genuinely distinct farm implement.
- NEW: But all the Israelites went down to the Philistines, to sharpen every man his share, and his hook, and his axe, and his mattock.

## coulters → hooks — I Samuel 13:21
*(from batch_0022_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: For consistency with the parallel verse above (coulter → hook), and because "hooks" (126 occurrences in KJV.db) is a well-attested, genuinely distinct farm-implement word not otherwise used in this verse's list (mattocks, forks, axes, goads).
- NEW: Yet they had a file for the mattocks, and for the hooks, and for the forks, and for the axes, and to sharpen the goads.

## countervail → recompense — Esther 7:4
*(from batch_0023_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "could not laid the king's damage" is an ungrammatical verb form. Geneva1599 reads this exact verse "the aduersarie could not recompense the Kings losse"; "recompense" is common in KJV.db (182 occurrences).
- NEW: For we are sold, I and my people, to be destroyed, to be slain, and to perish. But if we had been sold for bondmen and bondwomen, I had held my tongue, although the enemy could not recompense the king's damage.

*(from batch_0023_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "mine own kind" loses the specific sense of fellow-countrymen. Geneva1599 and DRC both read "of mine own nation" / "from my own nation" for this verse; "nation" is extremely common in KJV.db (1015 occurrences).
- NEW: In journeyings often, in perils of waters, in perils of robbers, in perils by mine own nation, in perils by the heathen, in perils in the city, in perils in the wilderness, in perils in the sea, in perils among false brethren;

- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same word as II Corinthians 11:26 above; for consistency and the same reasoning, "nation" (1015 occurrences in KJV.db) replaces "countrymen," which CUR wrongly rendered as the vague "kind."

*(from batch_0023_proposals.md)*

## courteously → kindly — Acts 28:7

*(from batch_0023_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "kinsmen" is the wrong gender and number for Elisabeth. YLT and Darby both render this verse "Elisabeth, thy kinswoman"; "kinswoman" is attested in KJV.db (21 occurrences) and correctly singular and feminine.
- NEW: And, behold, thy kinswoman Elisabeth, she hath also conceived a son in her old age: and this is the sixth month with her, who was called barren.

## cousins → kindred — Luke 1:58
*(from batch_0023_proposals.md)*

- rationale: Matches YLT exactly ("her kindred"); "kindred" is common in KJV.db (196 occurrences) and genuinely different from "cousins."

*(from batch_0023_proposals.md)*

- rationale: "trucebreakers" is directly attested in KJV.db itself at II Timothy 3:3 ("without natural affection, trucebreakers..."), a parallel vice-list; it is also Tyndale's own word for a related term in this verse's list. Genuinely different from "covenantbreakers" and confirmed common enough in KJV usage (7 occurrences, all from the II Timothy 3:3 verse duplicated across joins).
- NEW: Without understanding, trucebreakers, without natural affection, implacable, unmerciful:

## coveredst → deckedst — Psalms 104:6
*(from batch_0023_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "cover" is a trivial re-inflection of the same flagged word (coveredst → cover), which the owner ruling forbids. "deckedst" is attested in KJV.db (Ezekiel 16:16, "deckedst thy high places with divers colours") in the identical "deckedst [object] with [garment-like thing]" construction, and is a genuinely different verb (to deck/adorn) conveying the same sense of being wrapped/arrayed.
- NEW: Thou deckedst it with the deep as with a garment: the waters stood above the mountains.

## coveredst → deckedst — Ezekiel 16:18
*(from batch_0023_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same problem as Psalms 104:6 — CUR "covered" is a trivial re-inflection of "coveredst." "deckedst" is attested nearby in this very chapter (Ezekiel 16:16, "deckedst thy high places with divers colours"), making it a natural, genuinely different, well-attested substitute in the identical construction.
- NEW: And tookest thy broidered garments, and deckedst them: and thou hast set mine oil and mine incense before them.

## crackling → noise — Ecclesiastes 7:6
*(from batch_0023_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Matches YLT exactly ("noise of thorns under the pot"); "noise" is common and genuinely different from "crackling."
- NEW: For as the noise of thorns under a pot, so is the laughter of the fool: this also is vanity.

## cracknels → cakes — I Kings 14:3
*(from batch_0023_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "crumbs" (from YLT alone) undersells the gift as scraps. Four witnesses (RWebster, Darby, ASV, ACV) render this word "cakes," a majority consensus; "cakes" is common in KJV.db (175 occurrences).
- NEW: And take with thee ten loaves, and cakes, and a cruse of honey, and go to him: he shall tell thee what shall become of the child.

## craftsman → artificer — Deuteronomy 27:15
*(from batch_0023_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "craftsmen" is a trivial re-inflection of the flagged word (craftsman → craftsmen, singular to plural of the same word), which the owner ruling forbids. DRC, Webster, and YLT all render this word "artificer(s)"; "artificer" is attested in KJV.db (14 occurrences).
- NEW: Cursed be the man that maketh any graven or molten image, an abomination unto the Lord, the work of the hands of the artificer, and putteth it in a secret place. And all the people shall answer and say, Amen.

## craftsman → artificers — Revelation 18:22
*(from batch_0023_proposals.md)*

Webster, YLT, and Darby all render this word "artificer" (YLT/Darby: "artisan"/"artificer"); "artificers" is attested in KJV.db (14 occurrences) and fits the plural sense "no craftsman...shall be found" better rendered generally, matching Webster's singular "no artificer."
- NEW: And the voice of harpers, and musicians, and of pipers, and trumpeters, shall be heard no more at all in thee; and no artificer, of whatsoever craft he be, shall be found any more in thee; and the sound of a millstone shall be heard no more at all in thee;

- rationale: CUR "teeth" is plural where the verse describes one rock; YLT renders this verse "he lodgeth on the tooth of a rock," matching the singular "the crag of the rock." "tooth" is attested in KJV.db (84 occurrences).

*(from batch_0023_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Matches Geneva1599 and DRC exactly ("a great destruction from the hills"); "destruction" is extremely common in KJV.db (658 occurrences).

## craved → asked — Mark 15:43
*(from batch_0023_proposals.md)*

- rationale: Matches Geneva1599 and YLT exactly ("asked the body of Jesus"); "asked" is very common in KJV.db (833 occurrences).

## craveth → desireth — Proverbs 16:26
*(from batch_0023_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "desireth" is genuinely different from "craveth," attested in KJV.db (119 occurrences), and fits the sense of the verse (the mouth's need driving the laborer on).

- rationale: CUR "bosom" is unclear (bosom of what?). Darby, ASV, and ACV all render this word "bay"; "bay" is attested in KJV.db (42 occurrences).
- NEW: And when it was day, they knew not the land: but they discovered a certain bay with a shore, into the which they were minded, if it were possible, to thrust in the ship.

*(from batch_0023_proposals.md)*

- rationale: CUR is broken, literally duplicating "unawares" ("certain men unawares in unawares"). Darby renders this verse "have got in unnoticed"; "gotten in" is attested in KJV.db (21 occurrences as this exact bigram; "gotten" alone 175 occurrences) and retains the original "unawares" already present in the verse.

## crime → wickedness — Job 31:11
*(from batch_0023_proposals.md)*

Darby's "infamy" is unattested in KJV.db (14 occurrences, borderline) while YLT's "a wicked thing" points to the common root; "wickedness" is extremely common in KJV.db (889 occurrences) and avoids duplicating "iniquity," which already appears later in the same verse.

The KJV itself uses "bowed together" for the identical bent-back condition at Luke 13:11 ("a woman which... was bowed together, and could in no wise lift up herself"); "bowed together" is attested in KJV.db and gives a genuinely different, period-authentic construction.

## cuckow → sea-mew — Leviticus 11:16

*(from batch_0026_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE

- NEW: Being blasphemed, we intreat: we are made as the filth of the world, and are the offscouring of all things unto this day.

## defaming → slander — Jeremiah 20:10
*(from batch_0026_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "slander" is genuinely different from "defaming," attested in KJV.db (21 occurrences), and fits the sense of malicious talk described in this verse.
- NEW: For I heard the slander of many, fear on every side. Report, say they, and we will report it. All my familiars watched for my halting, saying, Peradventure he will be enticed, and we shall prevail against him, and we shall take our revenge on him.

## defeat → make void — II Samuel 15:34
*(from batch_0026_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "break the counsel" is an unusual collocation unsupported by any witness. YLT renders this exact verse "thou hast made void for me the counsel of Ahithophel"; "made void" is attested in KJV.db (21 occurrences) and is a genuine KJV idiom for nullifying a plan.
- NEW: But if thou return to the city, and say unto Absalom, I will be thy servant, O king; as I have been thy father's servant hitherto, so will I now also be thy servant: then mayest thou for me make void the counsel of Ahithophel.

## defeat → made void — II Samuel 17:14
*(from batch_0026_proposals.md)*

- NEW: And Absalom and all the men of Israel said, The counsel of Hushai the Archite is better than the counsel of Ahithophel. For the Lord had appointed to make void the good counsel of Ahithophel, to the intent that the Lord might bring evil upon Absalom.

## defiledst → polluted — Genesis 49:4
*(from batch_0026_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "began thou it" bears no relation to the sense of defilement. YLT renders this verse "thou hast polluted"; "polluted" is attested in KJV.db (280 occurrences) and is a genuinely different, period-authentic verb for the same act.
- NEW: Unstable as water, thou shalt not excel; because thou wentest up to thy father's bed; then polluted thou it: he went up to my couch.

## degenerate → rotten — Jeremiah 2:21
*(from batch_0026_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR is broken, literally duplicating the word ("the plant plant"). BSB captures the sense of this verse as "a rotten, wild vine"; "rotten" is attested in KJV.db (35 occurrences) and is a genuinely different adjective conveying the same sense of a spoiled vine.
- NEW: Yet I had planted thee a noble vine, wholly a right seed: how then art thou turned into the rotten plant of a strange vine unto me?

*(from batch_0027_proposals.md)*

- rationale: CUR's "lived lively" is bad grammar and loses the sense of self-indulgent luxury. Geneva's "liued in pleasure" supplies both a genuine replacement and the correct KJV-style construction ("lived in pleasure" is attested once verbatim in KJV.db, and "pleasure" itself is common).
- NEW: How much she hath glorified herself, and lived in pleasure, so much torment and sorrow give her: for she saith in her heart, I sit a queen, and am no widow, and shall see no sorrow.

- rationale: CUR's "rightly before" subtly changes the sense (correctness rather than direction), the same fault fixed for Numbers 19:4 in the prior batch. YLT/DRC's "over against" is genuine and extremely well attested (93 occurrences) in KJV.db.
