*(from batch_0066_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "sinners" is genuinely different from "offenders," and matches YLT exactly.
- NEW: Otherwise it shall come to pass, when my lord the king shall sleep with his fathers, that I and my son Solomon shall be counted sinners.

## offscouring → waste — Lamentations 3:45
*(from batch_0066_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "waste" is genuinely different from "offscouring," and matches UKJV exactly.
- NEW: Thou hast made us as the waste and refuse in the midst of the people.

## offscouring → refuse — I Corinthians 4:13
*(from batch_0067_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "waste" loses the force of the image. DRC renders "the refuse of this world"; "refuse" is well attested in the KJV corpus (67 distinct verses, e.g. Amos 8:6 "the refuse of the wheat") and preserves the sense of cast-off filth.
- NEW: Being defamed, we intreat: we are made as the filth of the world, and are the refuse of all things unto this day.

## oftener → oftentimes — Acts 24:26
*(from batch_0067_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "the often" is ungrammatical (drops the comparative sense). DRC reads "oftentimes sending for him"; "oftentimes" is genuinely attested in the KJV corpus (6 distinct verses, e.g. Mark, Ecclesiastes) and carries the same repeated-summons meaning without needing "the."
- NEW: He hoped also that money should have been given him of Paul, that he might loose him: wherefore he sent for him oftentimes, and communed with him.

## oldness → old letter — Romans 7:6
*(from batch_0067_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR is identical to OLD — the rare word was never actually replaced. Tyndale rephrases the abstract noun away entirely ("the olde conversacion of the letter"); following that model, dropping the "-ness" abstraction in favor of the plain adjective avoids the flagged word while preserving Paul's antithesis with "newness of spirit."
- NEW: But now we are delivered from the law, that being dead wherein we were held; that we should serve in newness of spirit, and not in the old letter.

## oliveyard → olive trees — Exodus 23:11
*(from batch_0067_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "thy olive" is missing its noun. Geneva1599 reads "thine oliue trees"; "olive trees" is well attested in the KJV corpus (7 distinct verses, e.g. Judges, Psalm 128:3 "olive plants").
- NEW: But the seventh year thou shalt let it rest and lie still; that the poor of thy people may eat: and what they leave the beasts of the field shall eat. In like manner thou shalt deal with thy vineyard, and with thy olive trees.

## omitted → left undone — Matthew 23:23
*(from batch_0067_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "have suffered" inverts the meaning (suffered = allowed, the opposite of omitted/neglected). Wycliffe/DRC/Tyndale all render "left [undone]," and the KJV's own text in this very verse already uses "leave the other undone" — "left undone" matches that internal construction exactly.
- NEW: Woe unto you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! for ye pay tithe of mint and anise and cummin, and have left undone the weightier matters of the law, judgment, mercy, and faith: these ought ye to have done, and not to leave the other undone.

## omnipotent → almighty — Revelation 19:6
*(from batch_0067_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "almighty" is a genuinely different word from "omnipotent," construction is unchanged ("the Lord God almighty reigneth"), and DRC/ASV/YLT all render the underlying word as "the Almighty" — sound choice already in place.
- NEW: And I heard as it were the voice of a great multitude, and as the voice of many waters, and as the voice of mighty thunderings, saying, Alleluia: for the Lord God almighty reigneth.

## onward → forward — Exodus 40:36
*(from batch_0067_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "forward" is a genuinely different, common word, matches Geneva1599's own rendering exactly ("went forward"), and requires no grammar changes.
- NEW: And when the cloud was taken up from over the tabernacle, the children of Israel went forward in all their journeys:

## oppose → resist — II Timothy 2:25
*(from batch_0067_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR duplicates "themselves themselves," a broken non-word repetition. DRC reads "them that resist the truth"; "resist" is well attested in the KJV corpus (e.g. James 4:7, Romans 13:2) and needs no reflexive pronoun.
- NEW: In meekness instructing those that resist; if God peradventure will give them repentance to the acknowledging of the truth;

## opposed → resisted — Acts 18:6
*(from batch_0067_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "resisted themselves" is ungrammatical — one cannot "resist" oneself in this sense. Geneva1599 reads "when they resisted, and blasphemed" with no reflexive pronoun; that construction is adopted directly.
- NEW: And when they resisted, and blasphemed, he shook his raiment, and said unto them, Your blood be upon your own heads; I am clean: from henceforth I will go unto the Gentiles.

## opposest → persecutest — Job 30:21
*(from batch_0067_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "thou hatest thyself against me" is both ungrammatical and semantically wrong (a reflexive "hatest thyself" makes no sense in this adversarial context). ASV independently reads "thou persecutest me" — genuinely different from "opposest," and "persecutest" is one of the most recognizable second-person forms in the KJV idiom (echoing Acts 9:4, "Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me?").
- NEW: Thou art become cruel to me: with thy strong hand thou persecutest me.

## opposeth → resisteth — II Thessalonians 2:4
*(from batch_0067_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "resisteth" is a genuinely different, well-attested KJV word (e.g. Romans 13:2 "resisteth the power") and the sentence is grammatically sound as given.
- NEW: Who resisteth and exalteth himself above all that is called God, or that is worshipped; so that he as God sitteth in the temple of God, shewing himself that he is God.

## oppositions → gainsayings — I Timothy 6:20
*(from batch_0067_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "gainsayings" is genuinely different from "oppositions" and is attested in the KJV corpus (e.g. Jude 1:11 "gainsaying of Core," Romans 10:21 "gainsaying people").
- NEW: O Timothy, keep that which is committed to thy trust, avoiding profane and vain babblings, and gainsayings of science falsely so called:

*(from batch_0067_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR keeps the right word but the wrong article ("an speech" before a consonant sound). "speech" is a common, well-attested KJV noun (e.g. Exodus 4:10); only the article needs correcting.

## orator → enchanter — Isaiah 3:3
*(from batch_0067_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "eloquent enchantment" pairs an adjective with the wrong noun class and is nonsensical. Darby reads "one versed in enchantments," ASV/ACV "the skilful enchanter" — "enchanter" is attested in the KJV corpus (e.g. Daniel 2:2) and fits "eloquent" naturally.

## orator → advocate — Acts 24:1
*(from batch_0067_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "a certain prayer named Tertullus" mistakes the noun for the wrong word class entirely — a person cannot be named "prayer." No witness offers an alternate word for this legal speaker, but "advocate" is attested in the KJV corpus (1 John 2:1, "we have an advocate with the Father") and fits Tertullus's role as Paul's legal accuser before the governor.
- NEW: And after five days Ananias the high priest descended with the elders, and with a certain advocate named Tertullus, who informed the governor against Paul.

## orchard → paradise — Song of Solomon 4:13
*(from batch_0067_proposals.md)*

## orchards → (rephrased) — Ecclesiastes 2:5
*(from batch_0067_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "forests" names the wrong referent (a forest is not a cultivated orchard). No witness offers a period-authentic single-word substitute distinct from "orchard/park," so the clause is rephrased to fold the redundant noun into the following relative clause, per the "not one-for-one" grammar ruling — the planted-fruit-trees clause already carries the full sense.
- NEW: I made me gardens, and I planted trees in them of all kind of fruits:

## orderings → courses — I Chronicles 24:19
*(from batch_0067_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "officers" wrongly names people rather than the arrangement of their service. DRC reads "their courses according to their ministries," and "courses" is the KJV's own term used repeatedly throughout this very chapter (1 Chronicles 24) for the priestly divisions — the strongest possible internal match.
- NEW: These were the courses of them in their service to come into the house of the Lord, according to their manner, under Aaron their father, as the Lord God of Israel had commanded him.

## orderly → circumspectly — Acts 21:24
*(from batch_0067_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "circumspectly" is a genuinely different word and is directly attested in the KJV corpus (Ephesians 5:15, "walk circumspectly") — an excellent internal match.
- NEW: Them take, and purify thyself with them, and be at charges with them, that they may shave their heads: and all may know that those things, whereof they were informed concerning thee, are nothing; but that thou thyself also walkest circumspectly, and keepest the law.

## ordinary → appointed food — Ezekiel 16:27
*(from batch_0067_proposals.md)*

- rationale: CUR "thine statute food" reads as an awkward noun-pile. Darby reads "diminished thine appointed portion"; "appointed" is very common in the KJV corpus (131 distinct verses) and combines naturally with "food," which the verse already supplies.

*(from batch_0067_proposals.md)*

## ossifrage → vulture — Leviticus 11:13
*(from batch_0067_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "vulture" is genuinely attested pre-1611: Geneva1599 uses it 3 times elsewhere (e.g. "the glead nor the kite, nor the vulture") and Wycliffe uses it 6 times (e.g. "the iye of a vultur"). A real, different, period-documented bird name.
- NEW: And these are they which ye shall have in abomination among the fowls; they shall not be eaten, they are an abomination: the eagle, and the vulture, and the ospray,

## ossifrage → vulture — Deuteronomy 14:12
*(from batch_0067_proposals.md)*

## outlandish → strange — Nehemiah 13:26
*(from batch_0068_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "strange" is genuinely different from "outlandish," matches Geneva1599 exactly ("strange women"), and is common, well-attested KJV idiom (cf. Proverbs' "strange woman").
- NEW: Did not Solomon king of Israel sin by these things? yet among many nations was there no king like him, who was beloved of his God, and God made him king over all Israel: nevertheless even him did strange women cause to sin.

## outlived → whose days were prolonged — Judges 2:7
*(from batch_0068_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "elders that required Joshua" is nonsensical. Darby/YLT independently render "whose days were prolonged after Joshua" — a genuinely different, well-attested construction ("prolong...days" is common KJV idiom, e.g. Deuteronomy 4:26, Isaiah 53:10).
- NEW: And the people served the Lord all the days of Joshua, and all the days of the elders whose days were prolonged after Joshua, who had seen all the great works of the Lord, that he did for Israel.

## outrageous → raging — Proverbs 27:4
*(from batch_0068_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "anger is waters" is nonsensical. Geneva1599 reads "wrath is raging"; "raging" is directly attested elsewhere in the same book (Proverbs 20:1, "strong drink is raging"), the strongest possible internal match.
- NEW: Wrath is cruel, and anger is raging; but who is able to stand before envy?

## outrun → run before — John 20:4
*(from batch_0068_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "did ran Peter" is ungrammatical and drops the sense of racing ahead. Wycliffe reads "ran bifor Petre" ("ran before Peter"); "before" is common throughout the KJV corpus, giving a genuinely different and grammatical construction.
- NEW: So they ran both together: and the other disciple did run before Peter, and came first to the sepulchre.

## outwardly → on the outside — Matthew 23:28
*(from batch_0068_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "openly" nearly inverts the sense — Jesus contrasts outward show with inward hypocrisy, not secrecy with disclosure. This same discourse already uses "outside" for the identical contrast three verses earlier (Matthew 23:25, "make clean the outside of the cup"), giving the strongest possible internal precedent for a genuinely different phrase.
- NEW: Even so ye also appear righteous unto men on the outside, but within ye are full of hypocrisy and iniquity.

## outwardly → outward — Romans 2:28
*(from batch_0068_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "openly" is a weaker, slightly different sense (visible vs. public) and duplicates the risk of the Matthew 23:28 entry. The same verse already uses "outward" (unflagged) in its second clause ("outward in the flesh"); repeating that word for the first clause gives a genuinely different part of speech than the flagged adverb and matches the verse's own internal parallelism.
- NEW: For he is not a Jew, which is one outward; neither is that circumcision, which is outward in the flesh:

## outwent → went before — Mark 6:33
*(from batch_0068_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "and before them" drops its verb, leaving the clause ungrammatical. YLT reads "went before them" — genuinely different from "outwent," grammatical, and a common KJV construction (e.g. Exodus 13:21, "the LORD went before them").
- NEW: And the people saw them departing, and many knew him, and ran afoot thither out of all cities, and went before them, and came together unto him.

## overcharge → burden — II Corinthians 2:5
*(from batch_0068_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "burden" is genuinely different and well attested (e.g. Galatians 6:2), matching DRC, RWebster, and YLT's independent renderings.
- NEW: But if any have caused grief, he hath not grieved me, but in part: that I may not burden you all.

## overcharged → laden — Luke 21:34
*(from batch_0068_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "laden" is genuinely different, matches Darby's independent rendering, and is well attested in the KJV corpus (e.g. Matthew 11:28, "heavy laden").
- NEW: And take heed to yourselves, lest at any time your hearts be laden with surfeiting, and drunkenness, and cares of this life, and so that day come upon you unawares.

## overdrive → beat — Genesis 33:13
*(from batch_0068_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "beat" is genuinely different and independently supported by YLT ("when they have beaten them one day"), giving cross-witness confirmation beyond the CUR selection alone.
- NEW: And he said unto him, My lord knoweth that the children are tender, and the flocks and herds with young are with me: and if men should beat them one day, all the flock will die.

## overlived → whose days were prolonged — Joshua 24:31
*(from batch_0068_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same reasoning as the Judges 2:7 "outlived" entry — CUR "elders that required Joshua" is nonsensical, and Darby/YLT's "whose days were prolonged after Joshua" is genuinely different and well attested.
- NEW: And Israel served the Lord all the days of Joshua, and all the days of the elders whose days were prolonged after Joshua, and which had known all the works of the Lord, that he had done for Israel.

## overmuch → greater — II Corinthians 2:7
*(from batch_0068_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "greater" is genuinely different and extremely common in the KJV corpus; the resulting sense ("swallowed up with greater sorrow") is sound.
- NEW: So that contrariwise ye ought rather to forgive him, and comfort him, lest perhaps such a one should be swallowed up with greater sorrow.

## overpass → exceed — Jeremiah 5:28
*(from batch_0068_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "pass" drops the "over-" sense entirely and reads as ambiguous ("pass the deeds," not "surpass" them), whereas UKJV's own rendering here is "pass over." RWebster's "exceed" is genuinely different, unambiguous, and attested elsewhere in the KJV (e.g. 2 Corinthians 3:9, "exceed in glory").
- NEW: They are waxen fat, they shine: yea, they exceed the deeds of the wicked: they judge not the cause, the cause of the fatherless, yet they prosper; and the right of the needy do they not judge.

## overpast → past — Psalms 57:1
*(from batch_0068_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "calamities be through" is nonsensical. RWebster reads "calamities are past by"; "past" is genuinely different from "overpast" and extremely common in the KJV.
- NEW: Be merciful unto me, O God, be merciful unto me: for my soul trusteth in thee: yea, in the shadow of thy wings will I make my refuge, until these calamities be past.

## overpast → past — Isaiah 26:20
*(from batch_0068_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same reasoning as Psalms 57:1 — CUR "indignation be through" is nonsensical, and RWebster/Darby independently render "shall be past"/"be past."
- NEW: Come, my people, enter thou into thy chambers, and shut thy doors about thee: hide thyself as it were for a little moment, until the indignation be past.

## overplus → remainder — Leviticus 25:27
*(from batch_0068_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "the numbers" misnames the referent (a surplus sum, not a count). "remainder" is genuinely different, well attested in the KJV (e.g. Leviticus 14, Exodus 29), and matches the general sense of RWebster's "balance" and ACV's "excess" without introducing a new rare word.
- NEW: Then let him count the years of the sale thereof, and restore the remainder unto the man to whom he sold it; that he may return unto his possession.

## overran → outran — II Samuel 18:23
*(from batch_0068_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "and through Cushi" drops its verb entirely, leaving the clause ungrammatical. Webster, RWebster, UKJV, and Darby independently render "outran" — a genuinely different word (distinct from the flagged "overran") that is well attested across these witnesses.
- NEW: But howsoever, said he, let me run. And he said unto him, Run. Then Ahimaaz ran by the way of the plain, and outran Cushi.

## overrunning → overwhelming — Nahum 1:8
*(from batch_0068_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "an passing flood" has a wrong article and weakens the sense. "overwhelming" is genuinely different, well attested in the KJV (e.g. Psalm 124:4, "then the waters had overwhelmed us"), and matches the flood imagery of BSB's "overwhelming flood."
- NEW: But with an overwhelming flood he will make an utter end of the place thereof, and darkness shall pursue his enemies.

## oversee → be over — I Chronicles 9:29
*(from batch_0068_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "appointed to above the vessels" is ungrammatical (above is not a verb). Geneva1599/ASV/Darby/YLT independently render "appointed over" — a genuinely different, common KJV construction ("set/appointed over" recurs throughout).
- NEW: Some of them also were appointed over the vessels, and all the instruments of the sanctuary, and the fine flour, and the wine, and the oil, and the frankincense, and the spices.

## oversee → be over — II Chronicles 2:2
*(from batch_0068_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same reasoning as I Chronicles 9:29 — CUR "to above them" is ungrammatical; Geneva1599 independently reads "to oversee/ouersee" but the parallel entry's fix ("appointed over") is adopted for consistency and grammar.
- NEW: And Solomon told out threescore and ten thousand men to bear burdens, and fourscore thousand to hew in the mountain, and three thousand and six hundred to be over them.

## overspread → replenished — Genesis 9:19
*(from batch_0068_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "covered" changes the sense (covering vs. populating). "replenished" is genuinely different, iconic KJV vocabulary for populating the earth (Genesis 1:28, "replenish the earth"; Genesis 9:1), and fits this verse's population theme precisely.
- NEW: These are the three sons of Noah: and of them was the whole earth replenished.

## overspreading → wing — Daniel 9:27
*(from batch_0068_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "wing" is genuinely different and independently attested by YLT, ASV, and ACV ("the wing of abominations"), giving strong cross-witness confirmation.
- NEW: And he shall confirm the covenant with many for one week: and in the midst of the week he shall cause the sacrifice and the oblation to cease, and for the wing of abominations he shall make it desolate, even until the consummation, and that determined shall be poured upon the desolate.

## overtaken → taken — Galatians 6:1
*(from batch_0068_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "be overtake in a fault" is ungrammatical (wrong verb form). Geneva1599 and Darby independently render "be...taken in a fault/some fault" — a genuinely different, simpler, well-attested word.
- NEW: Brethren, if a man be taken in a fault, ye which are spiritual, restore such an one in the spirit of meekness; considering thyself, lest thou also be tempted.

## overwhelm → fall upon — Job 6:27
*(from batch_0068_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "ye fall the fatherless" is ungrammatical. Geneva1599 reads "make your wrath to fall vpon the fatherlesse," DRC "rush in upon the fatherless" — adding the preposition "upon" to "fall" gives a genuinely different, grammatical, and period-attested construction.
- NEW: Yea, ye fall upon the fatherless, and ye dig a pit for your friend.

## oweth → is in thy debt — Philemon 1:18
*(from batch_0068_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "or ought thee ought" is broken (reuses "ought" as both verb and pronoun, nonstandard). DRC reads "or is in thy debt"; this is genuinely different from "oweth" and avoids merely re-inflecting the same root.
- NEW: If he hath wronged thee, or is in thy debt, put that on mine account;

## paces → steps — II Samuel 6:13
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "steps" is genuinely different from "paces" and matches YLT and BBE's independent renderings ("stepped six steps"/"gone six steps").
- NEW: And it was so, that when they that bare the ark of the Lord had gone six steps, he sacrificed oxen and fatlings.

## pacified → appeased — Esther 7:10
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "purged" is the wrong concept entirely (purged ≠ calmed). Darby independently reads "the king's wrath was appeased"; "appeased" is genuinely different and attested in the KJV (Genesis 32:20, Acts 19:35).
- NEW: So they hanged Haman on the gallows that he had prepared for Mordecai. Then was the king's wrath appeased.

## pacified → appeased — Ezekiel 16:63
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same reasoning as Esther 7:10 — "purged toward thee" is ungrammatical and wrong in sense; "appeased" is genuinely different and well attested.
- NEW: That thou mayest remember, and be confounded, and never open thy mouth any more because of thy shame, when I am appeased toward thee for all that thou hast done, saith the Lord God.

## pacifieth → quencheth — Proverbs 21:14
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "remaineth" nearly inverts the sense. Wycliffe/DRC read "quencheth"; "quench" is genuinely different and well attested in the KJV (Matthew 12:20, Ephesians 6:16, Song of Solomon 8:7).
- NEW: A gift in secret quencheth anger: and a reward in the bosom strong wrath.

## pacifieth → appeaseth — Ecclesiastes 10:4
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "remaineth great offences" is nonsensical. "appeaseth" is genuinely different, matches the calming sense of YLT's "quieteth"/ASV's "allayeth," and keeps consistency with the other "pacify"-family entries in this batch.
- NEW: If the spirit of the ruler rise up against thee, leave not thy place; for yielding appeaseth great offences.

## pacify → appease — Proverbs 16:14
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "will atonement it" is ungrammatical (a noun used as a verb). "appease" is genuinely different, matches the sense of Darby's own rendering, and stays consistent with the batch's other pacify-family fixes.
- NEW: The wrath of a king is as messengers of death: but a wise man will appease it.

## paddle → nail — Deuteronomy 23:13
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "nail" is genuinely different from "paddle" and is independently attested by YLT ("a nail thou hast on thy staff").
- NEW: And thou shalt have a nail upon thy weapon; and it shall be, when thou wilt ease thyself abroad, thou shalt dig therewith, and shalt turn back and cover that which cometh from thee:

## painful → grievous — Psalms 73:16
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "too labour for me" is ungrammatical (a noun where an adjective is required). Darby's "a grievous task" points to "grievous," genuinely different and extremely common throughout the KJV.
- NEW: When I thought to know this, it was too grievous for me;

## painfulness → travail — II Corinthians 11:27
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "travail" is genuinely different and independently attested by ASV ("in labor and travail").
- NEW: In weariness and travail, in watchings often, in hunger and thirst, in fastings often, in cold and nakedness.

## painted → stained — II Kings 9:30
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "anointed her face" wrongly imports religious/ceremonial anointing where the text describes cosmetic makeup — a real change of meaning. "stained" is genuinely different, well attested in the KJV (Isaiah 63:3, "I will stain all my raiment"; Job 3:5), and fits the application of red pigment to skin.
- NEW: And when Jehu was come to Jezreel, Jezebel heard of it; and she stained her face, and tired her head, and looked out at a window.

## painted → stained — Jeremiah 22:14
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same reasoning as II Kings 9:30 — CUR "anointed with vermilion" misapplies ceremonial anointing to a house wall. "stained" is genuinely different and fits a red pigment being applied to woodwork.
- NEW: That saith, I will build me a wide house and large chambers, and cutteth him out windows; and it is cieled with cedar, and stained with vermilion.

## paintedst → stainedst — Ezekiel 23:40
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "thyself thy eyes" is a broken non-word duplicate. Using "stainedst," the second-person form of the "stained" swap adopted at II Kings 9:30 and Jeremiah 22:14, keeps this entry consistent with its sibling verses and is genuinely different from "paintedst."
- NEW: And furthermore, that ye have sent for men to come from far, unto whom a messenger was sent; and, lo, they came: for whom thou didst wash thyself, stainedst thine eyes, and deckedst thyself with ornaments,

## painting → colour — Jeremiah 4:30
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "with colouring" is an awkward, weakly attested form. "colour" as a plain noun is well attested in the KJV (repeatedly in Leviticus 13, e.g. "if the colour be white"), and substitutes cleanly into the same clause structure.
- NEW: And when thou art spoiled, what wilt thou do? Though thou clothest thyself with crimson, though thou deckest thee with ornaments of gold, though thou rentest thy face with colour, in vain shalt thou make thyself fair; thy lovers will despise thee, they will seek thy life.

## pale → green — Revelation 6:8
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Unlike the Isaiah 29:22 entry, here CUR's "green" has direct period-witness support: Tyndale independently reads "a grene horsse." The underlying Greek chloros (source of "chlorophyll") denotes a pale yellow-green, so "green" is arguably the more source-faithful rendering as well as a genuinely different, attested word.
- NEW: And I looked, and behold a green horse: and his name that sat on him was Death, and Hell followed with him. And power was given unto them over the fourth part of the earth, to kill with sword, and with hunger, and with death, and with the beasts of the earth.

## paleness → whiteness — Jeremiah 30:6
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "whiteness" is genuinely different from "paleness" and expresses a plausible period idiom for a fear-blanched face; the underlying concept (face changing color with fear) is confirmed by BBE ("turned green") and Wycliffe/DRC ("turned yellow"), all pointing to color change rather than requiring the exact word "pale."
- NEW: Ask ye now, and see whether a man doth travail with child? wherefore do I see every man with his hands on his loins, as a woman in travail, and all faces are turned into whiteness?

## pant → gape — Amos 2:7
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "swallow after the dust" is nonsensical. Geneva1599 reads "gape ouer the head of the poore"; "gape" is genuinely different and attested in the KJV (Job 16:10, 29:23).
- NEW: That gape after the dust of the earth on the head of the poor, and turn aside the way of the meek: and a man and his father will go in unto the same maid, to profane my holy name:

## panted → failed — Isaiah 21:4
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "My heart caused" is broken and incomplete. Geneva1599/DRC independently read "My heart failed"; "failed" is genuinely different and a well-attested KJV idiom for the heart (Psalm 40:12, "my heart faileth me"; Genesis 42:28, "their heart failed them").
- NEW: My heart failed, fearfulness affrighted me: the night of my pleasure hath he turned into fear unto me.

## paper → meadows — Isaiah 19:7
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "the reed reeds" is a broken duplicate. ASV/ACV independently read "the meadows by the Nile"; "meadow" is genuinely different and attested in the KJV (Genesis 41:2, 41:18).
- NEW: The meadows by the brooks, by the mouth of the brooks, and every thing sown by the brooks, shall wither, be driven away, and be no more.

## paramours → concubines — Ezekiel 23:20
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "concubines" is genuinely different from "paramours" and is heavily attested throughout the KJV (Genesis, Judges, etc.).
- NEW: For she doted upon their concubines, whose flesh is as the flesh of asses, and whose issue is like the issue of horses.

## pare → trim — Deuteronomy 21:12
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "prepared her nails" is the wrong action (prepared ≠ cut/trim). UKJV/BSB independently read "trim her nails"; "trim" is genuinely different and attested in the KJV (Matthew 25:7, "trimmed their lamps").
- NEW: Then thou shalt bring her home to thine house; and she shall shave her head, and trim her nails;

## partakest → art made partaker — Romans 11:17
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "partake" merely drops the "-est" ending — a re-inflection, not a genuine replacement. Geneva1599, DRC, and Tyndale independently render this as "made partaker of the root" — a genuinely different construction (noun rather than verb) with strong period support.
- NEW: And if some of the branches be broken off, and thou, being a wild olive tree, wert graffed in among them, and with them art made partaker of the root and fatness of the olive tree;

## partial → had respect of persons — Malachi 2:9
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "have been bear in the law" is nonsensical. DRC/YLT/ASV/Darby/ACV all independently render this with the idiom "respect of persons" — one of the most recognizable KJV phrases (Leviticus 19:15, Deuteronomy 1:17, James 2:1) — a genuinely different and strongly attested construction.
- NEW: Therefore have I also made you contemptible and base before all the people, according as ye have not kept my ways, but have had respect of persons in the law.

## partial → respecters of persons — James 2:4
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "bear in yourselves" is nonsensical. Using the KJV's own idiom "respecter of persons" (Acts 10:34, "God is no respecter of persons") gives a genuinely different, strongly attested construction consistent with the companion Malachi 2:9 fix.
- NEW: Are ye not then respecters of persons among yourselves, and are become judges of evil thoughts?

## partiality → favour — I Timothy 5:21
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR left the flagged word unchanged. Darby independently reads "doing nothing by favour"; "favour" is genuinely different and one of the most common nouns in the KJV.
- NEW: I charge thee before God, and the Lord Jesus Christ, and the elect angels, that thou observe these things without preferring one before another, doing nothing by favour.

## partiality → variance — James 3:17
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR left the flagged word unchanged. ASV independently reads "without variance"; "variance" is genuinely different and attested in the KJV (Galatians 5:20, listed among the works of the flesh).
- NEW: But the wisdom that is from above is first pure, then peaceable, gentle, and easy to be intreated, full of mercy and good fruits, without variance, and without hypocrisy.

## particular → severally — I Corinthians 12:27
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "members in every" is ungrammatical. "severally" is genuinely different, matches ASV's rendering, and is the KJV's own word used earlier in this very chapter (1 Corinthians 12:11, "dividing to every man severally as he will").
- NEW: Now ye are the body of Christ, and members severally.

## particular → severally — Ephesians 5:33
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "in every so love his wife" is ungrammatical. "severally" (matching ASV's "severally love each one his own wife") is genuinely different and consistent with the 1 Corinthians 12:27 fix in this same batch.
- NEW: Nevertheless let every one of you severally so love his wife even as himself; and the wife see that she reverence her husband.

## particularly → one by one — Acts 21:19
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "declared daily" changes the meaning entirely (frequency vs. detail). YLT/Darby/ASV independently render "one by one," a genuinely different, well-attested KJV idiom (John 8:9; 1 Corinthians 14:31).
- NEW: And when he had saluted them, he declared one by one what things God had wrought among the Gentiles by his ministry.

## particularly → severally — Hebrews 9:5
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "speak daily" changes the meaning entirely. ASV independently reads "speak severally"; using "severally" also keeps this entry consistent with the batch's other particular/particularly fixes.
- NEW: And over it the cherubims of glory shadowing the mercyseat; of which we cannot now speak severally.

## partition → (rephrased) — I Kings 6:21
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "he made a through by the chains of gold" is ungrammatical (through is not a noun). ASV rephrases the clause entirely, "he drew chains of gold across before the oracle," avoiding the need for a single-word substitute per the "not one-for-one" grammar ruling.
- NEW: So Solomon overlaid the house within with pure gold: and he drew chains of gold across before the oracle; and he overlaid it with gold.

## partition → separation — Ephesians 2:14
*(from batch_0069_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "the middle wall of through" is nonsensical. "separation" is genuinely different from "partition," conveys the same sense of a dividing wall, and is attested in the KJV (Numbers 6, the Nazarite "separation").
- NEW: For he is our peace, who hath made both one, and hath broken down the middle wall of separation between us;

## passedst → wentest — Judges 12:1
*(from batch_0070_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "through thou over" is nonsensical (a preposition used as a verb). Geneva1599 independently reads "Wherefore wentest thou to fight"; "wentest" is genuinely different, grammatical, and a common KJV second-person form.
- NEW: And the men of Ephraim gathered themselves together, and went northward, and said unto Jephthah, Wherefore wentest thou over to fight against the children of Ammon, and didst not call us to go with thee? we will burn thine house upon thee with fire.

## passion → he had suffered — Acts 1:3
*(from batch_0070_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "after his suffer" is ungrammatical (a bare verb used as a noun). Geneva1599 and Darby independently read "after that he had suffered"/"after he had suffered" — a genuinely different verb-phrase construction with strong period support.
- NEW: To whom also he shewed himself alive after he had suffered by many infallible proofs, being seen of them forty days, and speaking of the things pertaining to the kingdom of God:

## passions → affections — Acts 14:15
*(from batch_0070_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "affections" is genuinely different from "passions" and is well attested in the KJV (Romans 1:26, Colossians 3:2, Galatians 5:24).
- NEW: And saying, Sirs, why do ye these things? We also are men of like affections with you, and preach unto you that ye should turn from these vanities unto the living God, which made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and all things that are therein:

## passions → affections — James 5:17
*(from batch_0070_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same reasoning as Acts 14:15 — "affections" is genuinely different and well attested.
- NEW: Elias was a man subject to like affections as we are, and he prayed earnestly that it might not rain: and it rained not on the earth by the space of three years and six months.

## pastor → shepherd — Jeremiah 17:16
*(from batch_0070_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "being a feed" is ungrammatical (a verb used as a noun). RWebster, Darby, ASV, and ACV all independently render "shepherd" — genuinely different and one of the most common nouns in the KJV.
- NEW: As for me, I have not hastened from being a shepherd to follow thee: neither have I desired the woeful day; thou knowest: that which came out of my lips was right before thee.

## pate → crown — Psalms 7:16
*(from batch_0070_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "his own head" duplicates the word already used earlier in the same verse ("return upon his own head"), collapsing an intended distinction. YLT/RWebster independently render "crown"; "crown" is genuinely different and preserves the two-part structure of the verse.
- NEW: His mischief shall return upon his own head, and his violent dealing shall come down upon his own crown.

## pathway → path — Proverbs 12:28
*(from batch_0070_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "path" is genuinely different in form from "pathway" (not merely re-inflected) and is independently attested in Wycliffe's rendering of this verse ("path of riytfulnesse").
- NEW: In the way of righteousness is life; and in the path thereof there is no death.

## patrimony → inheritance — Deuteronomy 18:8
*(from batch_0070_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "sale of his father" is nonsensical (a father cannot be sold). Wycliffe independently reads "eritage of fadir" ("heritage/inheritance of father"); "inheritance" is genuinely different and one of the most common nouns in the KJV, especially in land-allotment contexts.
- NEW: They shall have like portions to eat, beside that which cometh of the sale of his inheritance.

## paved → wrought — Exodus 24:10
*(from batch_0070_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "wrought" is genuinely different from "paved" and is an extremely common KJV word for craftsmanship; YLT and Darby also independently drop "paved" in favor of a generic "work" description.
- NEW: And they saw the God of Israel: and there was under his feet as it were a wrought work of a sapphire stone, and as it were the body of heaven in his clearness.

## paved → wrought — Song of Solomon 3:10
*(from batch_0070_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same reasoning as Exodus 24:10 — "wrought with love" is a genuinely different, sensible, and common KJV construction.
- NEW: He made the pillars thereof of silver, the bottom thereof of gold, the covering of it of purple, the midst thereof being wrought with love, for the daughters of Jerusalem.

## paw → claw — I Samuel 17:37
*(from batch_0070_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "hand of the lion" collapses the deliberate distinction the verse draws between animal "paw" and the human "hand of this Philistine" later in the same sentence. BSB independently renders "claws of the lion and the bear"; "claw" is genuinely different and preserves that contrast.
- NEW: David said moreover, The Lord that delivered me out of the claw of the lion, and out of the claw of the bear, he will deliver me out of the hand of this Philistine. And Saul said unto David, Go, and the Lord be with thee.

## paweth → diggeth — Job 39:21
*(from batch_0070_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "seeketh in the valley" changes the action entirely (searching vs. stamping/scraping the ground). Wycliffe/YLT independently read "diggith"/"dig"; "diggeth" is genuinely different and a common KJV verb.
- NEW: He diggeth in the valley, and rejoiceth in his strength: he goeth on to meet the armed men.

## paws → hands — Leviticus 11:27
*(from batch_0070_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: DRC independently renders this "that which walketh upon hands of all animals," confirming "hands" is a legitimate, source-consistent rendering (the underlying Hebrew does not distinguish paw from hand) and genuinely different from "paws."
- NEW: And whatsoever goeth upon his hands, among all manner of beasts that go on all four, those are unclean unto you: whoso toucheth their carcase shall be unclean until the even.

## payment → the debt to be paid — Matthew 18:25
*(from batch_0070_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "render to be made" is ungrammatical (a verb where a noun is required). Geneva1599 independently reads "the dette to be payed"; "debt...to be paid" is a genuinely different construction using two extremely common KJV words.
- NEW: But forasmuch as he had not to pay, his lord commanded him to be sold, and his wife, and children, and all that he had, and the debt to be paid.

## pedigrees → genealogies — Numbers 1:18
*(from batch_0070_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "declared their midwives" is nonsensical in a census context. RWebster independently reads "genealogies"; "genealogy/genealogies" is genuinely different and well attested in the KJV (1 Chronicles 5:1, Ezra 2:62, 1 Timothy 1:4).
- NEW: And they assembled all the congregation together on the first day of the second month, and they declared their genealogies after their families, by the house of their fathers, according to the number of the names, from twenty years old and upward, by their polls.

## peep → whisper — Isaiah 8:19
*(from batch_0070_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR left the flagged word unchanged. Geneva1599 independently reads "which whisper and murmure"; "whisper" is genuinely different and well attested in the KJV (Psalm 41:7, 2 Samuel 12:19).
- NEW: And when they shall say unto you, Seek unto them that have familiar spirits, and unto wizards that whisper, and that mutter: should not a people seek unto their God? for the living to the dead?

## peeped → whispered — Isaiah 10:14
*(from batch_0070_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "or whispering" breaks the past-tense parallel with "moved...opened." Using "whispered" (the past-tense form of the swap adopted at Isaiah 8:19 in this same batch) is genuinely different from "peeped" and restores correct tense agreement.
- NEW: And my hand hath found as a nest the riches of the people: and as one gathereth eggs that are left, have I gathered all the earth; and there was none that moved the wing, or opened the mouth, or whispered.

## penknife → knife — Jeremiah 36:23

YLT independently uses the grammatical appositive construction "found this man a pestilence"; dropping "fellow" and adopting YLT's exact construction gives a genuinely different part of speech (noun rather than adjective) and fixes the grammar.

## philosophers → wise men — Acts 17:18
*(from batch_0072_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "philosophers" is KJV-rare (single occurrence) and CUR left it untouched. No period witness offers a true synonym (Geneva/DRC/Wycliffe all keep "philosophers"/"filosofris"), so a periphrasis is needed; "wise men" is common in the KJV corpus (43 hits) and fits the sense of Epicurean/Stoic disputants without inventing new content.
- NEW: Then certain wise men of the Epicureans, and of the Stoicks, encountered him. And some said, What will this babbler say? other some, He seemeth to be a setter forth of strange gods: because he preached unto them Jesus, and the resurrection.

## philosophy → the wisdom of men — Colossians 2:8
*(from batch_0072_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "philosophy" is a KJV hapax and CUR left it unchanged. No period witness supplies an English synonym (Wycliffe/Geneva/DRC all keep "philosophie/philosophy"). "the wisdom of men" is an existing KJV phrase (1 Corinthians 2:5) carrying the same Pauline contrast of human wisdom vs. Christ, and BBE independently glosses this verse as "man's wisdom."
- NEW: Beware lest any man spoil you through the wisdom of men and vain deceit, after the tradition of men, after the rudiments of the world, and not after Christ.

*(from batch_0072_proposals.md)*

## piss → the water of their feet — II Kings 18:27
*(from batch_0072_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's bare "water" inverts the meaning of the taunt (drinking plain water is no degradation). "urine" (Webster/DRC/Darby) has zero occurrences in the KJV corpus and would itself become a new rare word. The underlying Hebrew idiom (mê raglêhem) is literally "water of their feet," a euphemism partly preserved by Wycliffe's "pisse of her feet"; rendering it "the water of their feet" keeps the euphemistic sense using only common KJV vocabulary.
- NEW: But Rab–shakeh said unto them, Hath my master sent me to thy master, and to thee, to speak these words? hath he not sent me to the men which sit on the wall, that they may eat their own dung, and drink the water of their feet with you?

## plantation → planting — Ezekiel 17:7
*(from batch_0072_proposals.md)*

- rationale: CUR's "plant" ("furrows of her plant") is ungrammatical — a furrow belongs to a "planting" (the act/bed of planting), not to "a plant." YLT ("its planting") and Darby/ASV ("plantation/beds of its plantation") support "planting," which also matches the KJV's own use of "planting" elsewhere in this chapter's sense (cf. Isaiah 60:21, 61:3 entries below).

## plantedst → plantedst — Deuteronomy 6:11
*(from batch_0072_proposals.md)*

- rationale: CUR's "which thou plant not" breaks the second-person past-tense pattern shared by "filledst"/"diggedst" in the same verse. Webster and RWebster render this clause "which thou didst not plant," a construction that preserves the thou-past-tense set without re-inflecting or keeping "plantedst" itself.

## planters → husbandmen — Jeremiah 31:5
*(from batch_0072_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "the plants shall plant" is nonsensical (plants cannot plant) and merely re-inflects the rare word's root rather than replacing it, which the rules forbid. "husbandmen" is a common, well-attested KJV word for agricultural workers (e.g. Matthew 21:33) and avoids reusing "plant" as the subject noun.

## plantings → vineyards — Micah 1:6
*(from batch_0072_proposals.md)*

- rationale: CUR's "as plants of a vineyard" re-inflects the flagged word's root rather than replacing it, and is redundant with "vineyard" in the same clause. ASV/ACV render this "places for planting vineyards"; to avoid re-inflection and redundancy, "vineyards" alone is proposed, taking "an heap of the field, and as vineyards" as parallel to Wycliffe's sense of a vineyard reduced to a stone-heap — flagged for owner review given the resulting near-repetition of "vineyard."

## playedst → didst play — Ezekiel 16:15
*(from batch_0072_proposals.md)*

- rationale: CUR's "played" drops the second-person -edst inflection that the surrounding "didst trust"/"pouredst" forms require. RWebster's "didst play the harlot" preserves the thou-form pattern using the common auxiliary construction.

## playedst → didst play — Ezekiel 16:16
*(from batch_0072_proposals.md)*

- rationale: CUR's "play" (present tense) breaks the past-tense narrative of the verse ("didst take," "deckedst"). RWebster's "didst play the harlot" supplies the correct past-tense thou-form.
- NEW: And of thy garments thou didst take, and deckedst thy high places with divers colours, and didst play the harlot thereupon: the like things shall not come, neither shall it be so.

## player → harper — I Samuel 16:16
*(from batch_0073_proposals.md)*

- rationale: CUR's "cunning play on an harp" strips the noun down to a verb, ungrammatical after "a cunning." "harper" is a genuine, attested KJV noun for one who plays the harp (Revelation 14:2, "harpers harping with their harps") and fits the same slot without re-inflecting "player."
- NEW: Let our lord now command thy servants, which are before thee, to seek out a man, who is a cunning harper on an harp: and it shall come to pass, when the evil spirit from God is upon thee, that he shall play with his hand, and thou shalt be well.

- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "the ears plowed upon my back" is nonsensical (ears cannot plow). No witness offers an alternate word for the plowman-figure here, but "husbandmen" is a genuine, common KJV noun for agricultural workers (cf. plowmen→husbandmen at Isaiah 61:5 and Jeremiah 14:4 below) and restores sense to the verse.
- NEW: The husbandmen plowed upon my back: they made long their furrows.

## plowman → husbandman — Isaiah 28:24
*(from batch_0073_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "doth the peace plow" is nonsensical — "peace" cannot plow. "husbandman" is a genuine, common, well-attested KJV noun (2 Timothy 2:6, "the husbandman that laboureth") naming the same figure.
- NEW: Doth the husbandman plow all day to sow? doth he open and break the clods of his ground?

## plowman → husbandman — Amos 9:13
*(from batch_0073_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same reasoning as Isaiah 28:24 — CUR's "the peace shall overtake the reaper" is nonsensical; "husbandman" restores sense and is a genuine, common KJV word.
- NEW: Behold, the days come, saith the Lord, that the husbandman shall overtake the reaper, and the treader of grapes him that soweth seed; and the mountains shall drop sweet wine, and all the hills shall melt.

## plowmen → husbandmen — Isaiah 61:5
*(from batch_0073_proposals.md)*

## plowmen → husbandmen — Jeremiah 14:4
*(from batch_0073_proposals.md)*

- NEW: Because the ground is chapt, for there was no rain in the earth, the husbandmen were ashamed, they covered their heads.

## pluckt → torn off — Genesis 8:11
*(from batch_0073_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "plucked" is merely a modernized respelling of the flagged word "pluckt," not a genuine substitute. YLT's "an olive leaf torn off" supplies a genuinely different verb, common in the corpus (18 hits for "torn"), fitting the same clause.
- NEW: And the dove came in to him in the evening; and, lo, in her mouth was an olive leaf torn off: so Noah knew that the waters were abated from off the earth.

## plunge → dip — Job 9:31
*(from batch_0073_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "dip" is directly attested by YLT, "in corruption Thou dost dip me," a genuinely different, common word.

## pole → standard — Numbers 21:8
*(from batch_0073_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "standard" is directly attested by ASV and ACV, a genuinely different, common KJV word (used of military ensigns, cf. Numbers 2:2).
- NEW: And the Lord said unto Moses, Make thee a fiery serpent, and set it upon a standard: and it shall come to pass, that every one that is bitten, when he looketh upon it, shall live.

## pole → standard — Numbers 21:9
*(from batch_0073_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same reasoning as Numbers 21:8 — "standard" is directly attested by ASV and ACV for this parallel verse.
- NEW: And Moses made a serpent of brass, and put it upon a standard, and it came to pass, that if a serpent had bitten any man, when he beheld the serpent of brass, he lived.

## policy → cunning — Daniel 8:25
*(from batch_0073_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "wisdom" wrongly attributes a positive, godly virtue to this hostile figure's scheming. Darby renders this word "cunning," which preserves the negative, crafty sense of "policy" and is a common, well-attested KJV word (32 hits) used substantively elsewhere for shrewd ability.
- NEW: And through his cunning also he shall cause craft to prosper in his hand; and he shall magnify himself in his heart, and by peace shall destroy many: he shall also stand up against the Prince of princes; but he shall be broken without hand.

## polishing → form — Lamentations 4:7
*(from batch_0073_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "separateing" is not even a valid word (misspelled gerund of an unrelated verb). YLT renders this clause "of sapphire their form," supplying a genuine, common KJV noun describing the Nazarites' sapphire-like brilliance.
- NEW: Her Nazarites were purer than snow, they were whiter than milk, they were more ruddy in body than rubies, their form was of sapphire:

## pollution → uncleanness — Ezekiel 22:10
*(from batch_0073_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "set apart for unclean" is ungrammatical — "unclean" is an adjective and needs a noun after "for." "uncleanness" is the natural noun form, well attested in the KJV corpus (37 hits, e.g. Leviticus 15), and matches the sense given by Darby/ASV ("unclean in her separation/impurity").
- NEW: In thee have they discovered their fathers' nakedness: in thee have they humbled her that was set apart for uncleanness.

## pollutions → filthiness — Acts 15:20
*(from batch_0073_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "filthiness" is directly attested by Tyndale ("filthynes of ymages") and Geneva1599 ("filthinesse of idoles"), period witnesses for this exact clause.
- NEW: But that we write unto them, that they abstain from filthiness of idols, and from fornication, and from things strangled, and from blood.

## pollutions → filthiness — II Peter 2:20
*(from batch_0073_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "filthiness" is directly attested by Geneva1599, "the filthinesse of the world," a period witness for this exact clause.
- NEW: For if after they have escaped the filthiness of the world through the knowledge of the Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ, they are again entangled therein, and overcome, the latter end is worse with them than the beginning.

## ponder → weigh — Proverbs 4:26
*(from batch_0073_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "weigh" is a genuine, common KJV word (84 hits), and Geneva1599's rendering of the parallel verse (5:6, "she weigheth not") shows the same root already applied to this Hebrew concept in a period text.
- NEW: Weigh the path of thy feet, and let all thy ways be established.

## ponder → weigh — Proverbs 5:6
*(from batch_0073_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Geneva1599 directly attests "weigheth" for this very verse ("She weigheth not the way of life"), giving period support for "weigh" as the replacement.
- NEW: Lest thou shouldest weigh the path of life, her ways are moveable, that thou canst not know them.

## pondered → conferred — Luke 2:19
*(from batch_0073_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "conferred" (from Latin conferre, "to bring together") closely matches the Greek symballousa ("throwing/bringing together") behind this verse, echoing Wycliffe's "berynge togider in hir herte." It is a genuine, attested KJV word (Galatians 1:16, "I conferred not with flesh and blood"). Flag for owner awareness: this shifts the nuance from simple silent reflection to active comparing/weighing of the events, a subtle theological difference from "pondered."
- NEW: But Mary kept all these things, and conferred them in her heart.

## poorer → less able — Leviticus 27:8
*(from batch_0073_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "poor than thy estimation" is ungrammatical — "poor" is not a comparative and cannot take "than." No witness supplies a different single-word comparative for this sense; "less able" is proposed as a minimal rephrase (not a bare word-swap, per the rules' allowance for clarity) describing inability to meet the valuation, matching the sense of DRC ("not able to pay the estimation") and BSB ("too poor to pay the valuation"). Flagged for owner review as an imperfect one-word-rule fit.
- NEW: But if he be less able than thy estimation, then he shall present himself before the priest, and the priest shall value him; according to his ability that vowed shall the priest value him.

## populous → plenteous — Deuteronomy 26:5
*(from batch_0074_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "plenteous" is a genuinely different, attested KJV word (14 hits) conveying the same sense of abundance/greatness as "populous," and no witness offers a cleaner single-word alternative for this sense of "many people."
- NEW: And thou shalt speak and say before the Lord thy God, A Syrian ready to perish was my father, and he went down into Egypt, and sojourned there with a few, and became there a nation, great, mighty, and plenteous:

## populous → plenteous — Nahum 3:8
*(from batch_0074_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same reasoning as Deuteronomy 26:5 — "plenteous" is a genuinely different, attested KJV word conveying abundance, consistent with Geneva1599's "full of people."
- NEW: Art thou better than plenteous No, that was situate among the rivers, that had the waters round about it, whose rampart was the sea, and her wall was from the sea?

## port → gate — Nehemiah 2:13
*(from batch_0074_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "gate" is directly attested by DRC and ASV ("dung gate"), a genuinely different, common word; its repetition alongside "gate of the valley" and "gates thereof" elsewhere in the verse reflects Nehemiah's own naming convention for the city's several gates, not an error.
- NEW: And I went out by night by the gate of the valley, even before the dragon well, and to the dung gate, and viewed the walls of Jerusalem, which were broken down, and the gates thereof were consumed with fire.

## possessor → maker — Genesis 14:19
*(from batch_0074_proposals.md)*

- rationale: CUR's "buy" is ungrammatical (a bare verb where a noun is required: "God, buy of heaven and earth"). DRC and BSB both render this title "creator/maker of heaven and earth"; "maker" is a common, well-attested KJV word (25 hits) used of God elsewhere (e.g. Job, Isaiah).
- NEW: And he blessed him, and said, Blessed be Abram of the most high God, maker of heaven and earth:

*(from batch_0074_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE

## possessors → owners — Zechariah 11:5
*(from batch_0074_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR drops the noun ending entirely, leaving "Whose possess slay them," which is ungrammatical (no subject noun). Darby and DRC support "owners"/"possessed" as the acting party; "owners" is a genuine, attested KJV word (5 hits) restoring the sentence's subject.
- NEW: Whose owners slay them, and hold themselves not guilty: and they that sell them say, Blessed be the Lord; for I am rich: and their own shepherds pity them not.

*(from batch_0074_proposals.md)*

## pouredst → didst pour out — Ezekiel 16:15
*(from batch_0074_proposals.md)*

RWebster's "didst pour out thy harlotries" supplies the correct construction.
- NEW: But thou didst trust in thine own beauty, and didst play the harlot because of thy renown, and didst pour out thy fornications on every one that passed by; his it was.

## pourtray → grave — Ezekiel 4:1
*(from batch_0074_proposals.md)*

*(from batch_0074_proposals.md)*

"ordain" is a common, well-attested KJV word (43 hits) and matches the replacement already chosen for "predestinated" at Ephesians 1:5 and 1:11 below, keeping the audit's terminology consistent across the Pauline corpus.

## predestinate → ordain — Romans 8:30
*(from batch_0074_proposals.md)*

*(from batch_0074_proposals.md)*

## preparedst → madest — Psalms 80:9

## presbytery → elders — I Timothy 4:14
*(from batch_0075_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "estate" is meaningless in context ("the hands of the estate"). Geneva1599 renders this "the companie of the Eldership," and BSB "the elders"; "elders" is a genuine, extremely common KJV word (177 hits) naming the same body that laid on hands.

*(from batch_0075_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "written" duplicates "write" earlier in the same clause ("write grievousness which they have written"). "devised" is a genuine, common KJV word (13 hits) for the malicious planning behind unjust decrees, avoiding the repetition.

*(from batch_0075_proposals.md)*

- rationale: CUR's "keep of men" is ungrammatical (a bare verb where a noun is required). DRC directly attests "O keeper of men" for this exact clause, a genuine, common KJV word (46 hits).
- NEW: I have sinned; what shall I do unto thee, O thou keeper of men? why hast thou set me as a mark against thee, so that I am a burden to myself?

## pressfat → winepress — Haggai 2:16
*(from batch_0075_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's bare "wine" reads oddly beside "out of the press" later in the clause. RWebster directly attests "winepress" for this exact verse, a genuine, common KJV word (19 hits) naming the same vessel.

*(from batch_0075_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "shall fill to speak a word in my name" is nonsensical. "dare" is a genuine, common KJV word (20 hits) matching BSB's "dares to speak," capturing the sense of unauthorized boldness without merely re-inflecting "presume."
- NEW: But the prophet, which shall dare to speak a word in my name, which I have not commanded him to speak, or that shall speak in the name of other gods, even that prophet shall die.

## presume → imagine — Esther 7:5
*(from batch_0075_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "durst fill in his heart to do so" is ungrammatical. Since the verse already contains "durst" (itself covering the daring sense), a genuinely different word is needed for "presume": "imagine," a common KJV word already used elsewhere in this audit for secret scheming (cf. Mark 13:11; Psalm 2:1, "why do the heathen ... imagine a vain thing"), fits "presume in his heart" well.
- NEW: Then the king Ahasuerus answered and said unto Esther the queen, Who is he, and where is he, that durst imagine in his heart to do so?

## presumed → durst — Numbers 14:44
*(from batch_0075_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "they lifted to go up unto the hill top" is nonsensical (lifted requires an object). "durst" is a genuine, common KJV word (11 hits) fitting this exact sense of defiant boldness, matching BSB's "dared to go up."
- NEW: But they durst go up unto the hill top: nevertheless the ark of the covenant of the Lord, and Moses, departed not out of the camp.

*(from batch_0075_proposals.md)*

*(from batch_0075_proposals.md)*

## pricked → pierced — Psalms 73:21
*(from batch_0075_proposals.md)*

- rationale: CUR's "sharpened in my reins" is an odd, unclear image (a person is not "sharpened"). BSB directly attests "I was pierced within" for this verse; "pierced" is a genuine, common KJV word conveying the same inward pain.
- NEW: Thus my heart was grieved, and I was pierced in my reins.

## pricked → pierced — Acts 2:37
*(from batch_0075_proposals.md)*

## pricking → fretting — Ezekiel 28:24
*(from batch_0075_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE

- NEW: And there shall be no more a fretting brier unto the house of Israel, nor any grieving thorn of all that are round about them, that despised them; and they shall know that I am the Lord God.

## princess → queen — Lamentations 1:1
*(from batch_0075_proposals.md)*

- rationale: CUR's "queens" (plural) breaks number agreement with the singular "she" describing the city throughout the verse ("she that was great ... and queens among the provinces" is grammatically broken). No witness offers an alternate word, but the singular "queen" restores agreement while remaining a genuinely different, common word from "princess."

## principality → rule — Ephesians 1:21
*(from batch_0075_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE

## principles → elements — Hebrews 5:12
*(from batch_0075_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "principalities" inverts the meaning entirely — principalities are ruling powers, not basic teachings, and this breaks the sense of elementary doctrine. DRC, YLT, and Darby all render this "elements," a genuine, attested KJV word (4 hits) matching the correct sense.
- NEW: For when for the time ye ought to be teachers, ye have need that one teach you again which be the first elements of the oracles of God; and are become such as have need of milk, and not of strong meat.

## principles → word — Hebrews 6:1
*(from batch_0075_proposals.md)*

## prised → valued — Zechariah 11:13
*(from batch_0075_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "wasted at of them" is nonsensical. Geneva1599 directly attests "a goodly price, that I was valued at of them," a genuine, attested KJV word (4 hits).
- NEW: And the Lord said unto me, Cast it unto the potter: a goodly price that I was valued at of them. And I took the thirty pieces of silver, and cast them to the potter in the house of the Lord.

## private → particular — II Peter 1:20
*(from batch_0075_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "due interpretation" shifts the theological sense from "one's own individual interpretation" to "proper/rightful interpretation," weakening the verse's specific point. Darby renders this "its own particular interpretation," and "particular" is a genuine, attested KJV word (5 hits) that preserves the original sense of an individual's private reading.

## prize → reward — I Corinthians 9:24
*(from batch_0075_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "reward" is directly attested by BBE, "only one gets the reward," a genuinely different, extremely common word preserving the sense of a race's winning token.
- NEW: Know ye not that they which run in a race run all, but one receiveth the reward? So run, that ye may obtain.

## prize → reward — Philippians 3:14
*(from batch_0075_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same reasoning as I Corinthians 9:24 — "reward" is a genuinely different, common word matching BBE's rendering of this same concept ("the reward of the high purpose of God").
- NEW: I press toward the mark for the reward of the high calling of God in Christ Jesus.

## procure → commit — Jeremiah 26:19
*(from batch_0075_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "we thee great evil" is nonsensical (a pronoun used as a verb). ASV and ACV both attest "commit great evil against our own souls" for this exact clause, a genuine, common word (178 hits).
- NEW: Did Hezekiah king of Judah and all Judah put him at all to death? did he not fear the Lord, and besought the Lord, and the Lord repented him of the evil which he had pronounced against them? Thus might we commit great evil against our souls.

## procure → provide — Jeremiah 33:9
*(from batch_0075_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "prosperity that I thee unto it" is nonsensical (a pronoun used as a verb). BSB renders this clause "the prosperity that I will provide for it"; "provide" is a genuine, attested KJV word (25 hits) fitting the same slot.
- NEW: And it shall be to me a name of joy, a praise and an honour before all the nations of the earth, which shall hear all the good that I do unto them: and they shall fear and tremble for all the goodness and for all the prosperity that I provide unto it.

## procured → done — Jeremiah 2:17
*(from batch_0075_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "committed this unto thyself" is an unusual pairing (committed typically takes a direct offense as object, not "this unto thyself"). DRC directly attests "Hath not this been done to thee" for this exact clause, a genuine, extremely common word.
- NEW: Hast thou not done this unto thyself, in that thou hast forsaken the Lord thy God, when he led thee by the way?

## procured → brought — Jeremiah 4:18
*(from batch_0075_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "have committed these things unto thee" is the same unusual pairing as Jeremiah 2:17. DRC attests "have brought these things upon thee" for this exact clause; "brought" is a genuine, extremely common word (884 hits) that also avoids echoing "doings...done" awkwardly.
- NEW: Thy way and thy doings have brought these things unto thee; this is thy wickedness, because it is bitter, because it reacheth unto thine heart.

## procureth → seeketh — Proverbs 11:27
*(from batch_0075_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "seeketh favour" is directly attested by both YLT ("Seeketh a pleasing thing") and ASV ("seeketh favor"), which independently choose this same repeated construction ("diligently seeketh good seeketh favor") as a deliberate parallelism, matching the verse's own rhetorical style.
- NEW: He that diligently seeketh good seeketh favour: but he that seeketh mischief, it shall come unto him.

## profaneness → wickedness — Jeremiah 23:15
*(from batch_0076_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "wickedness" is directly attested by Geneva1599 and DRC-style rendering ("wickednesse"/"corruption"), a genuinely different, common word preserving the sense of the verse without inversion.
- NEW: Therefore thus saith the Lord of hosts concerning the prophets; Behold, I will feed them with wormwood, and make them drink the water of gall: for from the prophets of Jerusalem is wickedness gone forth into all the land.

## profound → deep — Hosea 5:2
*(from batch_0076_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE

- NEW: And the revolters are deep to make slaughter, though I have been a rebuker of them all.

## progenitors → fathers — Genesis 49:26
*(from batch_0076_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "fathers" is directly attested by DRC, "the blessings of his fathers," a genuinely different, common, permitted kinship word.
- NEW: The blessings of thy father have prevailed above the blessings of my fathers unto the utmost bound of the everlasting hills: they shall be on the head of Joseph, and on the crown of the head of him that was separate from his brethren.

## prognosticators → soothsayers — Isaiah 47:13
*(from batch_0076_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "kinsfolks" is a severe meaning inversion — the verse names occult diviners (astrologers, stargazers) who read omens by the month, not family relations, and "kinsfolks" also runs afoul of the ruling that kinship terms are limited to husband/wife/brother/sister/father/mother. "soothsayers" is a genuine, attested KJV word (6 hits) naming the same class of diviner, matching the sense of UKJV's "monthly sign tellers" and Darby's "who predict."
- NEW: Thou art wearied in the multitude of thy counsels. Let now the astrologers, the stargazers, the monthly soothsayers, stand up, and save thee from these things that shall come upon thee.

## promotion → honour — Psalms 75:6
*(from batch_0076_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "high" is ungrammatical, used as a noun where none exists ("For high cometh"). BBE directly attests "honour does not come from the east," and "honour" is a genuine, extremely common KJV word (198 hits).
- NEW: For honour cometh neither from the east, nor from the west, nor from the south.

## promotion → honour — Proverbs 3:35
*(from batch_0076_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "high" is ungrammatical for the same reason as Psalms 75:6. "honour" is proposed for consistency; the resulting irony (shame is the fools' "honour") preserves the verse's original point that fools receive shame as their reward.
- NEW: The wise shall inherit glory: but shame shall be the honour of fools.

- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: The single concatenated "restingplace" is not standard KJV orthography; Geneva1599, DRC, Webster, ASV, UKJV, and ACV all render it as the two-word phrase "resting place" (3 KJV hits), which is the corpus's customary form.
- NEW: My people hath been lost sheep: their shepherds have caused them to go astray, they have turned them away on the mountains: they have gone from mountain to hill, they have forgotten their resting place.

## restorer → comforter — Ruth 4:15
*(from batch_0082_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "return" is a bare noun that cannot govern "of thy life" sensibly. Wycliffe/DRC read "comforte thy soul"; "comforter" is well-attested in the KJV corpus (7 hits, e.g. Job, Lamentations) and preserves the sense of one who restores wellbeing, paired naturally with "nourisher".
- NEW: And he shall be unto thee a comforter of thy life, and a nourisher of thine old age: for thy daughter in law, which loveth thee, which is better to thee than seven sons, hath born him.

## restorer → maker — Isaiah 58:12
*(from batch_0082_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE

"maker" (25 KJV hits) supplies a common, period-authentic noun that preserves the sense of one who fashions the paths anew for habitation, without duplicating "repairer" already in the verse.

## restrain → withdrawest — Job 15:8
*(from batch_0082_proposals.md)*

## restrain → gird upon thee — Psalms 76:10
*(from batch_0082_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR "gird" alone is transitive in a way the KJV does not use it here (one girds a thing "upon" oneself, not girds the thing itself). ASV/Darby read "shalt thou gird upon thee" for this verse; "gird" is well-attested (94 hits) and adding "upon thee" completes the authentic construction.
- NEW: Surely the wrath of man shall praise thee: the remainder of wrath shalt thou gird upon thee.

*(from batch_0082_proposals.md)*

- rationale: CUR "makest" inverts the meaning (making prayer, rather than hindering it). "hinderest"/"withholdest" return zero hits in the KJV corpus, so both are barred by rule 4. Wycliffe/DRC read "hast taken away prayers before God"; "takest away" is attested in the corpus (1 hit) and "takest" itself is common (9 hits), giving a genuinely different, corpus-supported phrase.

*(from batch_0082_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
