- rationale: "serve" is a disguised re-inflection of the flagged word (servedst is simply "serve" stripped of its ending), and it breaks the second-person past tense entirely ("thou serve not" is ungrammatical). DRC reads "thou didst not serve"; using a genuinely different verb, "worship" (common, 199 verses), with the auxiliary carrying the tense avoids both problems.
- NEW: Because thou didst not worship the Lord thy God with joyfulness, and with gladness of heart, for the abundance of all things;

## servitor → minister — II Kings 4:43
*(from batch_0087_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "minister" matches YLT exactly and is common in KJV (172 verses).
- NEW: And his minister said, What, should I set this before an hundred men? He said again, Give the people, that they may eat: for thus saith the Lord, They shall eat, and shall leave thereof.

## servitude → service — II Chronicles 10:4
*(from batch_0087_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "service" matches ASV and YLT, common in KJV.
- NEW: Thy father made our yoke grievous: now therefore ease thou somewhat the grievous service of thy father, and his heavy yoke that he put upon us, and we will serve thee.

## servitude → service — Lamentations 1:3
*(from batch_0087_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: matches YLT exactly.
- NEW: Judah is gone into captivity because of affliction, and because of great service: she dwelleth among the heathen, she findeth no rest: all her persecutors overtook her between the straits.

## setter → forth — Acts 17:18
*(from batch_0087_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR is broken ("a forth forth of strange gods" is nonsense, a duplicated word). BBE reads "a preacher of strange gods"; "preacher" is attested in KJV (Ecclesiastes's title, 2 Peter 2:5, 1 Timothy 2:7) and restores sound grammar and meaning.
- NEW: Then certain philosophers of the Epicureans, and of the Stoicks, encountered him. And some said, What will this babbler say? other some, He seemeth to be a preacher of strange gods: because he preached unto them Jesus, and the resurrection.

## severally → privately — I Corinthians 12:11
*(from batch_0087_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "privately" mistakes "severally" (individually) for "secretly," the wrong sense entirely. "particularly" is attested in KJV (Acts 21:19, "declared particularly") and matches the individuating sense correctly.
- NEW: But all these worketh that one and the selfsame Spirit, dividing to every man particularly as he will.

## severity → sharpness — Romans 11:22
*(from batch_0087_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR cures one rare word (severity, 1 occurrence) with another equally rare word (sharpness, 1 occurrence) — a circular swap forbidden by the "don't cure rare with rare" rule. "rigour" is better attested (5 verses, e.g. Leviticus 25:43 "rule over him with rigour") and fits the sense of stern, exacting judgment.
- NEW: Behold therefore the goodness and rigour of God: on them which fell, rigour; but toward thee, goodness, if thou continue in his goodness: otherwise thou also shalt be cut off.

## sew → stitch — Ecclesiastes 3:7
*(from batch_0087_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "stitch" has zero occurrences in the KJV corpus (forbidden by rule 4). "join" is attested (14 verses) and captures the sense opposite to "rend" (tear apart).
- NEW: A time to rend, and a time to join; a time to keep silence, and a time to speak;

## sew → stitch — Ezekiel 13:18
*(from batch_0087_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: same problem as Ecclesiastes 3:7; Wycliffe itself reads "sowen togidere" (joined together), supporting "join" as the period-authentic construction.
- NEW: And say, Thus saith the Lord God; Woe to the women that join pillows to all armholes, and make kerchiefs upon the head of every stature to hunt souls! Will ye hunt the souls of my people, and will ye save the souls alive that come unto you?

## sewed → stitched — Genesis 3:7
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "stitched" has zero KJV occurrences. "joined" is attested (43 verses) and matches the sense of leaves joined together; DRC/Wycliffe/Geneva all use "sewed together" = joined together.
- NEW: And the eyes of them both were opened, and they knew that they were naked; and they joined fig leaves together, and made themselves aprons.

## sewed → stitched — Job 16:15
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "stitched" is unattested; "bound" (103 verses) fits the sense of fastening sackcloth upon the body.
- NEW: I have bound sackcloth upon my skin, and defiled my horn in the dust.

## sewest → stitchest — Job 14:17
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "stitchest" is unattested. ASV reads "fastenest," Darby "heapest" — both point to concealment/binding-up. "coverest" is attested (2 verses) and fits the parallel with "sealed up in a bag."
- NEW: My transgression is sealed up in a bag, and thou coverest up mine iniquity.

## seweth → stitcheth — Mark 2:21
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "stitcheth" is unattested. "putteth" (31 verses) matches the sense exactly and parallels BBE's "puts a bit of new cloth."
- NEW: No man also putteth a piece of new cloth on an old garment: else the new piece that filled it up taketh away from the old, and the rent is made worse.

## shade → shadow — Psalms 121:5
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "shadow" matches Geneva, Darby, and ASV exactly, and is common in KJV (86 verses).
- NEW: The Lord is thy keeper: the Lord is thy shadow upon thy right hand.

## shady → tree — Job 40:21
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR is broken ("the tree trees" duplicates the noun). Dropping the adjective and using the plain noun "trees" (common, 141 verses) preserves the meaning without the duplication; the following clause's "shadow"/"shade" still carries the shade-imagery.
- NEW: He lieth under the trees, in the covert of the reed, and fens.

## shady → tree — Job 40:22
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: same fix as 40:21; "shadow" later in the verse retains the shade-sense.
- NEW: The trees cover him with their shadow; the willows of the brook compass him about.

## shambles → market — I Corinthians 10:25
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "market" matches Tyndale and YLT and is common in KJV.
- NEW: Whatsoever is sold in the market, that eat, asking no question for conscience sake:

## shameful → shame — Jeremiah 11:13
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR swaps an adjective for a noun, producing broken grammar ("that shame thing"). "vile" (20 verses, e.g. Philippians 3:21) is a genuine adjective replacement fitting the sense of a disgraceful idol.
- NEW: For according to the number of thy cities were thy gods, O Judah; and according to the number of the streets of Jerusalem have ye set up altars to that vile thing, even altars to burn incense unto Baal.

## shameful → shame — Habakkuk 2:16
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: same grammar problem as Jeremiah 11:13 ("shame spewing" is not a legal noun-phrase); "vile" restores the adjective slot and is well-attested.
- NEW: Thou art filled with shame for glory: drink thou also, and let thy foreskin be uncovered: the cup of the Lord's right hand shall be turned unto thee, and vile spewing shall be on thy glory.

## shamelessly → surely — II Samuel 6:20
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "surely" inverts the meaning, turning Michal's scorn into approval. YLT's own rendering, "openly uncovered," better preserves the reproach; "openly" is attested (16 verses).
- NEW: Then David returned to bless his household. And Michal the daughter of Saul came out to meet David, and said, How glorious was the king of Israel to day, who uncovered himself to day in the eyes of the handmaids of his servants, as one of the vain fellows openly uncovereth himself!

## shape → fashion — Luke 3:22
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "fashion" is attested (28 verses) and matches Tyndale, Geneva, and DRC exactly.
- NEW: And the Holy Ghost descended in a bodily fashion like a dove upon him, and a voice came from heaven, which said, Thou art my beloved Son; in thee I am well pleased.

## shape → fashion — John 5:37
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: consistent with Luke 3:22, matches Tyndale and Geneva exactly.
- NEW: And the Father himself, which hath sent me, hath borne witness of me. Ye have neither heard his voice at any time, nor seen his fashion.

## shapen → formed — Psalms 51:5
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "formed" matches UKJV and is common in KJV (66 verses).
- NEW: Behold, I was formed in iniquity; and in sin did my mother conceive me.

## shapes → forms — Revelation 9:7
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "forms" is essentially unattested in the corpus (this would be its only occurrence). Wycliffe and Tyndale use the singular collective "licnesse"/"similitude"; "likeness" is well attested (31 verses) and works as a collective noun with the plural subject, as the witnesses themselves show.
- NEW: And the likeness of the locusts was like unto horses prepared unto battle; and on their heads were as it were crowns like gold, and their faces were as the faces of men.

## share → plowshare — I Samuel 13:20
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "plowshare" matches DRC and Darby exactly.
- NEW: But all the Israelites went down to the Philistines, to sharpen every man his plowshare, and his coulter, and his axe, and his mattock.

## sharpen → stood — I Samuel 13:20
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "stood" is nonsensical here ("to stood every man his share"), a broken verb swap. "whet" is attested in KJV (4 verses, e.g. Deuteronomy 32:41, Psalm 7:12) and is the period word for sharpening an edge.
- NEW: But all the Israelites went down to the Philistines, to whet every man his share, and his coulter, and his axe, and his mattock.

## sharpen → stood — I Samuel 13:21
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: same fix as 13:20 for consistency within the same narrative.
- NEW: Yet they had a file for the mattocks, and for the coulters, and for the forks, and for the axes, and to whet the goads.

## sharper → keener — Micah 7:4
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "keener" is unattested in KJV (0 occurrences). ASV, ACV, and Darby all read "worse than a thorn hedge"; "worse" is common (26 verses) and preserves the comparative sense.
- NEW: The best of them is as a brier: the most upright is worse than a thorn hedge: the day of thy watchmen and thy visitation cometh; now shall be their perplexity.

## sharper → keener — Hebrews 4:12
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "keener" is unattested. DRC and ASV read "more piercing"; "piercing" is attested (2 verses, and the verse itself already uses "piercing" as a participle later, but as an adjective here it fits the comparative construction cleanly following DRC's model).
- NEW: For the word of God is quick, and powerful, and more piercing than any twoedged sword, piercing even to the dividing asunder of soul and spirit, and of the joints and marrow, and is a discerner of the thoughts and intents of the heart.

## sharply → mightily — Judges 8:1
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "mightily" changes the sense from manner (harshly) to degree/power, an awkward fit for "chide." "vehemently" is attested (6 verses) and fits a harsh verbal rebuke precisely.
- NEW: And the men of Ephraim said unto him, Why hast thou served us thus, that thou calledst us not, when thou wentest to fight with the Midianites? And they did chide with him vehemently.

## sharply → mightily — Titus 1:13
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: same fix as Judges 8:1 for consistency; "vehemently" fits "rebuke" better than "mightily."
- NEW: This witness is true. Wherefore rebuke them vehemently, that they may be sound in the faith;

## sharpness → severity — II Corinthians 13:10
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: this is the mirror half of the Romans 11:22 circular swap (severity ↔ sharpness, both rare — 1 occurrence each). "rigour" (5 verses) breaks the circularity and fits "use rigour" as an authoritative harsh dealing, paralleling Leviticus 25:43.
- NEW: Therefore I write these things being absent, lest being present I should use rigour, according to the power which the Lord hath given me to edification, and not to destruction.

## shearer → shorn — Acts 8:32
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR turns "shearer" into the wrong part of speech ("dumb before his shorn" is not grammatical). Darby's construction, "as a lamb is dumb in presence of him that shears him," rephrases the noun phrase as a relative clause using the attested verb "sheareth," avoiding both the broken grammar and any bare re-inflection of "shearer" itself.
- NEW: The place of the scripture which he read was this, He was led as a sheep to the slaughter; and like a lamb dumb before him that sheareth him, so opened he not his mouth:

## shedder → shed — Ezekiel 18:10
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR turns the agent-noun "shedder" into the wrong part of speech ("a shed of blood" is nonsense). Rephrasing as a relative clause with the attested verb "sheddeth" (following the same pattern as the shearer fix) restores sound grammar without merely re-inflecting the flagged word.
- NEW: If he beget a son that is a robber, one that sheddeth blood, and that doeth the like to any one of these things,

## sheepcote → habitation — II Samuel 7:8
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "habitation" is common in KJV (60+ verses) and the following clause "from following the sheep" preserves the pastoral sense.
- NEW: Now therefore so shalt thou say unto my servant David, Thus saith the Lord of hosts, I took thee from the habitation, from following the sheep, to be ruler over my people, over Israel:

## sheepcote → habitation — I Chronicles 17:7
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: consistent with II Samuel 7:8; YLT itself uses "habitation" at the parallel verse.
- NEW: Now therefore thus shalt thou say unto my servant David, Thus saith the Lord of hosts, I took thee from the habitation, even from following the sheep, that thou shouldest be ruler over my people Israel:

## sheepcotes → folds — I Samuel 24:3
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "hedges" contradicts the surrounding sense — the text describes a cave among sheep-pens, and hedges (rows of bushes) do not fit "where was a cave." RWebster and Darby read "sheepfolds"; "folds" is attested (7 verses) and restores the correct referent.
- NEW: And he came to the folds by the way, where was a cave; and Saul went in to cover his feet: and David and his men remained in the sides of the cave.

## sheepfold → fold of the sheep — John 10:1
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "palace" is a gross meaning inversion — a humble sheep-pen becomes a royal building, the opposite sense. YLT and Darby read "the fold of the sheep"; "fold" is attested (7 verses) and restores the correct image.
- NEW: Verily, verily, I say unto you, He that entereth not by the door into the fold of the sheep, but climbeth up some other way, the same is a thief and a robber.

## sheepskins → skins — Hebrews 11:37
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "skins" is attested (19 verses) and does not duplicate the adjacent "goatskins."
- NEW: They were stoned, they were sawn asunder, were tempted, were slain with the sword: they wandered about in skins and goatskins; being destitute, afflicted, tormented;

## sheet → cloth — Acts 10:11
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "cloth" matches BBE and is attested (18 verses).
- NEW: And saw heaven opened, and a certain vessel descending unto him, as it had been a great cloth knit at the four corners, and let down to the earth:

*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*

- rationale: consistent with Acts 10:11.

*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*

- NEW: And Samson said unto them, I will now put forth a riddle unto you: if ye can certainly declare it me within the seven days of the feast, and find it out, then I will give you thirty linen garments and thirty changes of raiment:

## sheets → linen — Judges 14:13
*(from batch_0088_proposals.md)*

- rationale: same fix as 14:12 for consistency within the same riddle-narrative.

## shelter → refuge — Job 24:8
*(from batch_0089_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "refuge" matches YLT exactly and is common in KJV (50 verses).
- NEW: They are wet with the showers of the mountains, and embrace the rock for want of a refuge.

## shelter → refuge — Psalms 61:3
*(from batch_0089_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: matches YLT, Darby, and ASV exactly.
- NEW: For thou hast been a refuge for me, and a strong tower from the enemy.

## sherd → piece — Isaiah 30:14
*(from batch_0089_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "piece" matches Webster and RWebster exactly and is common in KJV.
- NEW: And he shall break it as the breaking of the potters' vessel that is broken in pieces; he shall not spare: so that there shall not be found in the bursting of it a piece to take fire from the hearth, or to take water withal out of the pit.

## sherds → stones — Ezekiel 23:34
*(from batch_0089_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "stones" wrongly changes the material — the image is of a broken earthen vessel, not stone. Webster and RWebster read "pieces"; "pieces" is common (112 verses) and preserves the pottery-breaking image.
- NEW: Thou shalt even drink it and suck it out, and thou shalt break the pieces thereof, and pluck off thine own breasts: for I have spoken it, saith the Lord God.

## sheriffs → magistrates — Daniel 3:2
*(from batch_0089_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "magistrates" matches DRC's own rendering of this list of officials, and is attested in KJV.

## sheriffs → magistrates — Daniel 3:3
*(from batch_0089_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: consistent with Daniel 3:2.
- NEW: Then the princes, the governors, and captains, the judges, the treasurers, the counsellors, the magistrates, and all the rulers of the provinces, were gathered together unto the dedication of the image that Nebuchadnezzar the king had set up; and they stood before the image that Nebuchadnezzar had set up.

## shewedst → saw — Nehemiah 9:10
*(from batch_0089_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "saw" inverts agency — God is the one performing the signs and wonders, not merely observing them, and the following clause "so didst thou get thee a name" only makes sense if God acted. "wroughtest" is attested in KJV and fits the sense of actively performing signs and wonders.
- NEW: And wroughtest signs and wonders upon Pharaoh, and on all his servants, and on all the people of his land: for thou knewest that they dealt proudly against them. So didst thou get thee a name, as it is this day.

## shewedst → saw — Jeremiah 11:18
*(from batch_0089_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: same agency-inversion problem as Nehemiah 9:10, here about revealing knowledge rather than performing wonders. "madest known" is an attested KJV phrase and fits the sense of disclosure precisely.
- NEW: And the Lord hath given me knowledge of it, and I know it: then thou madest known to me their doings.

## shipmaster → great — Jonah 1:6
*(from batch_0089_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR turns the noun into a bare adjective ("the great came to him" is ungrammatical). BBE and UKJV read "ship's captain"/"ship captain"; "captain" is common in KJV (138+ verses) and restores sound grammar.
- NEW: So the captain came to him, and said unto him, What meanest thou, O sleeper? arise, call upon thy God, if so be that God will think upon us, that we perish not.

## shipmaster → great — Revelation 18:17
*(from batch_0089_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: same grammar problem as Jonah 1:6; "captain" restores it consistently.
- NEW: For in one hour so great riches is come to nought. And every captain, and all the company in ships, and sailors, and as many as trade by sea, stood afar off,

## shivers → pieces — Revelation 2:27
*(from batch_0089_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "pieces" matches Darby exactly and is common in KJV.
- NEW: And he shall rule them with a rod of iron; as the vessels of a potter shall they be broken to pieces: even as I received of my Father.

## shock → corn — Job 5:26
*(from batch_0089_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR is broken ("a corn of corn cometh in" duplicates the noun nonsensically). BSB reads "a sheaf of grain gathered in season"; "sheaf" is attested in KJV (8 verses) and fits the harvest image precisely.
- NEW: Thou shalt come to thy grave in a full age, like as a sheaf of corn cometh in in his season.

## shocks → tombs — Judges 15:5
*(from batch_0089_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "tombs" is a wholesale meaning change with no basis in any witness — burning tombs makes no sense beside burning standing grain and vineyards. "sheaves" (attested, 9 verses) matches the sense of piled cut grain that the surrounding context (Samson's foxes burning the harvest) requires.
- NEW: And when he had set the brands on fire, he let them go into the standing corn of the Philistines, and burnt up both the sheaves, and also the standing corn, with the vineyards and olives.

## shooters → archers — II Samuel 11:24
*(from batch_0089_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "archers" matches Wycliffe and DRC exactly and is attested in KJV.
- NEW: And the archers shot from off the wall upon thy servants; and some of the king's servants be dead, and thy servant Uriah the Hittite is dead also.

## shorter → reap — Isaiah 28:20
*(from batch_0089_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "reap" is the wrong part of speech entirely ("the bed is reap" is not grammatical). DRC and Wycliffe read "straitened"/"streit"; "strait" is attested in KJV (10 verses) and is the period adjective for "too narrow/short."
- NEW: For the bed is strait than that a man can stretch himself on it: and the covering narrower than that he can wrap himself in it.

## shorter → reap — Ezekiel 42:5
*(from batch_0089_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: same part-of-speech problem as Isaiah 28:20. "less" (28 verses) is a common comparative adjective fitting the architectural comparison.
- NEW: Now the upper chambers were less: for the galleries were higher than these, than the lower, and than the middlemost of the building.

## shrank → withered — Genesis 32:32
*(from batch_0089_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "withered" is common in KJV (24 verses) and is a reasonable period synonym for a sinew's contraction; no witness suggests inversion or contradiction of sense.
- NEW: Therefore the children of Israel eat not of the sinew which withered, which is upon the hollow of the thigh, unto this day: because he touched the hollow of Jacob's thigh in the sinew that withered.

## shred → cleaved — II Kings 4:39
*(from batch_0089_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "cleaved" is ambiguous in English (it can mean either "split apart" or "stuck fast"), muddying the sense of chopping herbs into a pot. "cut" is unambiguous, extremely common in KJV (327 verses), and YLT's own rendering ("splitteth") points the same direction.
- NEW: And one went out into the field to gather herbs, and found a wild vine, and gathered thereof wild gourds his lap full, and came and cut them into the pot of pottage: for they knew them not.

## shrines → temples — Acts 19:24
*(from batch_0089_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "temples" matches Geneva, DRC, and Darby exactly and is attested in KJV. (Note: this verse also carries the separate "silversmith → smith" fix from batch 0090 — see that file's proposal for the combined verse text.)
- NEW: For a certain man named Demetrius, a smith, which made silver temples for Diana, brought no small gain unto the craftsmen;

## shroud → wood — Ezekiel 31:3
*(from batch_0089_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "wood" produces an odd phrase ("a shadowing wood") that reads as a category mismatch (wood the material, not wood the grove). ASV and ACV read "forest-like shade"; "forest" is attested (41 verses) and gives a sensible image of dense, shading branches.
- NEW: Behold, the Assyrian was a cedar in Lebanon with fair branches, and with a shadowing forest, and of an high stature; and his top was among the thick boughs.

## shrubs → plants — Genesis 21:15
*(from batch_0089_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "plants" is attested (8 verses) and preserves the sense of desert vegetation.
- NEW: And the water was spent in the bottle, and she cast the child under one of the plants.

## shun → stand — II Timothy 2:16
*(from batch_0089_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR is broken ("stand profane and vain babblings" is ungrammatical, dropping the required verb). Wycliffe and ASV both read "shun" itself, but the word must go under rule 3; Darby's own construction supplies "shun" governing the noun phrase directly, so the attested KJV verb "avoid" (used at Romans 16:17, 2 Timothy 2:23 for turning from wrongdoing) is substituted, matching BSB's "avoid" for this very verse.
- NEW: But avoid profane and vain babblings: for they will increase unto more ungodliness.

## shunned → feared — Acts 20:27
*(from batch_0089_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "feared" narrows the sense from "held back/kept back" to an emotion, weakening Paul's claim of full disclosure. Tyndale and Geneva both render this clause "have kept nothing back, but have shewed you"; "kept back" as a phrase (with "keep back" attested, e.g. Joshua 22:19, Acts 5:2) restores the sense of withholding precisely.
- NEW: For I have not kept back to declare unto you all the counsel of God.

## sickly → feeble — I Corinthians 11:30
*(from batch_0089_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "feeble" is attested in KJV (25 verses, e.g. 1 Thessalonians 5:14) and matches BBE's own rendering "feeble and ill" for this verse, preserving the sense of bodily weakness paired with "weak."
- NEW: For this cause many are weak and feeble among you, and many sleep.

## sieve → coast — Isaiah 30:28
*(from batch_0089_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "coast" (a shoreline/border) has no relation to sifting grain and breaks the agricultural image entirely. Darby and ASV both render this "sieve of destruction"; "destruction" is common in KJV (44 verses) and supplies a fitting noun for the governing phrase "sieve of ___" while the rare headword itself is replaced per rule 3 with the same noun used at the parallel verse Amos 9:9's "riddle"/"sieve" imagery — see that entry for the matching fix to the vessel-word itself.
- NEW: And his breath, as an overflowing stream, shall reach to the midst of the neck, to sift the nations with the riddle of destruction: and there shall be a bridle in the jaws of the people, causing them to err.

## sieve → coast — Amos 9:9
*(from batch_0089_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: same problem as Isaiah 30:28 — "coast" does not fit sifting grain. Wycliffe renders the implement "riddil" (a coarse sieve), an authentic period word; "riddle" in this sense is not otherwise used in KJV.db for a different meaning within this corpus and directly matches the Wycliffe witness, so it is adopted consistently with the Isaiah 30:28 fix above.
- NEW: For, lo, I will command, and I will sift the house of Israel among all nations, like as corn is sifted in a riddle, yet shall not the least grain fall upon the earth.

## signification → dumb — I Corinthians 14:10
*(from batch_0090_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's construction is broken ("without dumb" is ungrammatical). Geneva reads "none of them is dumme" directly (without "without"); this construction is well-attested and preserves the meaning through equivalent double-negation.
- NEW: There are, it may be, so many kinds of voices in the world, and none of them is dumb.

## signifieth → sheweth — Hebrews 12:27
*(from batch_0090_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "sheweth" is common throughout KJV and preserves the sense of indicating/making evident.
- NEW: And this word, Yet once more, sheweth the removing of those things that are shaken, as of things that are made, that those things which cannot be shaken may remain.

## silversmith → coppersmith — Acts 19:24
*(from batch_0090_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "coppersmith" contradicts the verse's own statement that Demetrius "made silver shrines" — a plain internal inconsistency. "smith" is attested in KJV (16 verses, e.g. Isaiah 44:12, 54:16), is a genuinely different word from "silversmith," and does not duplicate "craftsmen" already present later in the verse. (Combine with the "shrines → temples" fix in batch 0089 for the full verse.)
- NEW: For a certain man named Demetrius, a smith, which made silver temples for Diana, brought no small gain unto the craftsmen;

## sincere → pure — Philippians 1:10
*(from batch_0090_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "pure" matches YLT exactly and is common in KJV (100+ verses).
- NEW: That ye may approve things that are excellent; that ye may be pure and without offence till the day of Christ;

## sincere → pure — I Peter 2:2
*(from batch_0090_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: matches Webster exactly.
- NEW: As newborn babes, desire the pure milk of the word, that ye may grow thereby:

## singer → sing — I Chronicles 6:33
*(from batch_0090_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR turns the noun into a bare verb ("Heman a sing" is ungrammatical). "musician" is well attested in KJV (55 verses, especially in the Psalm superscriptions' "chief Musician") and is the correct part of speech.
- NEW: And these are they that waited with their children. Of the sons of the Kohathites: Heman a musician, the son of Joel, the son of Shemuel,

## singer → sing — Habakkuk 3:19
*(from batch_0090_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: same grammar problem as I Chronicles 6:33. "To the chief musician" is in fact the KJV's own recurring idiom (used throughout the Psalm titles), making this an especially well-attested restoration.
- NEW: The Lord God is my strength, and he will make my feet like hinds' feet, and he will make me to walk upon mine high places. To the chief musician on my stringed instruments.

## single → sound — Matthew 6:22
*(from batch_0090_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "sound" is attested and fits the idiom of a healthy, undivided eye; ACV and RWebster use similar language ("sound"/"clear").
- NEW: The light of the body is the eye: if therefore thine eye be sound, thy whole body shall be full of light.

## single → sound — Luke 11:34
*(from batch_0090_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: consistent with Matthew 6:22.
- NEW: The light of the body is the eye: therefore when thine eye is sound, thy whole body also is full of light; but when thine eye is evil, thy body also is full of darkness.

## singular → wonderful — Leviticus 27:2
*(from batch_0090_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: matches YLT exactly; "wonderful" is common in KJV and carries the archaic sense of "remarkable/extraordinary," fitting a vow of unusual weight.
- NEW: Speak unto the children of Israel, and say unto them, When a man shall make a wonderful vow, the persons shall be for the Lord by thy estimation.

## sith → since — Ezekiel 35:6
*(from batch_0090_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "since" is extremely common in KJV and matches Webster and RWebster exactly.
- NEW: Therefore, as I live, saith the Lord God, I will prepare thee unto blood, and blood shall pursue thee: since thou hast not hated blood, even blood shall pursue thee.

## situation → seat — II Kings 2:19
*(from batch_0090_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "seat" is attested (54 verses) in the archaic Early Modern sense of "site/location" (as in "royal seat," "seat of government"), a legitimate period usage rather than the modern furniture sense.
- NEW: And the men of the city said unto Elisha, Behold, I pray thee, the seat of this city is pleasant, as my lord seeth: but the water is naught, and the ground barren.

## situation → seat — Psalms 48:2
*(from batch_0090_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: consistent with II Kings 2:19.
- NEW: Beautiful for seat, the joy of the whole earth, is mount Zion, on the sides of the north, the city of the great King.

## sixscore → twelve — I Kings 9:14
*(from batch_0090_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "twelve" is a tenfold numeric error (sixscore = 120, not 12) — a clear meaning inversion of the amount of gold sent. "an hundred and twenty" matches Webster and RWebster exactly and preserves the correct figure.
- NEW: And Hiram sent to the king an hundred and twenty talents of gold.

## sixscore → twelve — Jonah 4:11
*(from batch_0090_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: same tenfold numeric error as I Kings 9:14 (120,000 persons corrupted to 12,000); "an hundred and twenty thousand" matches DRC, Webster, and Darby.
- NEW: And should not I spare Nineveh, that great city, wherein are more than an hundred and twenty thousand persons that cannot discern between their right hand and their left hand; and also much cattle?

## sixtyfold → threescore — Matthew 13:8
*(from batch_0090_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "threescore" correctly preserves the number sixty (it is simply the period-English cardinal, common in KJV, 96 verses) even though it drops the "-fold" suffix present on the neighboring terms; this does not invert or corrupt the meaning.
- NEW: But other fell into good ground, and brought forth fruit, some an hundredfold, some threescore, some thirtyfold.

## skilfully → diligently — Psalms 33:3
*(from batch_0090_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "diligently" is common in KJV (39 verses) and, while not an exact synonym, preserves the sense of careful, deliberate playing rather than inverting it.
- NEW: Sing unto him a new song; play diligently with a loud noise.

## skilfulness → wisdom — Psalms 78:72
*(from batch_0090_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "wisdom" is common and fits the sense of guiding with skilled understanding; Geneva's own rendering uses "discretion," a near-synonym.
- NEW: So he fed them according to the integrity of his heart; and guided them by the wisdom of his hands.

## skip → dance — Psalms 29:6
*(from batch_0090_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "dance" is attested (8 verses) and preserves the leaping motion of the simile.
- NEW: He maketh them also to dance like a calf; Lebanon and Sirion like a young unicorn.

## skipped → leaped — Psalms 114:4
*(from batch_0090_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "leaped" is attested (8 verses) and matches the sense of the parallel witnesses (Geneva, DRC).
- NEW: The mountains leaped like rams, and the little hills like lambs.

## skipped → leaped — Psalms 114:6
*(from batch_0090_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: consistent with 114:4.
- NEW: Ye mountains, that ye leaped like rams; and ye little hills, like lambs?

## skippedst → bemoan — Jeremiah 48:27
*(from batch_0090_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR is nonsensical ("thou bemoan for joy" does not parse — bemoaning is grief, not joy). Webster and RWebster read "thou hast leaped for joy"/"thou didst leap for joy"; using the auxiliary "didst" with the attested bare verb "leap" avoids re-inflecting the flagged word while preserving the sense.
- NEW: For was not Israel a derision unto thee? was he found among thieves? for since thou spakest of him, thou didst leap for joy.

## skipping → leaping — Song of Solomon 2:8
*(from batch_0090_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR duplicates "leaping," which already appears earlier in the same verse ("leaping upon the mountains"). "dancing" is attested (8 verses) and supplies the needed distinct word for the second clause.
- NEW: The voice of my beloved! behold, he cometh leaping upon the mountains, dancing upon the hills.

## slackness → slothfulness — II Peter 3:9
*(from batch_0090_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "slothfulness" is itself KJV-rare (2 occurrences, both independently flagged as rare words elsewhere in this project) — curing one rare word with another rare word is forbidden. "idleness" is attested (3 verses) and is a genuinely different, sufficiently common word.
- NEW: The Lord is not slack concerning his promise, as some men count idleness; but is longsuffering to us-ward, not willing that any should perish, but that all should come to repentance.

## slanderers → accusers — I Timothy 3:11
*(from batch_0091_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "accusers" is attested in KJV (9 verses, cf. YLT "false accusers") and is a genuinely different word fitting the sense of malicious speech.
- NEW: Even so must their wives be grave, not accusers, sober, faithful in all things.

## slanderously → falsely — Romans 3:8
*(from batch_0091_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "falsely" matches BBE and is common in KJV (23 verses), fitting the sense of a false report.
- NEW: And not rather, (as we be falsely reported, and as some affirm that we say,) Let us do evil, that good may come? whose damnation is just.

## slang → sling — I Samuel 17:49
*(from batch_0091_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "sling" is the wrong tense (present-tense verb dropped into a past-tense narrative: "took...and sling it and smote" is grammatically broken). "slung" does not occur in the KJV corpus, so "cast" (extremely common, 497 verses) is used instead, matching the sense of hurling the stone and preserving correct tense throughout.
- NEW: And David put his hand in his bag, and took thence a stone, and cast it, and smote the Philistine in his forehead, that the stone sunk into his forehead; and he fell upon his face to the earth.

## slave → bondman — Jeremiah 2:14
*(from batch_0091_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "bondman" matches Darby exactly and is attested elsewhere in KJV (Leviticus 25, etc.).
- NEW: Is Israel a servant? is he a homeborn bondman? why is he spoiled?

## slaves → bodies — Revelation 18:13
*(from batch_0091_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "bodies" matches RWebster, YLT, and Darby exactly.
- NEW: And cinnamon, and odours, and ointments, and frankincense, and wine, and oil, and fine flour, and wheat, and beasts, and sheep, and horses, and chariots, and bodies, and souls of men.

## sleeper → sleep — Jonah 1:6
*(from batch_0091_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR turns the noun into the wrong part of speech ("O sleep" as vocative address is ungrammatical). Rephrasing as a verb clause avoids reintroducing "sleeper" while using the well-attested verb "sleepest" (4 verses).
- NEW: So the shipmaster came to him, and said unto him, What meanest thou, that thou sleepest? arise, call upon thy God, if so be that God will think upon us, that we perish not.

## sleight → subtlety — Ephesians 4:14
*(from batch_0091_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "subtlety" does not occur anywhere in the KJV corpus (0 occurrences), failing the "must not be KJV-rare" rule. "guile" is attested (11 verses, e.g. 1 Peter 2:1) and is a genuinely different word fitting the sense of crafty deception, and does not duplicate "cunning craftiness" already present in the verse.
- NEW: That we henceforth be no more children, tossed to and fro, and carried about with every wind of doctrine, by the guile of men, and cunning craftiness, whereby they lie in wait to deceive;

## slidden → turned — Jeremiah 8:5
*(from batch_0091_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "turned" matches YLT exactly and is extremely common in KJV (291 verses).
- NEW: Why then is this people of Jerusalem turned back by a perpetual backsliding? they hold fast deceit, they refuse to return.

## slightly → lightly — Jeremiah 6:14
*(from batch_0091_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "lightly" matches Darby and ACV exactly.
- NEW: They have healed also the hurt of the daughter of my people lightly, saying, Peace, peace; when there is no peace.

## slightly → lightly — Jeremiah 8:11
*(from batch_0091_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: consistent with Jeremiah 6:14.
- NEW: For they have healed the hurt of the daughter of my people lightly, saying, Peace, peace; when there is no peace.

## slime → pitch — Genesis 11:3
*(from batch_0091_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "pitch" is attested (16 verses) and is a reasonable period synonym for a bituminous building substance; this verse does not otherwise mention pitch, so there is no duplication.
- NEW: And they said one to another, Go to, let us make brick, and burn them throughly. And they had brick for stone, and pitch had they for morter.

## slime → clay — Exodus 2:3
*(from batch_0091_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "clay" is attested (30 verses) and, unlike "pitch," does not duplicate the "pitch" that already appears later in this same verse.
- NEW: And when she could not longer hide him, she took for him an ark of bulrushes, and daubed it with clay and with pitch, and put the child therein; and she laid it in the flags by the river's brink.

## slingers → archers — II Kings 3:25
*(from batch_0091_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "archers" is attested and fits the sense of men attacking with slings/missiles in a siege context.
- NEW: And they beat down the cities, and on every good piece of land cast every man his stone, and filled it; and they stopped all the wells of water, and felled all the good trees: only in Kir-haraseth left they the stones thereof; howbeit the archers went about it, and smote it.

## slingstones → stones — Job 41:28
*(from batch_0091_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "stones" is extremely common in KJV and matches YLT's own phrase "stones of the sling."
- NEW: The arrow cannot make him flee: stones are turned with him into stubble.

## slothfulness → idleness — Proverbs 19:15
*(from batch_0091_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR ("Idleness casteth...") duplicates the root of "idle soul" later in the same verse. "sluggard" is attested (6 verses, e.g. Proverbs 6:6, 6:9) and, rephrased as the subject of the clause, avoids the duplication while preserving the Hebrew's personifying sense of laziness.
- NEW: The sluggard falleth into a deep sleep; and an idle soul shall suffer hunger.

## slothfulness → slackness — Ecclesiastes 10:18
*(from batch_0091_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: this is a circular rare-for-rare swap (slothfulness: 2 occurrences; slackness: 1 occurrence — each independently flagged elsewhere in this project). Rephrasing with "slack" as an adjective describing the hands avoids both the rare noun and duplicating "idleness" in the same verse, and mirrors the Hebrew's own synonymous parallelism (Wycliffe/DRC: "feebleness of hands").
- NEW: Because the hands are slack the building decayeth; and through idleness of the hands the house droppeth through.

## slowly → hardly — Acts 27:7
*(from batch_0091_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "hardly" matches YLT, Darby, and ASV exactly ("with difficulty").
- NEW: And when we had sailed hardly many days, and scarce were come over against Cnidus, the wind not suffering us, we sailed under Crete, over against Salmone;

## sluices → pools — Isaiah 19:10
*(from batch_0091_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "pools" is attested (5 verses) and is a genuinely different word from the adjacent "ponds," so there is no exact duplication.
- NEW: And they shall be broken in the purposes thereof, all that make pools and ponds for fish.

## slumberings → dreams — Job 33:15
*(from batch_0091_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR duplicates "a dream," which already opens the same verse. Rephrasing the final clause as "as they rest upon the bed" avoids reusing any word already present (dream, vision, sleep) while preserving the sense of repose.
- NEW: In a dream, in a vision of the night, when deep sleep falleth upon men, as they rest upon the bed;

## smart → evil — Proverbs 11:15
*(from batch_0091_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR is broken grammar ("shall evil for it" is not a verb phrase). "suffer" is common in KJV and fits DRC's own rendering ("shall be afflicted with evil").
- NEW: He that is surety for a stranger shall suffer for it: and he that hateth suretiship is sure.

## smiters → murderers — Isaiah 50:6
*(from batch_0091_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "murderers" overstates the violence — the servant continues speaking afterward, describing ongoing shame and spitting, not death; "murderers" implies a fatal outcome inconsistent with the narrative. Rephrasing as a relative clause with the attested verb "beat" (common in KJV) preserves the sense of a severe but non-fatal beating without merely re-inflecting "smiters."
- NEW: I gave my back to them that beat me, and my cheeks to them that plucked off the hair: I hid not my face from shame and spitting.

## smoother → sweeter — Psalms 55:21
*(from batch_0092_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Verse carries two flagged words (smoother, softer) needing distinct fixes. YLT renders the first clause "Sweeter than honey hath been his mouth," giving a period-plausible, KJV-attested (3x) comparative distinct from "smoother." "Softer" is fixed separately below to avoid duplication.
- NEW: The words of his mouth were sweeter than butter, but war was in his heart: his words were more gentle than oil, yet were they drawn swords.

## smoother → soft — Proverbs 5:3
*(from batch_0092_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Geneva1599 reads "her mouth is more soft then oyle" — a genuinely different, KJV-attested word (soft, 7x) in a period-witnessed "more X than" construction.
- NEW: For the lips of a strange woman drop as an honeycomb, and her mouth is more soft than oil:

## snail → worm — Leviticus 11:30
*(from batch_0092_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Witnesses disagree wildly on this creature's identity (rat, shrew, stellio, gecko), none giving a KJV-attested alternative word; "worm" (15x in KJV) is a defensible generic term for an unclean creeping thing and no safer swap exists.
- NEW: And the ferret, and the chameleon, and the lizard, and the worm, and the mole.

## snail → worm — Psalms 58:8
*(from batch_0092_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same creature-identity problem as Leviticus 11:30; no witness supplies a KJV-attested alternate noun for the melting/dissolving image, so the existing "worm" (15x) stands as the least-risk choice.
- NEW: As a worm which melteth, let every one of them pass away: like the untimely birth of a woman, that they may not see the sun.

## snatch → cut — Isaiah 9:20
*(from batch_0092_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: YLT ("cutteth down") and BBE ("cutting off bits") both support "cut" for this sense of tearing/carving food in famine; "cut" is common in KJV (327x).
- NEW: And he shall cut on the right hand, and be hungry; and he shall eat on the left hand, and they shall not be satisfied: they shall eat every man the flesh of his own arm:

## sneezed → gaped — II Kings 4:35
*(from batch_0092_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: DRC reads "the child gaped seven times," a genuine period-witnessed alternative; "gaped" is attested in KJV (Job 16:10, Ps 22:13), correcting the prior agent's "no safe swap" finding.
- NEW: Then he returned, and walked in the house to and fro; and went up, and stretched himself upon him: and the child gaped seven times, and the child opened his eyes.

## snorting → gnashing — Jeremiah 8:16
*(from batch_0092_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Neighing" already occurs later in this verse, so reusing it duplicates a word. Wycliffe's "Gnastyng of horsis" supplies a genuinely different, KJV-attested word (gnashing, 8x, cf. "gnashing of teeth").
- NEW: The gnashing of his horses was heard from Dan: the whole land trembled at the sound of the neighing of his strong ones; for they are come, and have devoured the land, and all that is in it; the city, and those that dwell therein.

## snout → nose — Proverbs 11:22
*(from batch_0092_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: YLT and BBE both render "nose of a sow/pig"; "nose" is KJV-attested (14x) and CUR's "anger" made nonsense of the verse.
- NEW: As a jewel of gold in a swine's nose, so is a fair woman which is without discretion.

## snowy → winter — I Chronicles 11:22
*(from batch_0092_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR merely de-adjectived "snowy" to "snow" (disguised re-inflection of the same lemma, disallowed). "Winter" is a genuinely different, well-attested KJV word (15x, e.g. John 10:22) naming the same cold season the witnesses describe as "time of snow."
- NEW: Benaiah the son of Jehoiada, the son of a valiant man of Kabzeel, who had done many acts; he slew two lionlike men of Moab: also he went down and slew a lion in a pit in the time of winter.

## snuffed → drew — Jeremiah 14:6
*(from batch_0092_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Geneva1599 reads "drew in their winde like dragons" — a genuinely different, common KJV word ("drew") in the period witness's own construction, replacing CUR's thin "breathed" (4x).
- NEW: And the wild asses did stand in the high places, they drew in the wind like dragons; their eyes did fail, because there was no grass.

## snuffed → puffed — Malachi 1:13
*(from batch_0092_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: YLT and Darby both render "ye have puffed at it" exactly matching CUR; "puffed" is attested (6x) in KJV.
- NEW: Ye said also, Behold, what a weariness is it! and ye have puffed at it, saith the Lord of hosts; and ye brought that which was torn, and the lame, and the sick; thus ye brought an offering: should I accept this of your hand? saith the Lord.

## snuffeth → panteth — Jeremiah 2:24
*(from batch_0092_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: ASV/ACV render "pant for air/pants" for this sense; "panteth" is a genuinely different, KJV-attested word (3x) replacing CUR's ungrammatical "desireth up the wind."
- NEW: A wild ass used to the wilderness, that panteth up the wind at her pleasure; in her occasion who can turn her away? all they that seek her will not weary themselves; in her month they shall find her.

## soaked → drunken — Isaiah 34:7
*(from batch_0092_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Geneva1599 and ASV/ACV all render "their land shalbe drunken with blood" — a genuinely different, well-attested KJV word (drunken, 33x) with strong witness support.
- NEW: And the unicorns shall come down with them, and the bullocks with the bulls; and their land shall be drunken with blood, and their dust made fat with fatness.

## soberly → wisely — Romans 12:3
*(from batch_0092_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Darby ("so as to be wise") and YLT ("think wisely") both support "wisely," a genuinely different, well-attested KJV word (16x) fitting the sense of measured self-estimation.
- NEW: For I say, through the grace given unto me, to every man that is among you, not to think of himself more highly than he ought to think; but to think wisely, according as God hath dealt to every man the measure of faith.

## soberly → wisely — Titus 2:12
*(from batch_0092_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "rightly" duplicates the sense of "righteously" already in this verse. "Wisely" (16x, chosen consistently with Romans 12:3 above) is distinct and well-attested.
- NEW: Teaching us that, denying ungodliness and worldly lusts, we should live wisely, righteously, and godly, in this present world;

## soberness → sobriety — Acts 26:25
*(from batch_0092_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: BSB likewise renders "sobriety"; the word is a genuinely different lemma from "soberness" and attested in KJV (3x, e.g. I Timothy 2:15).
- NEW: But he said, I am not mad, most noble Festus; but speak forth the words of truth and sobriety.

## sobriety → temperance — I Timothy 2:9
*(from batch_0092_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "soberness" is barely attested (1x) in KJV; "temperance" is a genuinely different, better-attested KJV word (3x, a fruit of the Spirit at Galatians 5:23) fitting the sense of self-restraint.
- NEW: In like manner also, that women adorn themselves in modest apparel, with shamefacedness and temperance; not with broided hair, or gold, or pearls, or costly array;

## sobriety → temperance — I Timothy 2:15
*(from batch_0092_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same reasoning as I Timothy 2:9 — "temperance" (3x) is genuinely distinct and better-attested than CUR's "soberness" (1x).
- NEW: Notwithstanding she shall be saved in childbearing, if they continue in faith and charity and holiness with temperance.

## socket → base — Exodus 38:27
*(from batch_0092_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR merely re-pluralized the flagged word ("a talent for a sockets" — ungrammatical disguised re-inflection). Darby and BBE both render "a talent for a base"; "base" is genuinely distinct and KJV-attested (17x).
- NEW: And of the hundred talents of silver were cast the sockets of the sanctuary, and the sockets of the vail; an hundred sockets of the hundred talents, a talent for a base.

## sod → boiled — Genesis 25:29
*(from batch_0092_proposals.md)*

## sod → boiled — II Chronicles 35:13
*(from batch_0092_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "seethe" is merely the present-tense form of the same verb as "sod" (sod being seethe's past tense) — disguised re-inflection, disallowed. Webster, DRC, Darby, ASV, ACV, and BBE all instead render "boiled," a genuinely different lemma, attested in KJV (4x).
- NEW: And they roasted the passover with fire according to the ordinance: but the other holy offerings boiled they in pots, and in caldrons, and in pans, and divided them speedily among all the people.

## sodering → joining — Isaiah 41:7
*(from batch_0092_proposals.md)*

## sodomite → whoremonger — Deuteronomy 23:17
*(from batch_0092_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "unclean" is ungrammatical (a bare adjective standing in for a noun, with a wrong article). DRC reads "nor whoremonger among the sons of Israel" directly; "whoremonger" is attested in KJV (1x singular, 4x plural) and genuinely distinct.
- NEW: There shall be no whore of the daughters of Israel, nor a whoremonger of the sons of Israel.

## softer → gentle — Psalms 55:21
*(from batch_0092_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "faint" is nonsensical here. Geneva1599 reads "his words were more gentle then oyle" — a genuinely distinct, KJV-attested word (gentle, 5x) in the period witness's own "more X than" construction, avoiding duplication with "sweeter" chosen above for "smoother" in the same verse.

## soil → field — Ezekiel 17:8

- verdict: REVISE

## solace → delight — Proverbs 7:18
*(from batch_0092_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "rejoice ourselves" is ungrammatical (rejoice does not take a reflexive object in KJV usage). Darby reads "let us delight ourselves with loves," matching the common KJV idiom "delight ourselves" (cf. Isaiah 55:2, 58:14); "delight" is well-attested (51x).
- NEW: Come, let us take our fill of love until the morning: let us delight ourselves with loves.

## solemnity → set time — Deuteronomy 31:10

## solemnity → feast — Isaiah 30:29
*(from batch_0092_proposals.md)*

- NEW: Ye shall have a song, as in the night when a holy feast is kept; and gladness of heart, as when one goeth with a pipe to come into the mountain of the Lord, to the mighty One of Israel.

## solemnly → straitly — Genesis 43:3
*(from batch_0092_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Straitly" is the KJV's own idiom for a firm charge or protest (cf. Mark 5:43, Acts 4:17) and is well-attested (12x); it is a genuinely different word than CUR's vaguer "earnestly," and captures the emphatic force the witnesses (Geneva "charged...by an othe," DRC "attestation of an oath") describe.
- NEW: And Judah spake unto him, saying, The man did straitly protest unto us, saying, Ye shall not see my face, except your brother be with you.

## solemnly → straitly — I Samuel 8:9
*(from batch_0092_proposals.md)*

## solitarily → alone — Micah 7:14
*(from batch_0092_proposals.md)*

## somebody → some man — Luke 8:46

- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "man" wrongly asserts the toucher was male (it was the woman with the issue of blood), inverting a fact of the narrative. Wycliffe's "Summan" ("some man," used generically for "someone" in period English, as in "if any man") supplies a genuinely distinct, KJV-attested phrase (4x) that does not assert gender the way bare "man" would.
- NEW: And Jesus said, Some man hath touched me: for I perceive that virtue is gone out of me.

*(from batch_0093_proposals.md)*

*(from batch_0094_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "change" (matching YLT) is vague to the point of obscuring the tree's regrowth. DRC ("groweth green again") and BBE ("come to life again") both point to a coming-back-to-life sense; "revive" is genuinely distinct and attested in KJV (7x).
- NEW: For there is hope of a tree, if it be cut down, that it will revive again, and that the tender branch thereof will not cease.

*(from batch_0094_proposals.md)*

## spun → wrought — Exodus 35:26
*(from batch_0094_proposals.md)*

- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "stripped" is a mere re-inflection of the flagged word (disguised same-lemma swap), which the rules forbid. "Spoiled" is a genuinely different, well-attested KJV word for stripping/plundering someone of clothing or goods.
- NEW: And it came to pass, when Joseph was come unto his brethren, that they spoiled Joseph of his coat, his coat of many colours that was on him;

## striven → contended — Jeremiah 50:24
*(from batch_0097_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR breaks the auxiliary ("thou hast meddle"). Darby renders "contended with Jehovah"; "contended" is common in the KJV corpus and the auxiliary "hast" now agrees.
- NEW: I have laid a snare for thee, and thou art also taken, O Babylon, and thou wast not aware: thou art found, and also caught, because thou hast contended against the Lord.

## strongly → firmly — Ezra 6:3
*(from batch_0097_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "firmly" is a genuinely different word attested in period witnesses' sense (BSB "firmly laid") and common enough in English; preserves the sense of a sturdy foundation.
- NEW: In the first year of Cyrus the king the same Cyrus the king made a decree concerning the house of God at Jerusalem, Let the house be builded, the place where they offered sacrifices, and let the foundations thereof be firmly laid; the height thereof threescore cubits, and the breadth thereof threescore cubits;

## strowed → scattered — II Chronicles 34:4
*(from batch_0097_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "scattered" matches UKJV/RWebster exactly and is common in the KJV corpus.
- NEW: And they brake down the altars of Baalim in his presence; and the images, that were on high above them, he cut down; and the groves, and the carved images, and the molten images, he brake in pieces, and made dust of them, and scattered it upon the graves of them that had sacrificed unto them.

## struggled → strove — Genesis 25:22
*(from batch_0097_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "oppressed" inverts the meaning (children moving in the womb, not oppressing). Tyndale and Geneva both read "stroue"; "strove" is common in the KJV corpus and correctly describes the children's jostling.
- NEW: And the children strove together within her; and she said, If it be so, why am I thus? And she went to enquire of the Lord.

## stubbornness → hardness — Deuteronomy 9:27
*(from batch_0097_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "press" is meaningless. Wycliffe reads "hardnesse"; "hardness" (as in hardness of heart) is well attested in the KJV corpus and preserves the sense of obstinacy.
- NEW: Remember thy servants, Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob; look not unto the hardness of this people, nor to their wickedness, nor to their sin:

## stubbornness → hardness — I Samuel 15:23
*(from batch_0097_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same fix as Deuteronomy 9:27 for consistency; "hardness" is common and preserves the sense of obstinate rebellion parallel to iniquity and idolatry.
- NEW: For rebellion is as the sin of witchcraft, and hardness is as iniquity and idolatry. Because thou hast rejected the word of the Lord, he hath also rejected thee from being king.

## studieth → thinketh — Proverbs 15:28
*(from batch_0097_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "speaketh" duplicates the verb of the next clause's sense and changes the meaning ("heart speaketh" is odd; the heart deliberates before the mouth answers). Wycliffe reads "bithenkith" (bethinks) and YLT "meditateth"; "thinketh" is common in the KJV corpus and preserves the sense of deliberation.
- NEW: The heart of the righteous thinketh to answer: but the mouth of the wicked poureth out evil things.

## studieth → thinketh — Proverbs 24:2
*(from batch_0097_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same fix as Proverbs 15:28 for consistency and the same rationale (deliberate scheming, not mere speech).
- NEW: For their heart thinketh destruction, and their lips talk of mischief.

## studs → knops — Song of Solomon 1:11
*(from batch_0097_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "knops" is a genuine KJV word (used of the candlestick's ornamental knobs in Exodus 25 and 37), not an invented term; it preserves the sense of silver ornamental studs.
- NEW: We will make thee borders of gold with knops of silver.

## subduedst → didst humble — Nehemiah 9:24
*(from batch_0097_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR is grammatically broken ("thou himself"). DRC reads "didst humble before them the inhabitants"; "humble" is common in the KJV corpus, is a genuinely different word from "subdue," and preserves the sense of conquering the land's inhabitants.
- NEW: So the children went in and possessed the land, and thou didst humble before them the inhabitants of the land, the Canaanites, and gavest them into their hands, with their kings, and the people of the land, that they might do with them as they would.

## suborned → hired — Acts 6:11
*(from batch_0097_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "hired" is a genuinely different, common KJV word preserving the sense that these men were procured to give false testimony.
- NEW: Then they hired men, which said, We have heard him speak blasphemous words against Moses, and against God.

## subscribe → write — Isaiah 44:5
*(from batch_0097_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "write" matches YLT/Darby ("writeth with his hand") and is common in the KJV corpus.
- NEW: One shall say, I am the Lord's; and another shall call himself by the name of Jacob; and another shall write with his hand unto the Lord, and surname himself by the name of Israel.

## subscribe → write — Jeremiah 32:44
*(from batch_0097_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Same as Isaiah 44:5 — "write" is well attested and preserves the sense of recording the deed.

## subscribed → signed — Jeremiah 32:10
*(from batch_0097_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "signed" matches Geneva's "signed it" exactly.

## subvert → pervert — Lamentations 3:36
*(from batch_0097_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "pervert" is a genuinely different, attested KJV word (e.g. Proverbs 17:23 "pervert the ways of judgment") preserving the sense of wronging a man in judgment.

## subvert → pervert — Titus 1:11
*(from batch_0097_proposals.md)*

- rationale: Same as Lamentations 3:36; "pervert" preserves the sense of corrupting/undermining whole households.

## subverted → corrupted — Titus 3:11
*(from batch_0097_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "corrupted" is common in the KJV corpus and preserves the sense of being morally turned/ruined.
- NEW: Knowing that he that is such is corrupted, and sinneth, being condemned of himself.

## subverting → overthrowing — Acts 15:24
*(from batch_0097_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "overthrowing" is common in the KJV corpus and preserves the sense of unsettling/undermining the souls of the hearers.
- NEW: Forasmuch as we have heard, that certain which went out from us have troubled you with words, overthrowing your souls, saying, Ye must be circumcised, and keep the law: to whom we gave no such commandment:

## subverting → overthrowing — II Timothy 2:14
*(from batch_0097_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Same as Acts 15:24; consistent and well attested.

*(from batch_0097_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR is grammatically broken and nonsensical ("shall performed"). Darby/ASV read "shall stand in the name of his brother"; "stand" is common in the KJV corpus and correctly conveys the legal-heir sense (carrying on the dead brother's name).
- NEW: And it shall be, that the firstborn which she beareth shall stand in the name of his brother which is dead, that his name be not put out of Israel.

## succeedest → possessest — Deuteronomy 12:29
*(from batch_0098_proposals.md)*

- NEW: When the Lord thy God shall cut off the nations from before thee, whither thou goest to possess them, and thou possessest them, and dwellest in their land;

*(from batch_0098_proposals.md)*

## success → speed — Joshua 1:8
*(from batch_0098_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR duplicates "goods" nonsensically. "Speed" is a period idiom for success/prosperity attested in the KJV corpus (2 John 1:10-11 "God speed"), and preserves the sense of prospering.
- NEW: This book of the law shall not depart out of thy mouth; but thou shalt meditate therein day and night, that thou mayest observe to do according to all that is written therein: for then thou shalt make thy way prosperous, and then thou shalt have good speed.

## succourer → helper — Romans 16:2
*(from batch_0098_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "leader" inverts the servant role Paul describes. RWebster/ASV/Darby all render "helper," which is common in the KJV corpus and correctly preserves the sense that she assisted many.

*(from batch_0098_proposals.md)*

*(from batch_0098_proposals.md)*

*(from batch_0098_proposals.md)*

- NEW: Take ye heed every one of his neighbour, and trust ye not in any brother: for every brother will utterly deceive, and every neighbour will walk with slanders.

*(from batch_0098_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE

*(from batch_0098_proposals.md)*

*(from batch_0098_proposals.md)*

## support → help — I Thessalonians 5:14

## supreme → highest — I Peter 2:13
*(from batch_0098_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "as higher" is awkward and comparative where a superlative is meant. YLT reads "as the highest"; "highest" is common in the KJV corpus (Luke 2:14 "Glory to God in the highest") and reads naturally.
- NEW: Submit yourselves to every ordinance of man for the Lord's sake: whether it be to the king, as the highest;

## suretiship → pledges — Proverbs 11:15
*(from batch_0098_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "blow" is nonsensical. Since "surety" already appears earlier in this same verse and may not be duplicated, "pledges" (cf. Genesis 38:17, an attested KJV word for a surety-guarantee) is used instead to preserve the sense of hating to stand guarantee for another's debt.
- NEW: He that is surety for a stranger shall smart for it: and he that hateth pledges is sure.

## surfeiting → excess — Luke 21:34
*(from batch_0098_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "gluttony" is not well attested in the KJV corpus in this sense. "Excess" (cf. Ephesians 5:18 "wine, wherein is excess") is common and preserves the sense of overindulgence, paired here with drunkenness.
- NEW: And take heed to yourselves, lest at any time your hearts be overcharged with excess, and drunkenness, and cares of this life, and so that day come upon you unawares.

*(from batch_0098_proposals.md)*

*(from batch_0099_proposals.md)*

## sweetly → gently — Song of Solomon 7:9
*(from batch_0099_proposals.md)*

## sweetsmelling → odour of a sweet smell — Ephesians 5:2
*(from batch_0099_proposals.md)*

- NEW: And walk in love, as Christ also hath loved us, and hath given himself for us an offering and a sacrifice to God for an odour of a sweet smell.

## swerved → erred — I Timothy 1:6
*(from batch_0099_proposals.md)*

*(from batch_0099_proposals.md)*

- rationale: CUR's "bloated" is not attested in the KJV corpus and reads as a modern word. "Swelled" is attested in the KJV corpus and is a genuinely different form/word from "swollen" preserving the exact same sense.
- NEW: Howbeit they looked when he should have swelled, or fallen down dead suddenly: but after they had looked a great while, and saw no harm come to him, they changed their minds, and said that he was a god.

## swoon → faint — Lamentations 2:11
*(from batch_0099_proposals.md)*

## swooned → fainted — Lamentations 2:12
*(from batch_0099_proposals.md)*

- rationale: CUR's "overwhelmed" loses the specific sense of collapsing from want. "Fainted" is common in the KJV corpus and consistent with the companion verse (2:11), matching DRC's "fainted away."
- NEW: They say to their mothers, Where is corn and wine? when they fainted as the wounded in the streets of the city, when their soul was poured out into their mothers' bosom.

## sycamine → mulberry — Luke 17:6
*(from batch_0099_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR duplicates "tree tree" nonsensically. Tyndale, Geneva, DRC, and RWebster all render "mulberry tree," which is attested in the KJV corpus and preserves the sense of a specific rooted tree being uprooted.
- NEW: And the Lord said, If ye had faith as a grain of mustard seed, ye might say unto this mulberry tree, Be thou plucked up by the root, and be thou planted in the sea; and it should obey you.

## tabering → beating — Nahum 2:7

## taker → usurer — Isaiah 24:2
*(from batch_0099_proposals.md)*

- NEW: And it shall be, as with the people, so with the priest; as with the servant, so with his master; as with the maid, so with her mistress; as with the buyer, so with the seller; as with the lender, so with the borrower; as with the usurer, so with him that giveth to usury.

*(from batch_0099_proposals.md)*

## talkers → speakers — Titus 1:10
*(from batch_0099_proposals.md)*

## taller → stronger — Deuteronomy 1:28
*(from batch_0099_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR breaks the comparative grammar ("greater and high"). "Stronger" is common in the KJV corpus and preserves the intimidating sense of the report about the land's inhabitants.
- NEW: Whither shall we go up? our brethren have discouraged our heart, saying, The people is greater and stronger than we; the cities are great and walled up to heaven; and moreover we have seen the sons of the Anakims there.

## tame → subdue — Mark 5:4
*(from batch_0099_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE

- NEW: Because that he had been often bound with fetters and chains, and the chains had been plucked asunder by him, and the fetters broken in pieces: neither could any man subdue him.

## tame → subdue — James 3:8
*(from batch_0099_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same fix as Mark 5:4, matching YLT/ACV ("subdue") and consistent with the following verse's "tamed → subdued" resolution.
- NEW: But the tongue can no man subdue; it is an unruly evil, full of deadly poison.

## tamed → subdued — James 3:7
*(from batch_0099_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: CUR's "subdued" matches YLT/Darby exactly and is common in the KJV corpus.
- NEW: For every kind of beasts, and of birds, and of serpents, and of things in the sea, is subdued, and hath been subdued of mankind:

## tapestry → hangings — Proverbs 7:16
*(from batch_0099_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR duplicates "herself," which already appears in the verse's grammatical subject and is nonsensical here. "Hangings" is a common, attested KJV word (used of woven tabernacle coverings, e.g. Exodus 26) preserving the sense of ornamented bed coverings.
- NEW: I have decked my bed with coverings of hangings, with carved works, with fine linen of Egypt.

## tapestry → hangings — Proverbs 31:22
*(from batch_0099_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same fix as Proverbs 7:16 for consistency; "hangings" is attested and avoids duplicating "herself," which is the verse's own subject.
- NEW: She maketh herself coverings of hangings; her clothing is silk and purple.

## tarriest → standest — Acts 22:16
*(from batch_0099_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "meanest" changes the question entirely. The exact phrase "why standest" is attested in the KJV corpus, and "standest" correctly preserves the thou/-est inflection and the sense of Ananias urging Paul to delay no longer.
- NEW: And now why standest thou? arise, and be baptized, and wash away thy sins, calling on the name of the Lord.

## tarrieth → sitteth — I Samuel 30:24
*(from batch_0099_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: CUR's "sitteth" is common in the KJV corpus and preserves the sense of remaining with the baggage.
- NEW: For who will hearken unto you in this matter? but as his part is that goeth down to the battle, so shall his part be that sitteth by the stuff: they shall part alike.

## tarrieth → stayeth — Micah 5:7
*(from batch_0099_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR duplicates "waiteth," which already appears later in the same verse for a different Hebrew word. YLT renders "stayeth"; this word is attested in the KJV corpus (Isaiah 26:3) and avoids the duplication.
- NEW: And the remnant of Jacob shall be in the midst of many people as a dew from the Lord, as the showers upon the grass, that stayeth not for man, nor waiteth for the sons of men.

## task → portion — Exodus 5:14
*(from batch_0100_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: CUR's "portion" matches YLT exactly ("your portion in making brick") and is common in the KJV corpus.
- NEW: And the officers of the children of Israel, which Pharaoh's taskmasters had set over them, were beaten, and demanded, Wherefore have ye not fulfilled your portion in making brick both yesterday and to day, as heretofore?

## task → work — Exodus 5:19
*(from batch_0100_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "word" is nonsensical. Darby renders "the daily work"; "work" is extremely common in the KJV corpus and preserves the sense of the daily brick-quota.
- NEW: And the officers of the children of Israel did see that they were in evil case, after it was said, Ye shall not minish ought from your bricks of your daily work.

## tasks → work — Exodus 5:13
*(from batch_0100_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "words" is nonsensical. Darby renders "the daily work," consistent with the companion verse (5:19); "work" is common in the KJV corpus.
- NEW: And the taskmasters hasted them, saying, Fulfil your works, your daily work, as when there was straw.

## tattlers → talebearers — I Timothy 5:13
*(from batch_0100_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: CUR's "talebearers" is a genuine KJV word (Leviticus 19:16, Proverbs 11:13) and preserves the sense exactly.
- NEW: And withal they learn to be idle, wandering about from house to house; and not only idle, but talebearers also and busybodies, speaking things which they ought not.

## taunt → byword — Jeremiah 24:9
*(from batch_0100_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: CUR's "byword" matches DRC/YLT exactly and is well attested in the KJV corpus (Deuteronomy 28:37).
- NEW: And I will deliver them to be removed into all the kingdoms of the earth for their hurt, to be a reproach and a proverb, a byword and a curse, in all places whither I shall drive them.

## taunt → byword — Ezekiel 5:15
*(from batch_0100_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Same as Jeremiah 24:9 — "byword" is common and consistent.
- NEW: So it shall be a reproach and a byword, an instruction and an astonishment unto the nations that are round about thee, when I shall execute judgments in thee in anger and in fury and in furious rebukes. I the Lord have spoken it.

## taunting → mocking — Habakkuk 2:6
*(from batch_0100_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: CUR's "mocking" is common in the KJV corpus (Galatians 6:7) and preserves the sense of a derisive proverb.
- NEW: Shall not all these take up a parable against him, and a mocking proverb against him, and say, Woe to him that increaseth that which is not his! how long? and to him that ladeth himself with thick clay!

## taxation → estimation — II Kings 23:35
*(from batch_0100_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: CUR's "estimation" matches Darby exactly and preserves the sense of the assessed levy.
- NEW: And Jehoiakim gave the silver and the gold to Pharaoh; but he taxed the land to give the money according to the commandment of Pharaoh: he exacted the silver and the gold of the people of the land, of every one according to his estimation, to give it unto Pharaoh-nechoh.

## taxes → exactor — Daniel 11:20
*(from batch_0100_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "distresses" changes the sense entirely. ASV/Darby/ACV render "cause an exactor to pass through"; "exactor" is the KJV's own word (Isaiah 60:17), so this rewords the clause with an attested, genuinely different noun.
- NEW: Then shall stand up in his estate one that shall cause an exactor to pass through the glory of the kingdom: but within few days he shall be destroyed, neither in anger, nor in battle.

## taxing → numbering — Luke 2:2
*(from batch_0100_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "creeping" is nonsensical. BBE renders "numbering," and "numbering" is well attested in the KJV corpus (2 Samuel 24, Numbers), preserving the census sense.
- NEW: (And this numbering was first made when Cyrenius was governor of Syria.)

## taxing → numbering — Acts 5:37
*(from batch_0100_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same fix as Luke 2:2 for consistency; "numbering" is attested and preserves the census sense.
- NEW: After this man rose up Judas of Galilee in the days of the numbering, and drew away much people after him: he also perished; and all, even as many as obeyed him, were dispersed.

## tedious → wearisome — Acts 24:4
*(from batch_0100_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "grievous" shifts the sense toward painful/serious rather than lengthy. UKJV renders "wearisome," which is the KJV's own exact word (Job 7:3 "wearisome nights"), preserving the sense of not wishing to detain Felix long.
