- NEW: Notwithstanding, that I be not further wearisome unto thee, I pray thee that thou wouldest hear us of thy clemency a few words.

## temper → mingle — Ezekiel 46:14
*(from batch_0100_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: CUR's "mingle" matches Geneva exactly and is common in the KJV corpus.
- NEW: And thou shalt prepare a meat offering for it every morning, the sixth part of an ephah, and the third part of an hin of oil, to mingle with the fine flour; a meat offering continually by a perpetual ordinance unto the Lord.

## temporal → for a time — II Corinthians 4:18
*(from batch_0100_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR is grammatically broken ("are while"). Darby renders "for a time"; this clause rewrite replaces the flagged adjective with an attested phrase preserving the contrast with "eternal."
- NEW: While we look not at the things which are seen, but at the things which are not seen: for the things which are seen are for a time; but the things which are not seen are eternal.

## tempter → wicked one — Matthew 4:3
*(from batch_0100_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR is grammatically broken ("the tempt came"). "The wicked one" is the KJV's own standing epithet for Satan (Matthew 13:19, 1 John 2:13-14), a genuinely different and well-attested phrase preserving the reference to the devil.
- NEW: And when the wicked one came to him, he said, If thou be the Son of God, command that these stones be made bread.

## tempter → wicked one — I Thessalonians 3:5
*(from batch_0100_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same fix as Matthew 4:3 for consistency, using the KJV's attested epithet "the wicked one."
- NEW: For this cause, when I could no longer forbear, I sent to know your faith, lest by some means the wicked one have tempted you, and our labour be in vain.

## tend → lead — Proverbs 21:5
*(from batch_0100_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: CUR's "lead" is common in the KJV corpus and preserves the sense that diligence results in abundance.
- NEW: The thoughts of the diligent lead only to plenteousness; but of every one that is hasty only to want.

## tenderhearted → fainthearted — II Chronicles 13:7
*(from batch_0100_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: In this context "tenderhearted" describes young, timid Rehoboam unable to withstand rebels, not compassion; Darby's own witness rendering here is "faint-hearted," matching CUR exactly.
- NEW: And there are gathered unto him vain men, the children of Belial, and have strengthened themselves against Rehoboam the son of Solomon, when Rehoboam was young and fainthearted, and could not withstand them.

## tenderhearted → pitiful — Ephesians 4:32
*(from batch_0100_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "fainthearted" inverts the meaning here — this verse calls believers to be compassionate ("kind... tenderhearted, forgiving"), not cowardly. 1 Peter 3:8's "be pitiful, be courteous" is the KJV's own attested construction for this same virtue; "pitiful" (full of pity) is common in the KJV corpus (James 5:11) and correctly preserves the sense of compassion.
- NEW: And be ye kind one to another, pitiful, forgiving one another, even as God for Christ's sake hath forgiven you.

## tenderness → softness — Deuteronomy 28:56
*(from batch_0100_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: CUR's "softness" matches Geneva exactly ("for her softnesse and tendernesse").
- NEW: The tender and delicate woman among you, which would not adventure to set the sole of her foot upon the ground for delicateness and softness, her eye shall be evil toward the husband of her bosom, and toward her son, and toward her daughter,

## termed → called — Isaiah 62:4
*(from batch_0100_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "answered" is a wrong sense (to respond, not to be named). "Called" is extremely common in the KJV corpus and matches the parallel naming pattern of the verse ("thou shalt be called Hephzi-bah").
- NEW: Thou shalt no more be called Forsaken; neither shall thy land any more be called Desolate: but thou shalt be called Hephzi-bah, and thy land Beulah: for the Lord delighteth in thee, and thy land shall be married.

## terraces → stairs — II Chronicles 9:11
*(from batch_0100_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "courses" is an odd fit. Geneva/DRC/YLT/Darby all render "stairs"; "stairs" is common in the KJV corpus (1 Kings 6:8) and preserves the architectural sense.
- NEW: And the king made of the algum trees stairs to the house of the Lord, and to the king's palace, and harps and psalteries for singers: and there were none such seen before in the land of Judah.

## terrestrial → earthly — I Corinthians 15:40
*(from batch_0100_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR is grammatically broken ("bodies earth"). Geneva/Wycliffe/ASV render "earthly," which is extremely common in the KJV corpus (John 3:12) and correctly forms the adjective.
- NEW: There are also celestial bodies, and bodies earthly: but the glory of the celestial is one, and the glory of the earthly is another.

## terrifiest → troublest — Job 7:14
*(from batch_0100_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR drops the thou/-est inflection entirely ("thou...terrify"), a disguised re-inflection of the same word. "Troublest" is attested in the KJV corpus, keeps the correct inflection, and preserves the sense of being frightened by visions.
- NEW: Then thou scarest me with dreams, and troublest me through visions:

## testator → him that made it — Hebrews 9:16
*(from batch_0100_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "appoint" is nonsensical. ASV renders "the death of him that made it"; this rewording is attested and preserves the legal sense of the will-maker's death.
- NEW: For where a testament is, there must also of necessity be the death of him that made it.

## testator → him that made it — Hebrews 9:17
*(from batch_0100_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same fix as Hebrews 9:16 for consistency, matching ASV's construction.
- NEW: For a testament is of force after men are dead: otherwise it is of no strength at all while he that made it liveth.

## testifiedst → didst protest — Nehemiah 9:29
*(from batch_0100_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR merely drops the -st suffix ("testified"), a disguised re-inflection of the same word. Geneva renders "protestedst among them"; the exact inflection "protestedst" is unattested in the KJV corpus, so a periphrastic form is used instead — "protest"/"protested" is well attested (Genesis 43:3, 1 Samuel 8:9, Jeremiah 11:7), and "didst protest" is a genuinely different lemma from "testify," consistent with the project's precedent of pairing a changed auxiliary with a changed verb.
- NEW: And didst protest against them, that thou mightest bring them again unto thy law: yet they dealt proudly, and hearkened not unto thy commandments, but sinned against thy judgments, (which if a man do, he shall live in them;) and withdrew the shoulder, and hardened their neck, and would not hear.

## testifiedst → didst protest — Nehemiah 9:30
*(from batch_0100_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same fix as Nehemiah 9:29, consistent with Geneva's rendering ("protestedst among them by thy Spirite") and the attested form "protested" (Jeremiah 11:7).
- NEW: Yet many years didst thou forbear them, and didst protest against them by thy spirit in thy prophets: yet would they not give ear: therefore gavest thou them into the hand of the people of the lands.

## thankfulness → thanks — Acts 24:3
*(from batch_0100_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: CUR's "thanks" matches Tyndale/Geneva exactly ("with all thankes") and is common in the KJV corpus.
- NEW: We accept it always, and in all places, most noble Felix, with all thanks.

## thankworthy → grace — I Peter 2:19
*(from batch_0101_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: CUR's "grace" is Wycliffe's exact rendering ("this is grace, if...") and YLT uses "gracious"; in the period sense "grace" can mean a commendable/praiseworthy thing, preserving the sense.
- NEW: For this is grace, if a man for conscience toward God endure grief, suffering wrongfully.

## theatre → common place — Acts 19:29
*(from batch_0101_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: The King James agent flagged this as having no safe one-word swap. Geneva/Tyndale avoid the loanword entirely, rendering "the common place"; this is attested by period witnesses and is a genuinely different phrase preserving the sense of a public gathering place.
- NEW: And the whole city was filled with confusion: and having caught Gaius and Aristarchus, men of Macedonia, Paul's companions in travel, they rushed with one accord into the common place.

## theatre → common place — Acts 19:31
*(from batch_0101_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same fix as Acts 19:29 for consistency, using Geneva's "the Common place."
- NEW: And certain of the chief of Asia, which were his friends, sent unto him, desiring him that he would not adventure himself into the common place.

## thee-ward → toward thee — I Samuel 19:4
*(from batch_0101_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "sore" is nonsensical. RWebster/ASV render "toward thee"; this rewording replaces the compound with an attested, natural phrase preserving the sense of David's good conduct toward Saul.
- NEW: And Jonathan spake good of David unto Saul his father, and said unto him, Let not the king sin against his servant, against David; because he hath not sinned against thee, and because his works have been toward thee very good:

## thereabout → amazed — Luke 24:4
*(from batch_0101_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR merely drops the "there-" prefix ("about"), a disguised re-inflection of the same word. Tyndale/Geneva render "amased thereat"; "amazed" is common in the KJV corpus and is a genuinely different word preserving the women's perplexity.
- NEW: And it came to pass, as they were much amazed thereat, behold, two men stood by them in shining garments:

## thereinto → into it — Luke 21:21
*(from batch_0101_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR merely drops the "-to" suffix ("therein"), a disguised re-inflection of the same word. Wycliffe/DRC/Webster/Darby all render "enter into it"; this rewording uses an attested, genuinely different phrase.
- NEW: Then let them which are in Judea flee to the mountains; and let them which are in the midst of it depart out; and let not them that are in the countries enter into it.

## thereout → thence — Leviticus 2:2
*(from batch_0101_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: CUR's "thence" matches Geneva exactly ("he shall take thence his handfull") and is common in the KJV corpus.
- NEW: And he shall bring it to Aaron's sons the priests: and he shall take thence his handful of the flour thereof, and of the oil thereof, with all the frankincense thereof; and the priest shall burn the memorial of it upon the altar, to be an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the Lord:

## thereout → thence — Judges 15:19
*(from batch_0101_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "Thence" is common in the KJV corpus and preserves the sense of water coming out from the cleft place.
- NEW: But God clave an hollow place that was in the jaw, and there came water thence; and when he had drunk, his spirit came again, and he revived: wherefore he called the name thereof En-hakkore, which is in Lehi unto this day.

## thicket → bush — Genesis 22:13
*(from batch_0101_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR merely drops the "-et" suffix ("a thick"), a disguised re-inflection of the same word. Geneva renders "caught by the hornes in a bush"; "bush" is common in the KJV corpus (Exodus 3) and preserves the sense exactly.
- NEW: And Abraham lifted up his eyes, and looked, and behold behind him a ram caught in a bush by his horns: and Abraham went and took the ram, and offered him up for a burnt offering in the stead of his son.

## thicket → den — Jeremiah 4:7
*(from batch_0101_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR merely drops the "-et" suffix ("his thick"), a disguised re-inflection. DRC/Geneva render "his den"; "den" is common in the KJV corpus (e.g. Daniel's lions' den) and preserves the sense of the lion's lair.
- NEW: The lion is come up from his den, and the destroyer of the Gentiles is on his way; he is gone forth from his place to make thy land desolate; and thy cities shall be laid waste, without an inhabitant.

## thirtyfold → thirty — Matthew 13:8
*(from batch_0101_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's mixed forms ("hundredfold, sixtyfold, some thirty") are stylistically inconsistent within one sentence. ASV drops "-fold" after the first item consistently; matching that pattern reads more naturally and is fully attested.
- NEW: But other fell into good ground, and brought forth fruit, some an hundredfold, some sixty, some thirty.

## thirtyfold → thirty — Mark 4:20
*(from batch_0101_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: CUR's "some thirty, some sixty, and some an hundred" matches YLT/Darby/ASV/ACV exactly and is internally consistent.
- NEW: And these are they which are sown on good ground; such as hear the word, and receive it, and bring forth fruit, some thirty, some sixty, and some an hundred.

## thongs → cords — Acts 22:25
*(from batch_0101_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: CUR's "cords" matches Wycliffe exactly ("boundun hym with cordis") and is common in the KJV corpus.
- NEW: And as they bound him with cords, Paul said unto the centurion that stood by, Is it lawful for you to scourge a man that is a Roman, and uncondemned?

## thoroughly → wholly — Exodus 21:19
*(from batch_0101_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: No witness offers a distinct synonym here, but "wholly" (attested widely in the KJV corpus, e.g. Numbers 32:11-12) is a genuinely different word preserving the sense of complete healing.
- NEW: If he rise again, and walk abroad upon his staff, then shall he that smote him be quit: only he shall pay for the loss of his time, and shall cause him to be wholly healed.

## thoroughly → courageously — II Kings 11:18
*(from batch_0101_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Geneva renders "brake downe courageously"; "courageously" is attested in the KJV corpus (2 Chronicles 19:11 "deal courageously") and preserves the sense of the thorough destruction of Baal's altars.
- NEW: And all the people of the land went into the house of Baal, and brake it down; his altars and his images brake they in pieces courageously, and slew Mattan the priest of Baal before the altars. And the priest appointed officers over the house of the Lord.

## threaten → charge — Acts 4:17
*(from batch_0101_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: CUR's "charge" matches Tyndale/Geneva's pairing ("threaten and charge") and is common in the KJV corpus as a legal/injunction verb.
- NEW: But that it spread no further among the people, let us straitly charge them, that they speak henceforth to no man in this name.

## threatened → charged — Acts 4:21
*(from batch_0101_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: CUR's "charged" is attested and reasonably preserves the sense in this legal context (the council's formal injunction), echoing Tyndale/Geneva's "threatened and charged" pairing.
- NEW: So when they had further charged them, they let them go, finding nothing how they might punish them, because of the people: for all men glorified God for that which was done.

## threatened → charged — I Peter 2:23
*(from batch_0101_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "Charged" is common and attested; while it loses some of the specific retaliatory-threat nuance of the original, no witness offers a better distinct synonym, and it does not invert the sense that Christ did not respond in kind to His sufferers.
- NEW: Who, when he was reviled, reviled not again; when he suffered, he charged not; but committed himself to him that judgeth righteously:

## threatening → threats — Ephesians 6:9
*(from batch_0101_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR is a garbled non-word ("threateningeing"). BSB renders "threats," which is attested in the KJV corpus (Acts 4:29, 9:1 use the related plural "threatenings") and preserves the sense of masters giving up intimidation of servants.
- NEW: And, ye masters, do the same things unto them, forbearing threats: knowing that your Master also is in heaven; neither is there respect of persons with him.

## threatenings → threats — Acts 4:29
*(from batch_0101_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "revilings" wrongly casts the apostles as the ones insulting, when they are describing threats made against them. "Threats" (attested in the KJV corpus, cf. ACV's "threats") is a genuinely different noun form preserving the exact sense.
- NEW: And now, Lord, behold their threats: and grant unto thy servants, that with all boldness they may speak thy word,

## threatenings → threats — Acts 9:1
*(from batch_0101_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "revilings" understates Saul's violent intent (breathing out murderous threats, not mere insults). "Threats" is attested and preserves the sense, consistent with Acts 4:29.
- NEW: And Saul, yet breathing out threats and slaughter against the disciples of the Lord, went unto the high priest,

## threshingplace → threshingfloor — II Samuel 24:16
*(from batch_0101_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: CUR's "threshingfloor" matches DRC/Darby/ASV/YLT exactly and is the KJV's own standard term elsewhere (e.g. Ruth 3).
- NEW: And when the angel stretched out his hand upon Jerusalem to destroy it, the Lord repented him of the evil, and said to the angel that destroyed the people, It is enough: stay now thine hand. And the angel of the Lord was by the threshingfloor of Araunah the Jebusite.

## throng → press — Mark 3:9
*(from batch_0101_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "narrow" is nonsensical. YLT renders "press upon him"; "press" is very common in the KJV corpus and preserves the sense of the crowd pressing in on him.
- NEW: And he spake to his disciples, that a small ship should wait on him because of the multitude, lest they should press him.

## throng → crush — Luke 8:45
*(from batch_0101_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "narrow" is nonsensical, and "press" cannot be used here since it already appears later in the same verse ("and press thee"). ASV renders "crush thee"; "crush" is attested in the KJV corpus and is a genuinely different word avoiding the duplication.
- NEW: And Jesus said, Who touched me? When all denied, Peter and they that were with him said, Master, the multitude crush thee and press thee, and sayest thou, Who touched me?

## thronged → choked — Mark 5:24
*(from batch_0102_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "choked" inverts meaning (strangled vs. crowded around). KJV itself uses "press" for this exact scene at Luke 8:45 ("the multitudes throng thee and press thee"); Darby/BSB likewise render "pressed" here.
- NEW: And Jesus went with him; and much people followed him, and pressed him.

## thronged → choked — Luke 8:42
*(from batch_0102_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same fix as Mark 5:24; "pressed" matches the parallel KJV construction at Luke 8:45 and avoids the meaning-inverting "choked."
- NEW: For he had one only daughter, about twelve years of age, and she lay a dying. But as he went the people pressed him.

## thronging → pressing — Mark 5:31
*(from batch_0102_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "pressing" is grammatical, matches Darby ("pressing on thee") and BSB ("pressing in on You"), and is the natural companion to the Mark 5:24/Luke 8:42 fix above (cf. KJV Luke 8:45 "throng...and press").
- NEW: And his disciples said unto him, Thou seest the multitude pressing thee, and sayest thou, Who touched me?

## thunderbolts → coals — Psalms 78:48
*(from batch_0102_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "coals" changes the referent from a sky-fire (lightning) to hearth-coals. "Lightnings" is well-attested in KJV (Exodus 19:16, Psalm 135:7, Nahum 2:4) and matches BSB "bolts of lightning" / YLT "burning flames."
- NEW: He gave up their cattle also to the hail, and their flocks to hot lightnings.

## tie → bound — I Samuel 6:7
*(from batch_0102_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "bound" breaks the imperative series (take...tie...bring). "Fasten" preserves the imperative and avoids duplicating "yoke" already in the verse.
- NEW: Now therefore make a new cart, and take two milch kine, on which there hath come no yoke, and fasten the kine to the cart, and bring their calves home from them:

## tie → bound — Proverbs 6:21
*(from batch_0102_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same fix as I Samuel 6:7 for consistency; avoids duplicating "Bind" already used at the start of the verse.
- NEW: Bind them continually upon thine heart, and fasten them about thy neck.

## tile → brick — Ezekiel 4:1
*(from batch_0102_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "brick" is well-attested in KJV (Genesis 11:3, Exodus 1:14) and matches YLT, Geneva1599, Darby, and BSB.
- NEW: Thou also, son of man, take thee a brick, and lay it before thee, and pourtray upon it the city, even Jerusalem:

## tiling → roof — Luke 5:19
*(from batch_0102_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "roof" is distinct from "housetop" earlier in the verse (no duplication) and matches DRC/BBE.
- NEW: And when they could not find by what way they might bring him in because of the multitude, they went upon the housetop, and let him down through the roof with his couch into the midst before Jesus.

## tiller → serve — Genesis 4:2
*(from batch_0102_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "serve" is the wrong part of speech (verb for a noun slot). "Husbandman" is KJV-attested (Genesis 9:20 "an husbandman," 2 Timothy 2:6, James 5:7), matches DRC/Darby, and preserves the KJV convention "an husbandman."
- NEW: And she again bare his brother Abel. And Abel was a keeper of sheep, but Cain was an husbandman of the ground.

## tire → beauty — Ezekiel 24:17
*(from batch_0102_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "beauty" turns a headdress into an abstraction. "Bonnet" is the KJV's own word for this kind of headdress (Exodus 28:40, Leviticus 8:13) and matches YLT here.
- NEW: Forbear to cry, make no mourning for the dead, bind the bonnet of thine head upon thee, and put on thy shoes upon thy feet, and cover not thy lips, and eat not the bread of men.

## tired → adorned — II Kings 9:30
*(from batch_0102_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "adorned" matches Webster and DRC and is well-attested (1 Peter 3:5, Titus 2:10).
- NEW: And when Jehu was come to Jezreel, Jezebel heard of it; and she painted her face, and adorned her head, and looked out at a window.

## tires → attire — Isaiah 3:18
*(from batch_0102_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "attire" is well-attested in KJV (Jeremiah 2:32, Proverbs 7:10) and does not invert the sense of ornament being removed.
- NEW: In that day the Lord will take away the bravery of their tinkling ornaments about their feet, and their cauls, and their round attire like the moon,

## tires → attire — Ezekiel 24:23
*(from batch_0102_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: For consistency with the Ezekiel 24:17 fix (same headdress in the same book), "bonnets" matches YLT exactly and is KJV-attested (Exodus 28:40, Leviticus 8:13), sharper than the more generic "attire."
- NEW: And your bonnets shall be upon your heads, and your shoes upon your feet: ye shall not mourn nor weep; but ye shall pine away for your iniquities, and mourn one toward another.

## tittle → point — Matthew 5:18
*(from batch_0102_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "point" does not invert the sense (a minute detail of the law will not pass away); it is a well-attested KJV word and no period witness offers a genuinely different, non-circular alternative (Geneva/Tyndale's "title" is essentially the same word as "tittle").
- NEW: For verily I say unto you, Till heaven and earth pass, one jot or one point shall in no wise pass from the law, till all be fulfilled.

## tittle → point — Luke 16:17
*(from batch_0102_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same reasoning as Matthew 5:18.
- NEW: And it is easier for heaven and earth to pass, than one point of the law to fail.

## torch → lamp — Zechariah 12:6
*(from batch_0102_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "lamp" loses the firebrand-thrust-into-sheaf imagery. "Firebrand" is KJV-attested (Judges 15:4-5, Proverbs 26:18) and matches Geneva1599/DRC exactly.
- NEW: In that day will I make the governors of Judah like an hearth of fire among the wood, and like a firebrand of fire in a sheaf; and they shall devour all the people round about, on the right hand and on the left: and Jerusalem shall be inhabited again in her own place, even in Jerusalem.

## tormentors → executioners — Matthew 18:34
*(from batch_0102_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "executioners" inverts the sense — a dead debtor cannot pay. "Keepers" (i.e. jailers, cf. Tyndale "iaylers," KJV's own "keeper of the prison" idiom) is well-attested (Matthew 28:4, Acts 5:23) and preserves the meaning of imprisonment until payment.
- NEW: And his lord was wroth, and delivered him to the keepers, till he should pay all that was due unto him.

## tortoise → covered — Leviticus 11:29
*(from batch_0102_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "covered" is an adjective where a creature-noun is required. "Frog" matches Geneva1599 exactly and avoids duplicating "lizard," which the KJV itself uses in the very next verse (11:30).
- NEW: These also shall be unclean unto you among the creeping things that creep upon the earth; the weasel, and the mouse, and the frog after his kind,

## tortured → afflicted — Hebrews 11:35
*(from batch_0102_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "afflicted" is broad but does not invert the sense of suffering, and is far more KJV-common than the period witnesses' "racked."
- NEW: Women received their dead raised to life again: and others were afflicted, not accepting deliverance; that they might obtain a better resurrection:

## toss → themselves — Isaiah 22:18
*(from batch_0102_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "themselves" (a pronoun) replaces a verb, producing nonsense. "Roll" is KJV-attested (Joshua 10:18) and matches Geneva1599's "rolle and turne" and Darby/BSB.
- NEW: He will surely violently turn and roll thee like a ball into a large country: there shalt thou die, and there the chariots of thy glory shall be the shame of thy lord's house.

## toss → themselves — Jeremiah 5:22
*(from batch_0102_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR produces "themselves themselves," a broken duplicate. "Rage" matches Geneva1599 exactly ("though the waues thereof rage") and reads grammatically.
- NEW: Fear ye not me? saith the Lord: will ye not tremble at my presence, which have placed the sand for the bound of the sea by a perpetual decree, that it cannot pass it: and though the waves thereof rage, yet can they not prevail; though they roar, yet can they not pass over it?

## tossings → tossing — Job 7:4
*(from batch_0102_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR merely singularizes the flagged word (forbidden re-inflection). "Turning" is a genuinely different, KJV-attested word (James 1:17) that preserves the restless-motion sense, echoed by BSB's "toss and turn."
- NEW: When I lie down, I say, When shall I arise, and the night be gone? and I am full of turning to and fro unto the dawning of the day.

## tottering → casting — Psalms 62:3
*(from batch_0102_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "casting" is nonsensical for a fence. "Shaken" is KJV-attested (Matthew 24:29, Hebrews 12:26-27), echoes Geneva1599's rendering of this verse ("a wall shaken"), and avoids duplicating "bowing" earlier in the verse.
- NEW: How long will ye imagine mischief against a man? ye shall be slain all of you: as a bowing wall shall ye be, and as a shaken fence.

## towel → napkin — John 13:4
*(from batch_0102_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "napkin" is well-attested in KJV (John 11:44, 20:7, Luke 19:20) for a cloth of this kind.
- NEW: He riseth from supper, and laid aside his garments; and took a napkin, and girded himself.

## towel → napkin — John 13:5
*(from batch_0103_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Same reasoning as John 13:4 in batch 0102; "napkin" is well-attested KJV usage.
- NEW: After that he poureth water into a bason, and began to wash the disciples' feet, and to wipe them with the napkin wherewith he was girded.

## townclerk → scribe — Acts 19:35
*(from batch_0103_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Wycliffe attests "scribe" for this exact verse ("the scribe hadde ceessid the puple"), giving direct period justification despite the modern association of "scribe" with Jewish teachers of the law.
- NEW: And when the scribe had appeased the people, he said, Ye men of Ephesus, what man is there that knoweth not how that the city of the Ephesians is a worshipper of the great goddess Diana, and of the image which fell down from Jupiter?

## traffickers → merchants — Isaiah 23:8
*(from batch_0103_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR duplicates "merchants" already in the verse. "Chapmen" is KJV-attested (2 Chronicles 9:14 "chapmen and merchants") and matches Geneva1599 exactly, avoiding the duplicate.
- NEW: Who hath taken this counsel against Tyre, the crowning city, whose merchants are princes, whose chapmen are the honourable of the earth?

## traitor → betrayer — Luke 6:16
*(from batch_0103_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: The noun "betrayer" is not attested in the KJV corpus (a new rare word). Rephrasing as a relative clause with the well-attested verb "betrayed" (rule: agent-noun → relative clause) preserves the meaning without introducing another rarity.
- NEW: And Judas the brother of James, and Judas Iscariot, which also betrayed him.

## tranquillity → quietness — Daniel 4:27
*(from batch_0103_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "quietness" is well-attested (Ecclesiastes 4:6, Isaiah 30:15, 1 Thessalonians 4:11) and matches the sense of extended peace/prosperity.
- NEW: Wherefore, O king, let my counsel be acceptable unto thee, and break off thy sins by righteousness, and thine iniquities by shewing mercy to the poor; if it may be a lengthening of thy quietness.

## transferred → transformed — I Corinthians 4:6
*(from batch_0103_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "transformed" implies a change of nature, not Paul's figurative application of the argument to himself. Geneva1599 attests "applied" directly in this very verse ("I haue figuratiuely applied vnto mine owne selfe and Apollos").
- NEW: And these things, brethren, I have in a figure applied to myself and to Apollos for your sakes; that ye might learn in us not to think of men above that which is written, that no one of you be puffed up for one against another.

## transfigured → transformed — Matthew 17:2
*(from batch_0103_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "transformed" is KJV-attested (Romans 12:2, 2 Corinthians 3:18) and does not invert the meaning.
- NEW: And was transformed before them: and his face did shine as the sun, and his raiment was white as the light.

## transfigured → transformed — Mark 9:2
*(from batch_0103_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Same as Matthew 17:2.
- NEW: And after six days Jesus taketh with him Peter, and James, and John, and leadeth them up into an high mountain apart by themselves: and he was transformed before them.

## transforming → changing — II Corinthians 11:13
*(from batch_0103_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "changing" is vague; "fashioning" (ASV) is KJV-attested (Philippians 3:21 "fashioned like unto his glorious body") and better conveys the disguise sense of the Greek.
- NEW: For such are false apostles, deceitful workers, fashioning themselves into the apostles of Christ.

## translate → cause — II Samuel 3:10
*(from batch_0103_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "cause" leaves the sentence without a verb object ("to cause the kingdom from..."). "Remove" matches Geneva1599 exactly and is grammatical.
- NEW: To remove the kingdom from the house of Saul, and to set up the throne of David over Israel and over Judah, from Dan even to Beer-sheba.

## translation → change — Hebrews 11:5
*(from batch_0103_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "change" is KJV-attested in the same sense (Job 14:14 "till my change come") and fits the euphemism for Enoch's being taken up.
- NEW: By faith Enoch was translated that he should not see death; and was not found, because God had translated him: for before his change he had this testimony, that he pleased God.

## transparent → clear — Revelation 21:21
*(from batch_0103_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "clear" is KJV-attested (Revelation 21:11, Song of Solomon 6:10) and matches BBE/BSB.
- NEW: And the twelve gates were twelve pearls; every several gate was of one pearl: and the street of the city was pure gold, as it were clear glass.

## travel → journey — Acts 19:29
*(from batch_0103_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "in journey" is missing a needed possessive/gerund. "Journeying" is KJV-attested (Numbers 10:2, 10:6) and fixes the grammar.
- NEW: And the whole city was filled with confusion: and having caught Gaius and Aristarchus, men of Macedonia, Paul's companions in journeying, they rushed with one accord into the theatre.

## travel → journey — II Corinthians 8:19
*(from batch_0103_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "to journey with us" is grammatical as a verb and "journey" is KJV-attested as both noun and verb (Genesis 35:5, Numbers 9:17); matches Geneva1599's concept.
- NEW: And not that only, but who was also chosen of the churches to journey with us with this grace, which is administered by us to the glory of the same Lord, and declaration of your ready mind:

## travelled → passed — Acts 11:19
*(from batch_0103_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "passed" is common KJV usage and preserves the sense; matches Darby's "passed through."
- NEW: Now they which were scattered abroad upon the persecution that arose about Stephen passed as far as Phenice, and Cyprus, and Antioch, preaching the word to none but unto the Jews only.

## traveller → way — II Samuel 12:4
*(from batch_0103_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "way" is nonsensical as the subject ("there came a way"). "Stranger" matches Geneva1599/DRC and avoids duplicating "wayfaring man" later in the same verse.
- NEW: And there came a stranger unto the rich man, and he spared to take of his own flock and of his own herd, to dress for the wayfaring man that was come unto him; but took the poor man's lamb, and dressed it for the man that was come to him.

## traveller → way — Job 31:32
*(from batch_0103_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "way" is nonsensical as the object of "opened my doors." "Wayfaring man" is KJV-attested (Isaiah 33:8, Judges 19:17) and distinct from "stranger" already in the verse, preserving the poetic parallelism.
- NEW: The stranger did not lodge in the street: but I opened my doors to the wayfaring man.

## travellers → needs — Judges 5:6
*(from batch_0103_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "needs" is nonsensical as the subject. "Wayfaring men" is KJV-attested and fits; see cross-batch note — this verse also has "unoccupied" flagged in batch 0105, resolved together as "forsaken... wayfaring men."
- NEW: In the days of Shamgar the son of Anath, in the days of Jael, the highways were unoccupied, and the wayfaring men walked through byways.

## travelleth → walketh — Proverbs 6:11
*(from batch_0103_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "walketh" is bland and loses the ambush imagery. Four witnesses (ASV, ACV, Darby, BSB) independently converge on "robber"/"plunderer" for the underlying sense; ASV's exact phrasing is adopted.
- NEW: So shall thy poverty come as a robber, and thy want as an armed man.

## travelleth → walketh — Proverbs 24:34
*(from batch_0103_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same fix as Proverbs 6:11 (parallel verse).
- NEW: So shall thy poverty come as a robber; and thy want as an armed man.

## traversing → winding — Jeremiah 2:23
*(from batch_0103_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "winding" matches YLT exactly and preserves the erratic-motion imagery.
- NEW: How canst thou say, I am not polluted, I have not gone after Baalim? see thy way in the valley, know what thou hast done: thou art a swift dromedary winding her ways;

## treachery → deceit — II Kings 9:23
*(from batch_0103_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "deceit" matches YLT exactly and is well-attested; not a meaning inversion.
- NEW: And Joram turned his hands, and fled, and said to Ahaziah, There is deceit, O Ahaziah.

## treader → tread — Amos 9:13
*(from batch_0103_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "tread" is the wrong part of speech in a noun slot. Rephrasing as a relative clause ("he that treadeth grapes") restores grammar while keeping the same verb-lemma, per the agent-noun-to-relative-clause rule.
- NEW: Behold, the days come, saith the Lord, that the plowman shall overtake the reaper, and he that treadeth grapes him that soweth seed; and the mountains shall drop sweet wine, and all the hills shall melt.

## treaders → archers — Isaiah 16:10
*(from batch_0103_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "archers" is a clear error (unrelated to wine treading). Dropping the agent-noun and using ASV's verb-only structure ("none shall tread out wine") preserves the sense without the flagged word.
- NEW: And gladness is taken away, and joy out of the plentiful field; and in the vineyards there shall be no singing, neither shall there be shouting: none shall tread out wine in their presses; I have made their vintage shouting to cease.

## treasurer → profitable — Ezra 1:8
*(from batch_0103_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "profitable" is the wrong part of speech. "Steward" is well-attested KJV usage (Genesis 15:2, Luke 16:1) and matches the fix used at Isaiah 22:15 below, kept consistent within the batch.
- NEW: Even those did Cyrus king of Persia bring forth by the hand of Mithredath the steward, and numbered them unto Sheshbazzar, the prince of Judah.

## treasurer → profitable — Isaiah 22:15
*(from batch_0103_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same fix as Ezra 1:8; matches YLT/Darby's "steward" for this verse directly.
- NEW: Thus saith the Lord God of hosts, Go, get thee unto this steward, even unto Shebna, which is over the house, and say,

## treatise → word — Acts 1:1
*(from batch_0103_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "word" is too minimal for a written narrative. "Story" is KJV-attested in exactly this sense (2 Chronicles 13:22, 24:27 "story of the book of the kings") and preserves the meaning of a written account.
- NEW: The former story have I made, O Theophilus, of all that Jesus began both to do and teach,

## tributary → tribute — Lamentations 1:1
*(from batch_0104_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "become tribute" is grammatically odd (noun where a predicate is needed). "Servant" conveys the same subjugation sense idiomatically and is well-attested.
- NEW: How doth the city sit solitary, that was full of people! how is she become as a widow! she that was great among the nations, and princess among the provinces, how is she become a servant!

## trickleth → poureth — Lamentations 3:49
*(from batch_0104_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "poureth" matches Darby/ASV/ACV exactly.
- NEW: Mine eye poureth down, and ceaseth not, without any intermission,

## trimmed → committed — II Samuel 19:24
*(from batch_0104_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "committed his beard" is nonsensical. The verse already uses "dressed" (feet) and "washed" (clothes), so a third distinct verb is needed: "clipped" avoids duplication and is echoed by Wycliffe's "berd vnclippid."
- NEW: And Mephibosheth the son of Saul came down to meet the king, and had neither dressed his feet, nor clipped his beard, nor washed his clothes, from the day the king departed until the day he came again in peace.

## trimmed → committed — Matthew 25:7
*(from batch_0104_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "committed their lamps" is nonsensical. "Prepared" matches Tyndale exactly and is common KJV usage.
- NEW: Then all those virgins arose, and prepared their lamps.

## trimmest → goest — Jeremiah 2:33
*(from batch_0104_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "goest" loses the sense of adorning/making ready oneself to seek love. "Preparest" matches Geneva1599's "doest thou prepare thy way" and keeps the -est inflection.
- NEW: Why preparest thou thy way to seek love? therefore hast thou also taught the wicked ones thy ways.

## troubledst → troubled — Ezekiel 32:2
*(from batch_0104_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR merely drops the -st ending (forbidden re-inflection of the same word). "Stirredst" is a genuinely different, KJV-attested lemma ("stir") in the correct second-person-singular past form, and avoids duplicating "fouledst" later in the verse.
- NEW: Son of man, take up a lamentation for Pharaoh king of Egypt, and say unto him, Thou art like a young lion of the nations, and thou art as a whale in the seas: and thou camest forth with thy rivers, and stirredst the waters with thy feet, and fouledst their rivers.

## troubler → trouble — I Chronicles 2:7
*(from batch_0104_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "the trouble of Israel" is a weak epithet. Rephrasing as a relative clause, "who troubled Israel," matches Geneva1599/DRC exactly.
- NEW: And the sons of Carmi; Achar, who troubled Israel, who transgressed in the thing accursed.

## troublous → troubled — Daniel 9:25
*(from batch_0104_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "troubled" is common KJV usage and does not invert the sense of "times of trouble."
- NEW: Know therefore and understand, that from the going forth of the commandment to restore and to build Jerusalem unto the Messiah the Prince shall be seven weeks, and threescore and two weeks: the street shall be built again, and the wall, even in troubled times.

## trough → cistern — Genesis 24:20
*(from batch_0104_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "cistern" is KJV-attested (2 Kings 18:31, Jeremiah 2:13, Proverbs 5:15) and remains a plausible water-vessel for camels to drink from; not a meaning inversion.
- NEW: And she hasted, and emptied her pitcher into the cistern, and ran again unto the well to draw water, and drew for all his camels.

## troughs → galleries — Genesis 30:38
*(from batch_0104_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "galleries" is a clear error (architectural feature, not a water vessel). "Cisterns" matches the Genesis 24:20 fix above for consistency.
- NEW: And he set the rods which he had pilled before the flocks in the gutters in the watering cisterns when the flocks came to drink, that they should conceive when they came to drink.

## troughs → galleries — Exodus 2:16
*(from batch_0104_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same fix as Genesis 30:38.
- NEW: Now the priest of Midian had seven daughters: and they came and drew water, and filled the cisterns to water their father's flock.

## trow → suppose — Luke 17:9
*(from batch_0104_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "suppose" matches Webster and is common KJV usage.
- NEW: Doth he thank that servant because he did the things that were commanded him? I suppose not.

## trucebreakers → covenantbreakers — II Timothy 3:3
*(from batch_0104_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "covenantbreakers" is itself a KJV word (Romans 1:31, same list-style vice catalogue) and is close enough in sense to "trucebreakers" without inverting meaning.
- NEW: Without natural affection, covenantbreakers, false accusers, incontinent, fierce, despisers of those that are good,

## trump → trumpet — I Corinthians 15:52
*(from batch_0104_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR creates a duplicate ("at the last trumpet: for the trumpet shall sound"). "Horn" is a distinct, attested term for the same instrument (cf. rams'-horn trumpets, Joshua 6) and avoids the repetition; note this verse also has "twinkling" flagged below — combine both fixes.
- NEW: In a moment, in the wink of an eye, at the last horn: for the trumpet shall sound, and the dead shall be raised incorruptible, and we shall be changed.

## trump → trumpet — I Thessalonians 4:16
*(from batch_0104_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: No duplicate risk here (only one occurrence); matches Geneva1599/RWebster exactly.
- NEW: For the Lord himself shall descend from heaven with a shout, with the voice of the archangel, and with the trumpet of God: and the dead in Christ shall rise first:

## trusty → stedfast — Job 12:20
*(from batch_0104_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "stedfast" matches YLT exactly and is a reasonable synonym for reliable counselors.
- NEW: He removeth away the speech of the stedfast, and taketh away the understanding of the aged.

## tumbled → turned — Judges 7:13
*(from batch_0104_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "turned into" risks being read as transformation rather than rolling motion. "Rolled" matches Webster/RWebster exactly and is well-attested (Joshua 10:18, Genesis 29:3).
- NEW: And when Gideon was come, behold, there was a man that told a dream unto his fellow, and said, Behold, I dreamed a dream, and, lo, a cake of barley bread rolled into the host of Midian, and came unto a tent, and smote it that it fell, and overturned it, that the tent lay along.

## turtle → turtledove — Song of Solomon 2:12
*(from batch_0104_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "turtledove" is well-attested (Leviticus 1:14) and avoids modern confusion with the reptile; matches RWebster/Darby/ASV.
- NEW: The flowers appear on the earth; the time of the singing of birds is come, and the voice of the turtledove is heard in our land;

## turtle → turtledove — Jeremiah 8:7
*(from batch_0104_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Same reasoning as Song of Solomon 2:12.
- NEW: Yea, the stork in the heaven knoweth her appointed times; and the turtledove and the crane and the swallow observe the time of their coming; but my people know not the judgment of the Lord.

## tutors → stewards — Galatians 4:2
*(from batch_0104_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "stewards" matches YLT exactly and is well-attested (Genesis 15:2, Luke 16:1, 1 Corinthians 4:1-2).
- NEW: But is under stewards and governors until the time appointed of the father.

## twigs → branches — Ezekiel 17:4
*(from batch_0104_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: No duplication risk in this verse; "branches" is well-attested and does not invert the sense of cropping new growth.
- NEW: He cropped off the top of his young branches, and carried it into a land of traffick; he set it in a city of merchants.

## twigs → branches — Ezekiel 17:22
*(from batch_0104_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Unlike Ezekiel 17:4, this verse already uses "highest branch" earlier — "branches" would duplicate the root word. "Boughs" is KJV-attested (Genesis 49:22, Judges 9:48) and avoids the duplication.
- NEW: Thus saith the Lord God; I will also take of the highest branch of the high cedar, and will set it; I will crop off from the top of his young boughs a tender one, and will plant it upon an high mountain and eminent:

## twinkling → wink — I Corinthians 15:52
*(from batch_0104_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "wink of an eye" is a reasonable idiom for an instant and does not invert the meaning; combine with the "horn" fix above for the full verse.
- NEW: In a moment, in the wink of an eye, at the last horn: for the trumpet shall sound, and the dead shall be raised incorruptible, and we shall be changed.

## twofold → double — Matthew 23:15
*(from batch_0104_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "double more" is an awkward but non-inverting rendering of the same degree-idiom as the original "twofold more"; "double" is extremely common KJV usage.
- NEW: Woe unto you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! for ye compass sea and land to make one proselyte, and when he is made, ye make him double more the child of hell than yourselves.

## unaccustomed → seed — Jeremiah 31:18
*(from batch_0104_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "seed to the yoke" is nonsensical. "Not taught to the yoke" matches YLT closely with the well-attested verb "taught."
- NEW: I have surely heard Ephraim bemoaning himself thus; Thou hast chastised me, and I was chastised, as a bullock not taught to the yoke: turn thou me, and I shall be turned; for thou art the Lord my God.

## unadvisedly → rashly — Psalms 106:33
*(from batch_0104_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "rashly" matches BSB and is KJV-attested (Ecclesiastes 5:2, Acts 19:36); not a meaning inversion.
- NEW: Because they provoked his spirit, so that he spake rashly with his lips.

## unblameable → blameless — Colossians 1:22
*(from batch_0104_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "blameless" is well-attested (Philippians 2:15, 3:6). Cross-batch note: this verse also has "unreproveable" flagged in batch 0106 — resolved there as "without fault" to avoid duplicating "blameless" twice in one verse.
- NEW: In the body of his flesh through death, to present you holy and blameless and without fault in his sight:

## unblameable → blameless — I Thessalonians 3:13
*(from batch_0104_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: No duplication risk in this verse; "blameless" is well-attested.
- NEW: To the end he may stablish your hearts blameless in holiness before God, even our Father, at the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ with all his saints.

## unblameably → blamelessly — I Thessalonians 2:10
*(from batch_0104_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "blamelessly" matches YLT/Darby exactly and is a natural adverbial form of the well-attested "blameless."
- NEW: Ye are witnesses, and God also, how holily and justly and blamelessly we behaved ourselves among you that believe:

## uncertain → appear — I Corinthians 14:8
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "an appear sound" is ungrammatical (verb where adjective needed). "Doubtful" is KJV-attested (Luke 12:29, Romans 14:1) and fixes both grammar and the a/an agreement.
- NEW: For if the trumpet give a doubtful sound, who shall prepare himself to the battle?

## uncertain → appear — I Timothy 6:17
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same fix as I Corinthians 14:8 for consistency.
- NEW: Charge them that are rich in this world, that they be not highminded, nor trust in doubtful riches, but in the living God, who giveth us richly all things to enjoy;

## uncertainly → vainly — I Corinthians 9:26
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "vainly" is a reasonable adverbial rendering of the aimless-running sense, echoed by ACV/BSB "aimlessly"; not a meaning inversion.
- NEW: I therefore so run, not as vainly; so fight I, not as one that beateth the air:

## unchangeable → everlasting — Hebrews 7:24
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "everlasting" matches Tyndale and DRC exactly.
- NEW: But this man, because he continueth ever, hath an everlasting priesthood.

## unclothed → stripped — II Corinthians 5:4
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "stripped" is well-attested (Genesis 37:23, Matthew 27:28) and preserves the sense of being without a body.
- NEW: For we that are in this tabernacle do groan, being burdened: not for that we would be stripped, but clothed upon, that mortality might be swallowed up of life.

## uncomely → unseemly — I Corinthians 7:36
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Using "unseemly" here is circular — "unseemly" is itself flagged as a rare word elsewhere in this batch set (Romans 1:27, I Corinthians 13:5 in batch 0106). Rephrasing with the common KJV idiom "do wrong" avoids the circularity.
- NEW: But if any man think that he doeth wrong toward his virgin, if she pass the flower of her age, and need so require, let him do what he will, he sinneth not: let them marry.

## uncomely → unseemly — I Corinthians 12:23
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same circularity problem as I Corinthians 7:36. "Unhonest" is directly attested by both Tyndale and Geneva1599 for this exact verse.
- NEW: And those members of the body, which we think to be less honourable, upon these we bestow more abundant honour; and our unhonest parts have more abundant comeliness.

## uncondemned → untried — Acts 16:37
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "untried" (not yet tried in court) is a reasonable, non-inverting rendering matching BSB's "without a trial"; no period witness offers a genuinely different single word.
- NEW: But Paul said unto them, They have beaten us openly untried, being Romans, and have cast us into prison; and now do they thrust us out privily? nay verily; but let them come themselves and fetch us out.

## uncondemned → untried — Acts 22:25
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Same reasoning as Acts 16:37.
- NEW: And as they bound him with thongs, Paul said unto the centurion that stood by, Is it lawful for you to scourge a man that is a Roman, and untried?

## uncorruptible → incorruptible — Romans 1:23
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "incorruptible" matches Geneva1599, DRC, Webster, YLT, Darby, and ASV exactly.
- NEW: And changed the glory of the incorruptible God into an image made like to corruptible man, and to birds, and fourfooted beasts, and creeping things.

## uncorruptness → incorruption — Titus 2:7
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "incorruption" shifts the sense toward bodily immortality (1 Corinthians 15) rather than moral integrity in teaching. "Integrity" is well-attested (Genesis 20:5-6, 1 Kings 9:4, Psalm 7:8) and matches RWebster, DRC, and BSB.
- NEW: In all things shewing thyself a pattern of good works: in doctrine shewing integrity, gravity, sincerity,

## unction → anointing — I John 2:20
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "anointing" matches YLT/ASV exactly and is extremely well-attested.
- NEW: But ye have an anointing from the Holy One, and ye know all things.

## undergirding → binding — Acts 27:17
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "binding" is a reasonable generalization of the technical nautical term and does not invert the sense of securing the ship with ropes.
- NEW: Which when they had taken up, they used helps, binding the ship; and, fearing lest they should fall into the quicksands, strake sail, and so were driven.

## undertake → surety — Isaiah 38:14
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "surety for me" is missing its verb. "Be thou my surety" matches ASV exactly and restores the grammar.
- NEW: Like a crane or a swallow, so did I chatter: I did mourn as a dove: mine eyes fail with looking upward: O Lord, I am oppressed; be thou my surety.

## undertook → took — Esther 9:23
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "took to do" is awkward. "Promised" matches Geneva1599 exactly and is well-attested (Numbers 14:40, Deuteronomy 1:11).
- NEW: And the Jews promised to do as they had begun, and as Mordecai had written unto them;

## undo → thee — Isaiah 58:6
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "to thee the heavy burdens" is ungrammatical (pronoun for verb). "Take off" matches Geneva1599 exactly.
- NEW: Is not this the fast that I have chosen? to loose the bands of wickedness, to take off the heavy burdens, and to let the oppressed go free, and that ye break every yoke?

## undo → thee — Zephaniah 3:19
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "I will thee all that afflict thee" is nonsensical (double "thee"). "Deal with" is supported by four witnesses (Darby, ASV, ACV, BSB).
- NEW: Behold, at that time I will deal with all that afflict thee: and I will save her that halteth, and gather her that was driven out; and I will get them praise and fame in every land where they have been put to shame.

## undressed → separated — Leviticus 25:5
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "vine separated" is awkward and unclear. "Untrimmed" matches RWebster exactly and conveys the sense of an uncared-for vine.
- NEW: That which groweth of its own accord of thy harvest thou shalt not reap, neither gather the grapes of thy untrimmed vine: for it is a year of rest unto the land.

## undressed → separated — Leviticus 25:11
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same fix as Leviticus 25:5.
- NEW: A jubile shall that fiftieth year be unto you: ye shall not sow, neither reap that which groweth of itself in it, nor gather the grapes in it of thy untrimmed vine.

## unequal → equal — Ezekiel 18:25
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "equal" inverts the meaning — the verse is a rhetorical accusation ("are not your ways unequal/unjust?"), and CUR turns it into agreement. "Unjust" is well-attested (Luke 16:10, Romans 3:5) and matches BSB, preserving the accusation.
- NEW: Yet ye say, The way of the Lord is not equal. Hear now, O house of Israel; Is not my way equal? are not your ways unjust?

## unequal → equal — Ezekiel 18:29
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same meaning-inversion fix as Ezekiel 18:25.
- NEW: Yet saith the house of Israel, The way of the Lord is not equal. O house of Israel, are not my ways equal? are not your ways unjust?

## unequally → together — II Corinthians 6:14
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR duplicates "together...together" and was itself flagged by the automated system as unsafe. "Strangely" conveys the sense of an inappropriate mismatch and uses the well-attested KJV root "strange."
- NEW: Be ye not strangely yoked together with unbelievers: for what fellowship hath righteousness with unrighteousness? and what communion hath light with darkness?

## unfaithful → dealt — Proverbs 25:19
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "an dealt man" is ungrammatical (wrong part of speech, wrong article). "Treacherous" is KJV-attested (Isaiah 21:2 "the treacherous dealer dealeth treacherously") and matches YLT/Darby.
- NEW: Confidence in a treacherous man in time of trouble is like a broken tooth, and a foot out of joint.

## unfaithfully → treacherously — Psalms 78:57
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "treacherously" matches YLT/Darby exactly and is well-attested.
- NEW: But turned back, and dealt treacherously like their fathers: they were turned aside like a deceitful bow.

## ungirded → opened — Genesis 24:32
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "opened his camels" is nonsensical. "Loosed" is well-attested KJV usage and matches the sense of YLT's "looseth the camels."
- NEW: And the man came into the house: and he loosed his camels, and gave straw and provender for the camels, and water to wash his feet, and the men's feet that were with him.

## unite → joined — Psalms 86:11
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "joined" breaks the imperative mood of the verse ("Teach me... unite my heart"). "Knit" is imperative-compatible, KJV-attested (Colossians 2:2, 1 Samuel 18:1), and matches Geneva1599 exactly.
- NEW: Teach me thy way, O Lord; I will walk in thy truth: knit my heart to fear thy name.

## united → joined — Genesis 49:6
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "joined" matches Geneva1599 exactly and is grammatical.
- NEW: O my soul, come not thou into their secret; unto their assembly, mine honour, be not thou joined: for in their anger they slew a man, and in their selfwill they digged down a wall.

## unjustly → perversely — Psalms 82:2
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "perversely" matches YLT exactly.
- NEW: How long will ye judge perversely, and accept the persons of the wicked? Selah.

## unjustly → wickedly — Isaiah 26:10
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "wickedly" matches Geneva1599 exactly and is extremely well-attested.
- NEW: Let favour be shewed to the wicked, yet will he not learn righteousness: in the land of uprightness will he deal wickedly, and will not behold the majesty of the Lord.

## unlade → empty — Acts 21:3
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "to empty her burden" is grammatical and preserves the sense of unloading cargo; "empty" as a verb is well-attested (Ecclesiastes 11:3).
- NEW: Now when we had discovered Cyprus, we left it on the left hand, and sailed into Syria, and landed at Tyre: for there the ship was to empty her burden.

## unlawful → law — Acts 10:28
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "an law thing" is ungrammatical (noun for adjective, wrong article). "Forbidden" is KJV-attested (Leviticus 5:17, Colossians 2:21) and restores the grammar.
- NEW: And he said unto them, Ye know how that it is a forbidden thing for a man that is a Jew to keep company, or come unto one of another nation; but God hath shewed me that I should not call any man common or unclean.

## unlawful → law — II Peter 2:8
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same fix as Acts 10:28.
- NEW: (For that righteous man dwelling among them, in seeing and hearing, vexed his righteous soul from day to day with their forbidden deeds;)

## unmerciful → merciless — Romans 1:31
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "merciless" matches Tyndale exactly and is well-attested.
- NEW: Without understanding, covenantbreakers, without natural affection, implacable, merciless:

## unmindful → thee — Deuteronomy 32:18
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "thou art thee" is ungrammatical. "Forgetful" restores the predicate-adjective slot and mirrors the intentional Hebrew-style parallelism with "hast forgotten" in the following clause.
- NEW: Of the Rock that begat thee thou art forgetful, and hast forgotten God that formed thee.

## unmoveable → cannot — Acts 27:41
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "remained cannot" is ungrammatical. "Immovable" is a genuinely distinct formation (different prefix/etymology) from the flagged "unmoveable," matches Webster/YLT, and restores the grammar.
- NEW: And falling into a place where two seas met, they ran the ship aground; and the forepart stuck fast, and remained immovable, but the hinder part was broken with the violence of the waves.

## unmoveable → cannot — I Corinthians 15:58
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same fix as Acts 27:41.
- NEW: Therefore, my beloved brethren, be ye stedfast, immovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord, forasmuch as ye know that your labour is not in vain in the Lord.

## unoccupied → ceased — Judges 5:6
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "the highways were ceased" is ungrammatical. "Forsaken" is KJV-attested (Isaiah 62:12) and conveys abandoned roads; cross-batch note — this verse's "travellers" was already revised to "wayfaring men" in batch 0103, combined here.
- NEW: In the days of Shamgar the son of Anath, in the days of Jael, the highways were forsaken, and the wayfaring men walked through byways.

## unperfect → substance — Psalms 139:16
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR duplicates "substance," producing "my substance, yet being substance" (nonsensical). "Unformed" is supported by four witnesses (Darby, ASV, BBE, BSB) and avoids the duplication.
- NEW: Thine eyes did see my substance, yet being unformed; and in thy book all my members were written, which in continuance were fashioned, when as yet there was none of them.

## unprepared → unready — II Corinthians 9:4
*(from batch_0105_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "unready" is a plausible Early Modern compound and does not invert the meaning; matches BBE's "not ready."
- NEW: Lest haply if they of Macedonia come with me, and find you unready, we (that we say not, ye) should be ashamed in this same confident boasting.

## unprofitableness → vanity — Hebrews 7:18
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "vanity" (KJV's common word for futility/worthlessness, as throughout Ecclesiastes) overlaps substantially with "unprofitableness" and does not invert the sense.
- NEW: For there is verily a disannulling of the commandment going before for the weakness and vanity thereof.

## unquenchable → never — Matthew 3:12
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "never fire" is ungrammatical (adverb for adjective). Rephrasing as "fire that never shall be quenched" matches Tyndale and Geneva1599 exactly.
- NEW: Whose fan is in his hand, and he will throughly purge his floor, and gather his wheat into the garner; but he will burn up the chaff with fire that never shall be quenched.

## unquenchable → never — Luke 3:17
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same fix as Matthew 3:12, matching Tyndale/Geneva1599 for this verse.
- NEW: Whose fan is in his hand, and he will throughly purge his floor, and will gather the wheat into his garner; but the chaff he will burn with fire that never shall be quenched.

## unreasonable → harm — Acts 25:27
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "it seemeth to me harm" is ungrammatical. Wycliffe attests "with out resoun" for this exact verse; "without reason" restores sense and grammar with direct period support.
- NEW: For it seemeth to me without reason to send a prisoner, and not withal to signify the crimes laid against him.

## unreasonable → harm — II Thessalonians 3:2
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "harm and wicked men" awkwardly pairs a thing with persons. "Bad" (Darby) is a simple, well-attested adjective that fits the person-slot cleanly.
- NEW: And that we may be delivered from bad and wicked men: for all men have not faith.

## unrebukeable → blameless — I Timothy 6:14
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: No duplication risk in this verse; "blameless" is well-attested.
- NEW: That thou keep this commandment without spot, blameless, until the appearing of our Lord Jesus Christ:

## unreproveable → blameless — Colossians 1:22
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Cross-batch dependency — batch 0104 already revises "unblameable" to "blameless" in this same verse; using "blameless" again here would duplicate it. "Without fault" matches Geneva1599 exactly and avoids the repetition.
- NEW: In the body of his flesh through death, to present you holy and blameless and without fault in his sight:

## unrighteously → wickedly — Deuteronomy 25:16
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "wickedly" matches Geneva1599 exactly.
- NEW: For all that do such things, and all that do wickedly, are an abomination unto the Lord thy God.

## unripe → grape — Job 15:33
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR duplicates "grape grape" (nonsensical). "Sour" is directly attested by Geneva1599 ("her sower grape") and is well-attested KJV vocabulary (Jeremiah 31:29-30, Ezekiel 18:2).
- NEW: He shall shake off his sour grape as the vine, and shall cast off his flower as the olive.

## unsatiable → full — Ezekiel 16:28
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "full" directly contradicts the following clause "yet couldest not be satisfied" — a meaning inversion. "Not satisfied" restores internal consistency, echoing DRC's "not yet satisfied."
- NEW: Thou hast played the whore also with the Assyrians, because thou wast not satisfied; yea, thou hast played the harlot with them, and yet couldest not be satisfied.

## unsavoury → froward — II Samuel 22:27
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR duplicates "froward...froward." "Contrary" matches Darby and is well-attested, preserving the contrastive wordplay without repeating the word.
- NEW: With the pure thou wilt shew thyself pure; and with the froward thou wilt shew thyself contrary.

## unsavoury → froward — Job 6:6
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "froward" is a category error when applied to food. Rephrasing with the well-attested KJV word "savour" (Matthew 5:13) matches ASV's structure and preserves sense.
- NEW: Can that which hath no savour be eaten without salt? or is there any taste in the white of an egg?

## unseemly → uncomely — Romans 1:27
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "uncomely" is itself flagged as a rare word elsewhere in this batch set (I Corinthians 7:36, 12:23 in batch 0105) — using it here would be circular. "Filthy" matches Tyndale/Geneva1599's "filthiness" and is well-attested (Zechariah 3:3-4, Revelation 22:11).
- NEW: And likewise also the men, leaving the natural use of the woman, burned in their lust one toward another; men with men working that which is filthy, and receiving in themselves that recompence of their error which was meet.

## unseemly → uncomely — I Corinthians 13:5
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same circularity problem as Romans 1:27. Tyndale attests "dishonestly" directly for this verse.
- NEW: Doth not behave itself dishonestly, seeketh not her own, is not easily provoked, thinketh no evil;

## unshod → barefoot — Jeremiah 2:25
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "barefoot" is well-attested (2 Samuel 15:30, Isaiah 20:2-4) and preserves the meaning exactly.
- NEW: Withhold thy foot from being barefoot, and thy throat from thirst: but thou saidst, There is no hope: no; for I have loved strangers, and after them will I go.

## unskilful → unlearned — Hebrews 5:13
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "unlearned" is well-attested (1 Corinthians 14:16, 2 Timothy 2:23, 2 Peter 3:16) and preserves the sense of inexperience.
- NEW: For every one that useth milk is unlearned in the word of righteousness: for he is a babe.

## unspotted → undefiled — James 1:27
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR duplicates "undefiled," already used earlier in the same verse ("Pure religion and undefiled before God"). "Clean" is well-attested KJV vocabulary and avoids all duplication ("Pure"/"undefiled" already used).
- NEW: Pure religion and undefiled before God and the Father is this, To visit the fatherless and widows in their affliction, and to keep himself clean from the world.

## unstopped → opened — Isaiah 35:5
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR duplicates "opened," used for the eyes in the same verse, losing the KJV's deliberate variation. Shifting to "shall hear" preserves the sense (deaf ears unstopped = made able to hear) without repetition.
- NEW: Then the eyes of the blind shall be opened, and the ears of the deaf shall hear.

## untaken → away — II Corinthians 3:14
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR duplicates "away away" (nonsensical). Rephrasing with simple negation, "not taken away," restores grammar without introducing a new word.
- NEW: But their minds were blinded: for until this day remaineth the same vail, not taken away, in the reading of the old testament; which vail is done away in Christ.

## unthankful → ungrateful — Luke 6:35
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "ungrateful" is not attested in any pre-1611 witness here; both Tyndale and Geneva1599 use "unkind" directly for this verse ("he is kynde vnto the vnkynde").
- NEW: But love ye your enemies, and do good, and lend, hoping for nothing again; and your reward shall be great, and ye shall be the children of the Highest: for he is kind unto the unkind and to the evil.

## unthankful → ungrateful — II Timothy 3:2
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same period-attestation fix as Luke 6:35 ("unkind"), applied for consistency.
- NEW: For men shall be lovers of their own selves, covetous, boasters, proud, blasphemers, disobedient to parents, unkind, unholy,

## untoward → perverse — Acts 2:40
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "perverse" matches Webster, RWebster, YLT, Darby, and DRC exactly.
- NEW: And with many other words did he testify and exhort, saying, Save yourselves from this perverse generation.

## unweighed → untold — I Kings 7:47
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "untold" (innumerable) reasonably conveys the sense of an unmeasured quantity and fits the following clause "because they were exceeding many"; not a meaning inversion.
- NEW: And Solomon left all the vessels untold, because they were exceeding many: neither was the weight of the brass found out.

## unworthily → irreverently — I Corinthians 11:27
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "irreverently" narrows but does not invert "unworthily"; no period witness offers an alternative (all keep "unworthily").
- NEW: Wherefore whosoever shall eat this bread, and drink this cup of the Lord, irreverently, shall be guilty of the body and blood of the Lord.

## unworthily → irreverently — I Corinthians 11:29
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Same reasoning as I Corinthians 11:27.
- NEW: For he that eateth and drinketh irreverently, eateth and drinketh damnation to himself, not discerning the Lord's body.

## unworthy → worthy — Acts 13:46
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "worthy" is a direct meaning inversion — it flips the Jews' rejection of the gospel into acceptance of it, contradicting the following clause "we turn to the Gentiles." Simple negation "not worthy" restores the correct sense without needing the flagged compound.
- NEW: Then Paul and Barnabas waxed bold, and said, It was necessary that the word of God should first have been spoken to you: but seeing ye put it from you, and judge yourselves not worthy of everlasting life, lo, we turn to the Gentiles.

## unworthy → none — I Corinthians 6:2
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "are ye none to judge" is ungrammatical. Simple negation "not worthy" restores grammar and sense.
- NEW: Do ye not know that the saints shall judge the world? and if the world shall be judged by you, are ye not worthy to judge the smallest matters?

## upbraid → reproach — Judges 8:15
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "reproach" matches YLT/UKJV exactly and is extremely well-attested.
- NEW: And he came unto the men of Succoth, and said, Behold Zebah and Zalmunna, with whom ye did reproach me, saying, Are the hands of Zebah and Zalmunna now in thine hand, that we should give bread unto thy men that are weary?

## upbraid → reproach — Matthew 11:20
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "reproach" matches YLT/Darby/UKJV exactly.
- NEW: Then began he to reproach the cities wherein most of his mighty works were done, because they repented not:

## upbraided → reproached — Mark 16:14
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "reproached" matches Geneva1599/YLT exactly.
- NEW: Afterward he appeared unto the eleven as they sat at meat, and reproached them with their unbelief and hardness of heart, because they believed not them which had seen him after he was risen.

## upbraideth → reproaches — James 1:5
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "reproaches" matches Geneva1599/YLT/Darby/UKJV exactly.
- NEW: If any of you lack wisdom, let him ask of God, that giveth to all men liberally, and reproaches not; and it shall be given him.

## upheld → lay — Isaiah 63:5
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "my fury, it lay me" is nonsensical. "Sustained" matches Geneva1599 exactly and is well-attested (Psalm 3:5, 55:22).
- NEW: And I looked, and there was none to help; and I wondered that there was none to uphold: therefore mine own arm brought salvation unto me; and my fury, it sustained me.

## upholden → rose — Job 4:4
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "have rose him" is ungrammatical (intransitive verb used transitively). "Raised up" matches YLT and is grammatically clean.
- NEW: Thy words have raised up him that was falling, and thou hast strengthened the feeble knees.

## upholden → rose — Proverbs 20:28
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "is rose by mercy" is ungrammatical. "Established" matches Geneva1599 exactly ("shalbe established with mercie").
- NEW: Mercy and truth preserve the king: and his throne is established by mercy.

## uprising → rising — Psalms 139:2
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Geneva1599 attests exactly this simplification for both halves of the pair ("my sitting and my rising"), so dropping the "up-" prefix is a legitimate period-attested rendering, not mere mechanical trimming.
- NEW: Thou knowest my downsitting and mine rising, thou understandest my thought afar off.

## urge → quarrel — Luke 11:53
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "began to quarrel him" is ungrammatical (quarrel is normally intransitive). "Press" is KJV-attested in the identical construction at Luke 8:45 ("the multitude throng thee and press thee").
- NEW: And as he said these things unto them, the scribes and the Pharisees began to press him vehemently, and to provoke him to speak of many things:

## urgent → hold — Exodus 12:33
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "were hold upon the people" is ungrammatical. "Pressed" matches DRC's sense and is well-attested.
- NEW: And the Egyptians pressed upon the people, that they might send them out of the land in haste; for they said, We be all dead men.

## urgent → hold — Daniel 3:22
*(from batch_0106_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "was hold" is ungrammatical. "Strait" is directly attested by Geneva1599 for this exact verse ("the Kings commandement was straite").
- NEW: Therefore because the king's commandment was strait, and the furnace exceeding hot, the flame of the fire slew those men that took up Shadrach, Meshach, and Abed-nego.

## usurer → creditor — Exodus 22:25
*(from batch_0107_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "an exact" is not a word; ASV/ACV render "a creditor," a common KJV-attested noun (2 Kings 4:1, Luke 7:41). Article changes an→a for the consonant-initial replacement.
- NEW: If thou lend money to any of my people that is poor by thee, thou shalt not be to him as a creditor, neither shalt thou lay upon him usury.

## usurp → have dominion over — I Timothy 2:12
*(from batch_0107_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
