- rationale: "to authority authority" duplicates a noun and drops the verb. ASV renders "have dominion over," a common KJV phrase (Genesis 1:26); DRC/Wycliffe confirm the "exercise power" sense.
- NEW: But I suffer not a woman to teach, nor to have dominion over the man, but to be in silence.

## vainglory → pride — Philippians 2:3
*(from batch_0107_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "pride" is common in the KJV corpus, matches BBE, does not duplicate any word already in the verse, and does not invert the sense of self-exaltation.
- NEW: Let nothing be done through strife or pride; but in lowliness of mind let each esteem other better than themselves.

## vainly → proudly — Colossians 2:18
*(from batch_0107_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "proudly puffed up" preserves the sense of unwarranted self-exaltation (compare BBE "foolishly lifted up"); common word, no duplication, no inversion.
- NEW: Let no man beguile you of your reward in a voluntary humility and worshipping of angels, intruding into those things which he hath not seen, proudly puffed up by his fleshly mind,

## variableness → changing — James 1:17
*(from batch_0107_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "no changing, neither shadow of turning" preserves the parallel gerund structure of the original ("changing"/"turning"), a natural and common construction; meaning intact.
- NEW: Every good gift and every perfect gift is from above, and cometh down from the Father of lights, with whom is no changing, neither shadow of turning.

## variance → strife — Matthew 10:35
*(from batch_0107_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "strife" is an extremely common KJV noun and preserves the sense of setting family members against one another; no duplication in this verse.
- NEW: For I am come to set a man at strife against his father, and the daughter against her mother, and the daughter in law against her mother in law.

## variance → debate — Galatians 5:20
*(from batch_0107_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "strifes" duplicates "strife" later in the same list. Geneva1599 renders this word "debate" ("hatred, debate, emulations..."), a well-attested period synonym for contention that does not repeat another list item.
- NEW: Idolatry, witchcraft, hatred, debate, emulations, wrath, strife, seditions, heresies,

## vaunt → glory — Judges 7:2
*(from batch_0107_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "beautify themselves against me" is semantically empty in context. Geneva1599 "make their vaunt against me" and DRC/Wycliffe "glory against me" show the sense is boastful self-exaltation; "glory" used as a verb is common in the KJV (e.g. 1 Corinthians 3:21).
- NEW: And the Lord said unto Gideon, The people that are with thee are too many for me to give the Midianites into their hands, lest Israel glory against me, saying, Mine own hand hath saved me.

## vaunteth → boasteth — I Corinthians 13:4
*(from batch_0107_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Matches Geneva1599 "doeth not boast it selfe"; common word, no duplication, meaning intact.
- NEW: Charity suffereth long, and is kind; charity envieth not; charity boasteth not itself, is not puffed up,

## vein → mine — Job 28:1
*(from batch_0107_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "a going for the silver" is ungrammatical; ASV/BSB render "a mine for silver," which parallels "a place for gold" in the next clause and is common English.
- NEW: Surely there is a mine for the silver, and a place for gold where they fine it.

## venom → gall — Deuteronomy 32:33
*(from batch_0107_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Geneva1599 reads "the cruel gall of aspes" verbatim, a direct period attestation of this exact rendering.
- NEW: Their wine is the poison of dragons, and the cruel gall of asps.

## venomous → poisonous — Acts 28:4
*(from batch_0107_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Common English word, no meaning change, no duplication.
- NEW: And when the barbarians saw the poisonous beast hang on his hand, they said among themselves, No doubt this man is a murderer, whom, though he hath escaped the sea, yet vengeance suffereth not to live.

## vent → opened — Job 32:19
*(from batch_0107_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "hath no open" is ungrammatical (adjective misused as noun). YLT renders "wine not opened," recasting the noun "vent" as a passive verb clause — a legitimate rephrase (rule 9) matching the sense of an unvented, ready-to-burst vessel.
- NEW: Behold, my belly is as wine which is not opened; it is ready to burst like new bottles.

## verity → truth — Psalms 111:7
*(from batch_0107_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Matches Geneva1599 "trueth" and DRC; common word.
- NEW: The works of his hands are truth and judgment; all his commandments are sure.

## verity → truth — I Timothy 2:7
*(from batch_0107_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Matches YLT/DRC/ASV "truth"; common word, no duplication (verse's earlier "truth" is a deliberate cognate pairing, as YLT/DRC also render it).
- NEW: Whereunto I am ordained a preacher, and an apostle, (I speak the truth in Christ, and lie not;) a teacher of the Gentiles in faith and truth.

## vermilion → red — Jeremiah 22:14
*(from batch_0107_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "red" is plain, common English, preserves the sense (a color used for wall painting); not a meaning inversion.
- NEW: That saith, I will build me a wide house and large chambers, and cutteth him out windows; and it is cieled with cedar, and painted with red.

## vermilion → red — Ezekiel 23:14
*(from batch_0107_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same reasoning as Jeremiah 22:14; BBE independently glosses it "bright red."
- NEW: And that she increased her whoredoms: for when she saw men pourtrayed upon the wall, the images of the Chaldeans pourtrayed with red,

## vestments → garments — II Kings 10:22
*(from batch_0107_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Matches DRC "garments" and YLT "clothing"; common word, no duplication with "vestry" (kept elsewhere in the verse, addressed in the next entry).
- NEW: And he said unto him that was over the vestry, Bring forth garments for all the worshippers of Baal. And he brought them forth garments.

## vestry → wardrobe — II Kings 10:22
*(from batch_0107_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Matches DRC/YLT/Darby "wardrobe" exactly; common word. Cross-batch note: this verse also carries the vestments→garments entry above — the fully combined verse reads "over the wardrobe, Bring forth garments... he brought them forth garments."
- NEW: And he said unto him that was over the wardrobe, Bring forth vestments for all the worshippers of Baal. And he brought them forth vestments.

## vigilant → temperate — I Timothy 3:2
*(from batch_0107_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "husband of one wife, watch, sober" is ungrammatical (needs an adjective). "watchful" cannot be used — it is itself a flagged rare word elsewhere in this batch set (Revelation 3:2), which would create a circular rare-for-rare swap (rule 10). ASV/RWebster render "temperate," a common adjective that fits the list of qualities.
- NEW: A bishop then must be blameless, the husband of one wife, temperate, sober, of good behaviour, given to hospitality, apt to teach;

## vigilant → watch — I Peter 5:8
*(from batch_0107_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "be watch" is ungrammatical. Geneva1599 and DRC both render this clause as an imperative verb, "Be sober, and watch" — a legitimate rephrase (adjective slot to verb clause, rule 8/9) with strong period attestation.
- NEW: Be sober, and watch; because your adversary the devil, as a roaring lion, walketh about, seeking whom he may devour:

## vilely → shamefully — II Samuel 1:21
*(from batch_0107_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Common word, matches BBE "shamed" in sense; no inversion.
- NEW: Ye mountains of Gilboa, let there be no dew, neither let there be rain, upon you, nor fields of offerings: for there the shield of the mighty is shamefully cast away, the shield of Saul, as though he had not been anointed with oil.

## viler → meaner — Job 30:8
*(from batch_0107_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "baser" shares its root with "base men" earlier in the same verse ("children of base men: they were baser than the earth"), a near-duplicate (rule 12). "meaner" is a distinct, common KJV word for the lowly/contemptible (compare "mean men," Isaiah 2:9; Proverbs 22:29) and avoids the repetition.
- NEW: They were children of fools, yea, children of base men: they were meaner than the earth.

## villany → folly — Isaiah 32:6
*(from batch_0108_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "vile" duplicates "the vile person" earlier in the same verse. Wycliffe/DRC/ASV consistently render this word "folly"/"foolish things," a common KJV word (Proverbs, Ecclesiastes) that avoids the repetition.
- NEW: For the vile person will speak folly, and his heart will work iniquity, to practise hypocrisy, and to utter error against the Lord, to make empty the soul of the hungry, and he will cause the drink of the thirsty to fail.

## villany → folly — Jeremiah 29:23
*(from batch_0108_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "committed vile in Israel" misuses an adjective as a noun. Wycliffe/DRC/YLT/ASV all render this "folly" ("wrought folly in Israel"), a common KJV noun.
- NEW: Because they have committed folly in Israel, and have committed adultery with their neighbours’ wives, and have spoken lying words in my name, which I have not commanded them; even I know, and am a witness, saith the Lord.

## viol → psaltery — Isaiah 5:12
*(from batch_0108_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Matches YLT "psaltery" exactly; "psaltery" is extremely common in the KJV corpus (Psalms); no duplication in this verse.
- NEW: And the harp, and the psaltery, the tabret, and pipe, and wine, are in their feasts: but they regard not the work of the Lord, neither consider the operation of his hands.

## viol → psaltery — Amos 6:5
*(from batch_0108_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Matches YLT "psaltery" exactly; common KJV word.
- NEW: That chant to the sound of the psaltery, and invent to themselves instruments of musick, like David;

## violated → done violence to — Ezekiel 22:26
*(from batch_0108_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "have imagined my law" is nonsensical and changes the meaning entirely. Darby/ASV/BSB render "done violence to my law," a common KJV construction ("do violence," e.g. Jeremiah 22:3) that preserves the sense of transgression.
- NEW: Her priests have done violence to my law, and have profaned mine holy things: they have put no difference between the holy and profane, neither have they shewed difference between the unclean and the clean, and have hid their eyes from my sabbaths, and I am profaned among them.

## viols → harps — Isaiah 14:11
*(from batch_0108_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "the noise of thy flagons" is a meaning error — flagons are vessels, not instruments, and cannot make "noise" of music. "harps" is common, unambiguous, and not duplicated elsewhere in the verse.
- NEW: Thy pomp is brought down to the grave, and the noise of thy harps: the worm is spread under thee, and the worms cover thee.

## viols → harps — Amos 5:23
*(from batch_0108_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "the melody of thy flagons" is the same category error as Isaiah 14:11. RWebster attests "harps" directly for this verse.
- NEW: Take thou away from me the noise of thy songs; for I will not hear the melody of thy harps.

## virtuously → worthily — Proverbs 31:29
*(from batch_0108_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "valiantly" shifts the sense toward bravery rather than excellence of conduct. YLT/Darby/ASV all render "worthily," a common KJV word (e.g. "walk worthy") that better preserves the meaning of commendable behavior.
- NEW: Many daughters have done worthily, but thou excellest them all.

## visible → manifest — Colossians 1:16
*(from batch_0108_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "manifest" is a very common KJV word meaning visible/apparent; pairs sensibly with "invisible" and is not duplicated elsewhere in the verse.
- NEW: For by him were all things created, that are in heaven, and that are in earth, manifest and invisible, whether they be thrones, or dominions, or principalities, or powers: all things were created by him, and for him:

## vocation → calling — Ephesians 4:1
*(from batch_0108_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "call" is grammatically valid but far less KJV-common than "calling," which Geneva1599/Darby/ASV/ACV all use; "calling...called" is a deliberate cognate pairing attested by the witnesses, not a forbidden duplication.
- NEW: I therefore, the prisoner of the Lord, beseech you that ye walk worthy of the calling wherewith ye are called,

## volume → roll — Psalms 40:7
*(from batch_0108_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Matches Geneva1599/ASV "roll" exactly; common word.
- NEW: Then said I, Lo, I come: in the roll of the book it is written of me,

## volume → roll — Hebrews 10:7
*(from batch_0108_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Matches Darby/ASV "roll" exactly; common word.
- NEW: Then said I, Lo, I come (in the roll of the book it is written of me,) to do thy will, O God.

## voluntarily → freely — Ezekiel 46:12
*(from batch_0108_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Matches Geneva1599 verbatim ("peace offrings freely vnto the Lord"); strong period attestation.
- NEW: Now when the prince shall prepare a voluntary burnt offering or peace offerings freely unto the Lord, one shall then open him the gate that looketh toward the east, and he shall prepare his burnt offering and his peace offerings, as he did on the sabbath day: then he shall go forth; and after his going forth one shall shut the gate.

## vowedst → didst vow — Genesis 31:13
*(from batch_0108_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "where thou vow a vow" breaks the second-person past-tense inflection required by the surrounding "anointedst." RWebster's periphrastic "didst vow" restores correct grammar while replacing the flagged word.
- NEW: I am the God of Beth–el, where thou anointedst the pillar, and where thou didst vow a vow unto me: now arise, get thee out from this land, and return unto the land of thy kindred.

## voyage → course — Acts 27:10
*(from batch_0108_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "course" is a common KJV word and preserves the sense of a sea journey; no inversion, no duplication.
- NEW: And said unto them, Sirs, I perceive that this course will be with hurt and much damage, not only of the lading and ship, but also of our lives.

## vulture → falcon — Leviticus 11:14
*(from batch_0108_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "eagle" duplicates "the eagle" which opens this same list of unclean birds at verse 13. ASV/Darby render "falcon," avoiding the repetition while keeping a bird-of-prey sense.
- NEW: And the falcon, and the kite after his kind;

## vulture → falcon — Deuteronomy 14:13
*(from batch_0108_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same duplication problem as Leviticus 11:14 — "eagle" opens this list at verse 12. ASV/Darby attest "falcon."
- NEW: And the glede, and the kite, and the falcon after his kind,

## vulture's → falcon's — Job 28:7
*(from batch_0108_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR left "vulture's" unchanged, but the word is flagged and must go. ASV/ACV render "falcon's eye," a common, non-duplicating substitute.
- NEW: There is a path which no fowl knoweth, and which the falcon’s eye hath not seen:

## vultures → eagles — Isaiah 34:15
*(from batch_0108_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "eagles" is common and not duplicated within this verse (unlike the Leviticus/Deuteronomy bird-lists, this verse has no earlier "eagle"); meaning intact.
- NEW: There shall the great owl make her nest, and lay, and hatch, and gather under her shadow: there shall the eagles also be gathered, every one with her mate.

## wagon → wain — Numbers 7:3
*(from batch_0108_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "cart" is inconsistent with "wagons" used earlier in the same verse (the plural is not flagged and remains). Wycliffe attests "waynes" for this passage; "wain" is the same word-family in period English (cf. "hay wain") and avoids introducing a mismatched term.
- NEW: And they brought their offering before the Lord, six covered wagons, and twelve oxen; a wain for two of the princes, and for each one an ox: and they brought them before the tabernacle.

## wakened → stirred up — Joel 3:12
*(from batch_0108_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "raised" risks confusion with resurrection imagery, a meaning shift from the intended sense of being roused to action. Darby "rouse themselves" and YLT "wake and come up" point to "stirred up," a common KJV phrase (e.g. many uses of "stir up").
- NEW: Let the heathen be stirred up, and come up to the valley of Jehoshaphat: for there will I sit to judge all the heathen round about.

## wakened → stirred — Zechariah 4:1
*(from batch_0108_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "raised... out of his sleep" is an unnatural pairing (raised usually pairs with resurrection, not sleep). "Awakened" was avoided as a bare re-inflection of the flagged root; "stirred," in the sense used elsewhere in this same batch for related words, reads naturally with "out of his sleep."
- NEW: And the angel that talked with me came again, and waked me, as a man that is stirred out of his sleep,

## wakeneth → stirreth — Isaiah 50:4
*(from batch_0108_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "stirreth" preserves the sense of rousing, is not duplicated, and reflects common KJV usage ("stir up").
- NEW: The Lord God hath given me the tongue of the learned, that I should know how to speak a word in season to him that is weary: he stirreth morning by morning, he stirreth mine ear to hear as the learned.

## walkedst → wentest — John 21:18
*(from batch_0108_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's bare "walk" breaks the required -edst second-person inflection matching "girdedst." "wentest" is a very common KJV second-person past form (of "go," synonymous with "walked" in sense) that restores correct grammar.
- NEW: Verily, verily, I say unto thee, When thou wast young, thou girdedst thyself, and wentest whither thou wouldest: but when thou shalt be old, thou shalt stretch forth thy hands, and another shall gird thee, and carry thee whither thou wouldest not.

## wanderers → pourers — Jeremiah 48:12
*(from batch_0109_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "captives, that shall cause him to wander" is nonsensical (captives do not cause others to wander). Darby renders "pourers that shall pour him off," matching the wine-vessel metaphor of the verse (bottles emptied and broken); ASV likewise "them that pour off."
- NEW: Therefore, behold, the days come, saith the Lord, that I will send unto him pourers, that shall cause him to wander, and shall empty his vessels, and break their bottles.

## wanderers → wander — Hosea 9:17
*(from batch_0109_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "wanderers" is itself the flagged word and must go. Geneva1599 attests the verb form "they shall wander among the nations" verbatim — a legitimate rephrase (noun clause to verb clause) with strong period support.
- NEW: My God will cast them away, because they did not hearken unto him: and they shall wander among the nations.

## wantonness → lasciviousness — Romans 13:13
*(from batch_0109_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Matches YLT/Darby "lasciviousness" exactly; common KJV word (Mark 7:22, Galatians 5:19).
- NEW: Let us walk honestly, as in the day; not in rioting and drunkenness, not in chambering and lasciviousness, not in strife and envying.

## wantonness → lasciviousness — II Peter 2:18
*(from batch_0109_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Matches ASV "lasciviousness" exactly; common KJV word.
- NEW: For when they speak great swelling words of vanity, they allure through the lusts of the flesh, through much lasciviousness, those that were clean escaped from them who live in error.

## wardrobe → garments — II Kings 22:14
*(from batch_0109_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "keeper of the garment" (singular) reads as though he kept one garment. YLT renders "keeper of the garments" (plural), matching the role of custodian of a wardrobe.
- NEW: So Hilkiah the priest, and Ahikam, and Achbor, and Shaphan, and Asahiah, went unto Huldah the prophetess, the wife of Shallum the son of Tikvah, the son of Harhas, keeper of the garments; (now she dwelt in Jerusalem in the college;) and they communed with her.

## wardrobe → garments — II Chronicles 34:22
*(from batch_0109_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same fix as II Kings 22:14 (parallel passage); YLT attests "keeper of the garments."
- NEW: And Hilkiah, and they that the king had appointed, went to Huldah the prophetess, the wife of Shallum the son of Tikvath, the son of Hasrah, keeper of the garments; (now she dwelt in Jerusalem in the college:) and they spake to her to that effect.

## warrior → soldier — Isaiah 9:5
*(from batch_0109_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Common word, meaning preserved, no duplication.
- NEW: For every battle of the soldier is with confused noise, and garments rolled in blood; but this shall be with burning and fuel of fire.

## warriors → apt for war — I Kings 12:21
*(from batch_0109_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "thus" is grammatically empty ("chosen men, which were thus"). Darby renders "chosen men apt for war," a legitimate relative-clause rephrase (rule 9) with the same meaning as "warriors."
- NEW: And when Rehoboam was come to Jerusalem, he assembled all the house of Judah, with the tribe of Benjamin, an hundred and fourscore thousand chosen men, apt for war, to fight against the house of Israel, to bring the kingdom again to Rehoboam the son of Solomon.

## warriors → apt for war — II Chronicles 11:1
*(from batch_0109_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same fix as I Kings 12:21 (parallel passage); Darby attests "chosen men apt for war."
- NEW: And when Rehoboam was come to Jerusalem, he gathered of the house of Judah and Benjamin an hundred and fourscore thousand chosen men, apt for war, to fight against Israel, that he might bring the kingdom again to Rehoboam.

## washpot → pot for washing — Psalms 60:8
*(from batch_0109_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's bare "pot" loses the specific image of a washing vessel. UKJV attests the fuller phrase "pot for washing," which preserves the meaning using common words.
- NEW: Moab is my pot for washing; over Edom will I cast out my shoe: Philistia, triumph thou because of me.

## washpot → pot for washing — Psalms 108:9
*(from batch_0109_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same fix as Psalms 60:8 (parallel psalm); UKJV attests "pot for washing."
- NEW: Moab is my pot for washing; over Edom will I cast out my shoe; over Philistia will I triumph.

## wasteness → waste — Zephaniah 1:15
*(from batch_0109_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Matches YLT "waste" exactly; common word.
- NEW: That day is a day of wrath, a day of trouble and distress, a day of waste and desolation, a day of darkness and gloominess, a day of clouds and thick darkness,

## waster → destroyer — Proverbs 18:9
*(from batch_0109_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Matches UKJV "destroyer" exactly; common word.
- NEW: He also that is slothful in his work is brother to him that is a great destroyer.

## waster → destroyer — Isaiah 54:16
*(from batch_0109_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Matches UKJV/Geneva1599 "destroyer" exactly; common word.
- NEW: Behold, I have created the smith that bloweth the coals in the fire, and that bringeth forth an instrument for his work; and I have created the destroyer to destroy.

## watchers → besiegers — Jeremiah 4:16
*(from batch_0109_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "keepers" inverts the sense — "watchers" here denotes attacking scouts/besiegers (an aggressive military party from a far country), while "keepers" connotes protectors, the opposite valence. Darby attests "besiegers," matching the context of a hostile force approaching Jerusalem.
- NEW: Make ye mention to the nations; behold, publish against Jerusalem, that besiegers come from a far country, and give out their voice against the cities of Judah.

## watchful → awake — Revelation 3:2
*(from batch_0109_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "Be watch" is ungrammatical. Geneva1599 and Tyndale both read "Be awake," a period-attested imperative with the same sense.
- NEW: Be awake, and strengthen the things which remain, that are ready to die: for I have not found thy works perfect before God.

## watchtower → tower — Isaiah 21:5
*(from batch_0109_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "watch in the watch" duplicates the verb "watch" with the flagged noun's replacement. "tower" is a common, plain noun that avoids the repetition while preserving the sense (a tower for watching).
- NEW: Prepare the table, watch in the tower, eat, drink: arise, ye princes, and anoint the shield.

## watchtower → tower — Isaiah 21:8
*(from batch_0109_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: For consistency with Isaiah 21:5 (same oracle, same word); "tower" is common and unambiguous.
- NEW: And he cried, A lion: My lord, I stand continually upon the tower in the daytime, and I am set in my ward whole nights:

## watercourse → spring — II Chronicles 32:30
*(from batch_0109_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "the upper going of Gihon" is ungrammatical. ASV/ACV render "spring," a common word matching the context of Hezekiah's water project.
- NEW: This same Hezekiah also stopped the upper spring of Gihon, and brought it straight down to the west side of the city of David. And Hezekiah prospered in all his works.

## watercourse → channel — Job 38:25
*(from batch_0109_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "divided a going for the overflowing of waters" is ungrammatical. ASV/ACV/Darby render "channel," a common, clear word.
- NEW: Who hath divided a channel for the overflowing of waters, or a way for the lightning of thunder;

## wateredst → didst water — Deuteronomy 11:10
*(from batch_0110_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's bare "water" breaks the second-person past-tense parallel with "sowedst." Webster's periphrastic "didst sow... and water" shows this construction is acceptable; here restoring "didst water" keeps the clause unambiguously past-tense and second-person.
- NEW: For the land, whither thou goest in to possess it, is not as the land of Egypt, from whence ye came out, where thou sowedst thy seed, and didst water it with thy foot, as a garden of herbs:

## waterflood → flood — Psalms 69:15
*(from batch_0110_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Matches YLT/UKJV "flood"; common word, no inversion.
- NEW: Let not the flood overflow me, neither let the deep swallow me up, and let not the pit shut her mouth upon me.

## waterpot → pitcher — John 4:28
*(from batch_0110_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "pitcher" is a common KJV vessel word (e.g. Mark 14:13); meaning preserved.
- NEW: The woman then left her pitcher, and went her way into the city, and saith to the men,

## waterpots → vessels — John 2:6
*(from batch_0110_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Matches Darby's "stone water-vessels"; common word; kept consistent with John 2:7 below.
- NEW: And there were set there six vessels of stone, after the manner of the purifying of the Jews, containing two or three firkins apiece.

## waterpots → vessels — John 2:7
*(from batch_0110_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Consistent with the John 2:6 fix in this same passage; common word.
- NEW: Jesus saith unto them, Fill the vessels with water. And they filled them up to the brim.

## waterspouts → floods — Psalms 42:7
*(from batch_0110_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "floods" is common and preserves the sense of tumultuous waters; not duplicated with "waves"/"billows" later in the verse.
- NEW: Deep calleth unto deep at the noise of thy floods: all thy waves and thy billows are gone over me.

## watersprings → fountains — Psalms 107:33
*(from batch_0110_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Matches YLT/UKJV "fountains" exactly; common word.
- NEW: He turneth rivers into a wilderness, and the fountains into dry ground;

## watersprings → fountains — Psalms 107:35
*(from batch_0110_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Matches UKJV "fountains" exactly; common word.
- NEW: He turneth the wilderness into a standing water, and dry ground into fountains.

## wavereth → doubteth — James 1:6
*(from batch_0110_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "he that maketh is like a wave" is nonsensical. ASV renders "he that doubteth is like the surge of the sea," matching this verse's other flagged word ("wavering"→"doubting," below) in the same cognate-repetition pattern the source text itself uses.
- NEW: But let him ask in faith, nothing doubting. For he that doubteth is like a wave of the sea driven with the wind and tossed.

## wavering → doubting — Hebrews 10:23
*(from batch_0110_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Common word, meaning preserved, no duplication.
- NEW: Let us hold fast the profession of our faith without doubting; (for he is faithful that promised;)

## wavering → doubting — James 1:6
*(from batch_0110_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Matches ASV "nothing doubting" exactly; pairs intentionally with the wavereth→doubteth fix above in this same verse, mirroring ASV's full-verse rendering.
- NEW: But let him ask in faith, nothing doubting. For he that doubteth is like a wave of the sea driven with the wind and tossed.

## waymarks → signs — Jeremiah 31:21
*(from batch_0110_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Matches Geneva1599 "Set thee vp signes" verbatim; strong period attestation.
- NEW: Set thee up signs, make thee high heaps: set thine heart toward the highway, even the way which thou wentest: turn again, O virgin of Israel, turn again to these thy cities.

## wayside → way — I Samuel 4:13
*(from batch_0110_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "by the hand watching" is nonsensical. "way" (plain, common) preserves the sense of Eli sitting by the roadside, echoed by Wycliffe "ayens the weie."
- NEW: And when he came, lo, Eli sat upon a seat by the way watching: for his heart trembled for the ark of God. And when the man came into the city, and told it, all the city cried out.

## wayside → way — Psalms 140:5
*(from batch_0110_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same fix as I Samuel 4:13, for consistency; "by the way" is common and grammatical, unlike CUR's "by the hand."
- NEW: The proud have hid a snare for me, and cords; they have spread a net by the way; they have set gins for me. Selah.

## weaken → didst lay low — Isaiah 14:12
*(from batch_0110_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "didst away the nations" is ungrammatical. ASV attests "didst lay low the nations," a verb phrase using common words that preserves the sense of subduing nations.
- NEW: How art thou fallen from heaven, O Lucifer, son of the morning! how art thou cut down to the ground, which didst lay low the nations!

## weakeneth → looseth — Job 12:21
*(from batch_0110_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "draweth the strength" is a meaning error (draweth ≠ weakens). ASV renders "looseth the belt of the strong"; "looseth" alone (keeping "the strength of the mighty," which is not flagged) is a common KJV verb that correctly conveys weakening.
- NEW: He poureth contempt upon princes, and looseth the strength of the mighty.

## weakeneth → maketh feeble — Jeremiah 38:4
*(from batch_0110_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "draweth the hands" is a meaning error. YLT renders "making feeble the hands," and "feeble" is a very common KJV word.
- NEW: Therefore the princes said unto the king, We beseech thee, let this man be put to death: for thus he maketh feeble the hands of the men of war that remain in this city, and the hands of all the people, in speaking such words unto them: for this man seeketh not the welfare of this people, but the hurt.

## wealthy → watered — Psalms 66:12
*(from batch_0110_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Matches YLT "a watered place" verbatim; a plausible image of abundance/refreshment, not an inversion of the sense of prosperity.
- NEW: Thou hast caused men to ride over our heads; we went through fire and through water: but thou broughtest us out into a watered place.

## wealthy → at ease — Jeremiah 49:31
*(from batch_0110_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "unto the ease nation" misuses a noun as an adjective. ASV/DRC/Darby render "the nation that is at ease," which fits the surrounding "dwelleth without care."
- NEW: Arise, get you up unto the nation that is at ease, that dwelleth without care, saith the Lord, which have neither gates nor bars, which dwell alone.

## wearieth → ladeth — Job 37:11
*(from batch_0110_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "he laboureth the thick cloud" is unclear (laboureth is normally intransitive). ASV renders "he ladeth the thick cloud with moisture"; "laden/lade" is a common KJV word (Matthew 11:28).
- NEW: Also by watering he ladeth the thick cloud: he scattereth his bright cloud:

## wearieth → vexeth — Ecclesiastes 10:15
*(from batch_0110_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "The labour... laboureth" repeats the same root as its own subject, an awkward duplication. "vexeth" is a common KJV verb (Judges, Psalms) conveying the sense of wearying trouble.
- NEW: The labour of the foolish vexeth every one of them, because he knoweth not how to go to the city.

## wearisome → nights of misery — Job 7:3
*(from batch_0110_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "labour nights" misuses a noun as an adjective. YLT renders "nights of misery," using common words and preserving the meaning.
- NEW: So am I made to possess months of vanity, and nights of misery are appointed to me.

## weave → make — Isaiah 19:9
*(from batch_0110_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "they that like networks" is nonsensical. CUR's problem word aside, using "work" (as Geneva/RWebster suggest) would duplicate "work" already used earlier in this verse ("they that work in fine flax"); "make" is common, distinct, and preserves the sense.
- NEW: Moreover they that work in fine flax, and they that make networks, shall be confounded.

## weave → spin — Isaiah 59:5
*(from batch_0110_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "and like the spider's web" is nonsensical. All witnesses use "weave" (the flagged word itself), so a genuinely different verb is needed; "spin" is the common, plain English verb specifically associated with spiders' webs.
- NEW: They hatch cockatrice’ eggs, and spin the spider’s web: he that eateth of their eggs dieth, and that which is crushed breaketh out into a viper.

## weaver → webster — Exodus 35:35
*(from batch_0110_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "of the like, even of them that do any work" drops the craftsman category entirely. "webster" is the genuine period English word for a weaver (root of the surname); it names the craft without duplicating "work," which already saturates this verse.
- NEW: Them hath he filled with wisdom of heart, to work all manner of work, of the engraver, and of the cunning workman, and of the embroiderer, in blue, and in purple, in scarlet, and in fine linen, and of the webster, even of them that do any work, and of those that devise cunning work.

## weaver → webster — Isaiah 38:12
*(from batch_0110_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "I have cut off like a like my life" is a blatant duplication error. "webster," consistent with the Exodus 35:35 fix, restores the weaving-trade image without repeating a word.
- NEW: Mine age is departed, and is removed from me as a shepherd’s tent: I have cut off like a webster my life: he will cut me off with pining sickness: from day even to night wilt thou make an end of me.

## weavest → plaitest — Judges 16:13
*(from batch_0110_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Matches Geneva1599 "plattedst"/DRC "plattest" closely; well-attested period word, correct second-person inflection.
- NEW: And Delilah said unto Samson, Hitherto thou hast mocked me, and told me lies: tell me wherewith thou mightest be bound. And he said unto her, If thou plaitest the seven locks of my head with the web.

## wedlock → commit adultery — Ezekiel 16:38
*(from batch_0110_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "break adultery" is not idiomatic English (one commits, not breaks, adultery). Darby renders "women that commit adultery and shed blood," a clear common-word phrase.
- NEW: And I will judge thee, as women that commit adultery and shed blood are judged; and I will give thee blood in fury and jealousy.

## weeds → flags — Jonah 2:5
*(from batch_0110_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "flags" is a genuine KJV word for water plants (Exodus 2:3, 2:5); well attested, common, preserves meaning.
- NEW: The waters compassed me about, even to the soul: the depth closed me round about, the flags were wrapped about my head.

## weightier → greater — Matthew 23:23
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "heavy" drops the comparative sense (more important, not merely "heavy"). "greater" is a common KJV word that correctly conveys the comparative "more important matters of the law."
- NEW: Woe unto you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! for ye pay tithe of mint and anise and cummin, and have omitted the greater matters of the law, judgment, mercy, and faith: these ought ye to have done, and not to leave the other undone.

## weighty → grievous — Proverbs 27:3
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's second "heavy" duplicates "A stone is heavy" earlier in the same verse (rule 12). "grievous" is a common KJV word paired with burdens (Matthew 23:4, "heavy burdens and grievous to be borne") and avoids the repetition while keeping the sense of a hard-to-bear weight.
- NEW: A stone is heavy, and the sand grievous; but a fool’s wrath is heavier than them both.

## weighty → heavy — II Corinthians 10:10
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "heavy and powerful" reasonably conveys forceful, serious letters; common word, no duplication, no inversion of meaning.
- NEW: For his letters, say they, are heavy and powerful; but his bodily presence is weak, and his speech contemptible.

## wellfavoured → beautiful — Nahum 3:4
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "loved" changes the nuance (loved ≠ attractive); Geneva1599/DRC both render "beautiful," matching the sense of an attractive harlot who lures nations.
- NEW: Because of the multitude of the whoredoms of the beautiful harlot, the mistress of witchcrafts, that selleth nations through her whoredoms, and families through her witchcrafts.

## wellpleasing → pleasing — Philippians 4:18
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Matches DRC "pleasing to God" exactly; common word.
- NEW: But I have all, and abound: I am full, having received of Epaphroditus the things which were sent from you, an odour of a sweet smell, a sacrifice acceptable, pleasing to God.

## wellpleasing → pleasing — Hebrews 13:21
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Matches Darby's sense "pleasing before him"; common word.
- NEW: Make you perfect in every good work to do his will, working in you that which is pleasing in his sight, through Jesus Christ; to whom be glory for ever and ever. Amen.

## wellspring → fountain — Proverbs 16:22
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Matches YLT/DRC "fountain" exactly; common word.
- NEW: Understanding is a fountain of life unto him that hath it: but the instruction of fools is folly.

## wellspring → fountain — Proverbs 18:4
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Matches YLT/DRC "fountain" exactly; common word.
- NEW: The words of a man’s mouth are as deep waters, and the fountain of wisdom as a flowing brook.

## wen → running sore — Leviticus 22:22
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "having a have" is a blatant duplication error. RWebster attests "running sore," a plain, clear phrase preserving the sense of a blemish disqualifying an offering.
- NEW: Blind, or broken, or maimed, or having a running sore, or scurvy, or scabbed, ye shall not offer these unto the Lord, nor make an offering by fire of them upon the altar unto the Lord.

## wench → maidservant — II Samuel 17:17
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "handmaid" implies a personal servant of a specific master, which is not the sense here (a generic serving-girl carrying messages). Webster/RWebster/YLT render "maidservant," which is precise and common.
- NEW: Now Jonathan and Ahimaaz stayed by En–rogel; for they might not be seen to come into the city: and a maidservant went and told them; and they went and told king David.

## western → west — Numbers 34:6
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: The repetition of "west border" at the start and end of the verse is itself attested in YLT ("As to the west border... this is to you the west border"), mirroring the Hebrew's own repetitive style; not a forbidden duplication but a faithful echo.
- NEW: And as for the west border, ye shall even have the great sea for a border: this shall be your west border.

## whale → dragon — Job 7:12
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Matches YLT "sea-monster... or a dragon" exactly; well attested.
- NEW: Am I a sea, or a dragon, that thou settest a watch over me?

## whale → dragon — Ezekiel 32:2
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Matches Geneva1599/DRC/YLT "dragon in the sea(s)" exactly; strong period attestation.
- NEW: Son of man, take up a lamentation for Pharaoh king of Egypt, and say unto him, Thou art like a young lion of the nations, and thou art as a dragon in the seas: and thou camest forth with thy rivers, and troubledst the waters with thy feet, and fouledst their rivers.

## whale's → great fish's — Matthew 12:40
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR left "whale's" unchanged, but the word is flagged and must go. RWebster/Darby render "the great fish('s) belly," a common, clear substitute.
- NEW: For as Jonas was three days and three nights in the great fish’s belly; so shall the Son of man be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth.

## whales → fish — Genesis 1:21
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "great fish" is common, plain, and a period-natural way to describe great sea creatures (compare Jonah's "great fish"); no inversion of the creation account's meaning.
- NEW: And God created great fish, and every living creature that moveth, which the waters brought forth abundantly, after their kind, and every winged fowl after his kind: and God saw that it was good.

## wheaten → wheat — Exodus 29:2
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Matches Webster/RWebster "wheat flour" exactly; common word.
- NEW: And unleavened bread, and cakes unleavened tempered with oil, and wafers unleavened anointed with oil: of wheat flour shalt thou make them.

## whereabout → about which — I Samuel 21:2
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "where I send thee" is grammatically odd for "about which." Webster/RWebster render "about which I send thee," an exact common-word fix.
- NEW: And David said unto Ahimelech the priest, The king hath commanded me a business, and hath said unto me, Let no man know any thing of the business about which I send thee, and what I have commanded thee: and I have appointed my servants to such and such a place.

## whereinsoever → in whatever respect — II Corinthians 11:21
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "through any is bold" is ungrammatical. Webster/RWebster render "in whatever respect any is bold," a clear, grammatical fix using common words.
- NEW: I speak as concerning reproach, as though we had been weak. Howbeit in whatever respect any is bold, (I speak foolishly,) I am bold also.

## whip → scourge — Proverbs 26:3
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "scourge" is common in the KJV and preserves the sense; no duplication.
- NEW: A scourge for the horse, a bridle for the ass, and a rod for the fool’s back.

## whip → scourge — Nahum 3:2
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Common word, meaning preserved.
- NEW: The noise of a scourge, and the noise of the rattling of the wheels, and of the pransing horses, and of the jumping chariots.

## whirleth → turneth about — Ecclesiastes 1:6
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "walketh" wrongly personifies the wind as walking. Darby/ASV both render "turneth about continually," deliberately repeating "turneth about" from earlier in the same verse — an attested cognate repetition mirroring the Hebrew's own repetitive style (compare the wavereth/wavering precedent in this batch set).
- NEW: The wind goeth toward the south, and turneth about unto the north; it turneth about continually, and the wind returneth again according to his circuits.

## whisper → murmur — Psalms 41:7
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Common word, plausible synonym for secretive hostile talk, no duplication.
- NEW: All that hate me murmur together against me: against me do they devise my hurt.

## whisper → murmur — Isaiah 29:4
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Close to DRC "shall mutter"; common word.
- NEW: And thou shalt be brought down, and shalt speak out of the ground, and thy speech shall be low out of the dust, and thy voice shall be, as of one that hath a familiar spirit, out of the ground, and thy speech shall murmur out of the dust.

## whispered → murmured — II Samuel 12:19
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Common word conveying hushed, uneasy talk among the servants; not a meaning inversion given the context (they were withholding bad news).
- NEW: But when David saw that his servants murmured, David perceived that the child was dead: therefore David said unto his servants, Is the child dead? And they said, He is dead.

## whisperer → talebearer — Proverbs 16:28
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Matches Geneva1599 "tale teller"/YLT/Darby "tale-bearer" closely; strong attestation.
- NEW: A froward man soweth strife: and a talebearer separateth chief friends.

## whisperers → murmurers — Romans 1:29
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Common word, fits the vice list, no duplication.

## whiter → purer — Psalms 51:7
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "white than snow" (missing the comparative -er) is ungrammatical. "purer" is common (echoing "I shall be clean" earlier in the verse) and restores correct comparative grammar.
- NEW: Purge me with hyssop, and I shall be clean: wash me, and I shall be purer than snow.

## whiter → clearer — Lamentations 4:7
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "white than milk" is ungrammatical, and "purer" cannot be reused here since "purer than snow" already opens this same verse (would duplicate). "clearer" is a common word that supplies the needed comparative without repetition.
- NEW: Her Nazarites were purer than snow, they were clearer than milk, they were more ruddy in body than rubies, their polishing was of sapphire:

*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*

- rationale: CUR's "healed" changes the sense (a soothing/healing tongue, not a tongue that has itself been healed). ASV/Darby render "gentle tongue," a common KJV word that clearly conveys the intended soothing quality.
- NEW: A gentle tongue is a tree of life: but perverseness therein is a breach in the spirit.

## wholesome → sound — I Timothy 6:3
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: Matches YLT/ASV/Darby "sound words" exactly; common word.
- NEW: If any man teach otherwise, and consent not to sound words, even the words of our Lord Jesus Christ, and to the doctrine which is according to godliness;

## whoremonger → fornicator — Ephesians 5:5
*(from batch_0111_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: "no lewd, nor unclean person" misuses an adjective as a noun. ASV/DRC render "fornicator," a common KJV word (Hebrews 12:16, 1 Corinthians 5:9-11).
- NEW: For this ye know, that no fornicator, nor unclean person, nor covetous man, who is an idolater, hath any inheritance in the kingdom of Christ and of God.

*(from batch_0112_proposals.md)*

- rationale: "wideness" must be replaced (rule 3); "breadth" is common in KJV.db (81 hits) and matches Wycliffe "breede" and DRC "breadth" directly.
- NEW: And between the chambers was the breadth of twenty cubits round about the house on every side.

*(from batch_0112_proposals.md)*

Following rule 8 (copy the witness construction, not just the word), Geneva and Darby both render this "stormy wind" rather than "windy storm" — reordering the noun phrase around the common word "wind" (118 hits) and "stormy" (4 hits) preserves the sense without a bare re-inflection of "windy."

## wink → make a sign — Psalms 35:19
*(from batch_0112_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "formed" is ungrammatical ("neither let them formed with the eye"). ## winked → forbore — Acts 17:30
*(from batch_0112_proposals.md)*

- rationale: CUR is ungrammatical ("God forbore at" — "forbore" does not take "at"). RWebster/YLT/Darby/ASV all render this clause "overlooked," but "overlooked" has zero KJV.db occurrences and would itself become a new rare word (rule 4). "suffered" is common in KJV.db (50 hits) and fits the sense of permitting/tolerating exactly, matching Tyndale and Geneva1599's own rendering of this clause, "God regarded not" (i.e. let pass without judgment).
- NEW: And the times of this ignorance God suffered; but now commandeth all men every where to repent:

## winketh → maketh signs — Proverbs 6:13
*(from batch_0112_proposals.md)*

- rationale: CUR's "moveth" ("He moveth with his eyes") loses the specific malicious gesture. Geneva1599 uses the construction "maketh a signe with his eyes" for this verse (rule 8); the common word "signs" (53 hits) is used, matching ASV's own parallel clause "maketh signs with his fingers" later in the verse without duplicating it.
- NEW: He maketh signs with his eyes, he speaketh with his feet, he teacheth with his fingers;

## winketh → maketh a sign — Proverbs 10:10
*(from batch_0112_proposals.md)*

## winneth → getteth — Proverbs 11:30
*(from batch_0112_proposals.md)*

- rationale: CUR's "taketh" risks inverting the verse's positive sense (winning/gaining souls to wisdom) toward "taking" lives, which would contradict rule 5. "getteth" (8 hits) is common in the corpus and preserves the positive sense of acquiring/gaining, consistent with DRC's "gaineth souls."
- NEW: The fruit of the righteous is a tree of life; and he that getteth souls is wise.

## winnowed → scattered — Isaiah 30:24

## wretched → miserable — Romans 7:24

- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "miserable" is attested (3 hits) and preserves the sense of DRC's "unhappy man," with no meaning inversion.
- NEW: O miserable man that I am! who shall deliver me from the body of this death?

## wretched → vile — Revelation 3:17
*(from batch_0114_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "vile" is common (20 hits) and preserves the sense of worthlessness; "miserable" remains distinct later in the same verse, so no duplication occurs.
- NEW: Because thou sayest, I am rich, and increased with goods, and have need of nothing; and knowest not that thou art vile, and miserable, and poor, and blind, and naked:

## wretchedness → misery — Numbers 11:15
*(from batch_0114_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "wickedness" inverts the sense of the verse (rule 5) — Moses is asking not to witness his own suffering, not confessing sin. "misery" is attested (7 hits) and matches Geneva1599's own rendering, "let me not see my miserie," directly.
- NEW: And if thou deal thus with me, kill me, I pray thee, out of hand, if I have found favour in thy sight; and let me not see my misery.

*(from batch_0114_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "furrows" is attested (8 hits) and preserves the image of lines/creases from affliction; no meaning inversion.
- NEW: And thou hast filled me with furrows, which is a witness against me: and my leanness rising up in me beareth witness to my face.

*(from batch_0114_proposals.md)*

- rationale: CUR left the flagged word "writer's" unchanged in the verse text despite the header. YLT renders this clause "a scribe's inkhorn," which is adopted; "scribe" is common (55 hits).
- NEW: And, behold, six men came from the way of the higher gate, which lieth toward the north, and every man a slaughter weapon in his hand; and one man among them was clothed with linen, with a scribe's inkhorn by his side: and they went in, and stood beside the brasen altar.

*(from batch_0114_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE

## yearn → were moved — Genesis 43:30
*(from batch_0114_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "his bowels did black upon his brother" is nonsensical. Following rule 2 (auxiliary must agree with the new word's part of speech), Wycliffe, YLT, and DRC all render this "his bowels...moved"; "were moved" (moved: 78 hits) is adopted with the auxiliary changed from "did" to "were" to fit the past participle.
- NEW: And Joseph made haste; for his bowels were moved upon his brother: and he sought where to weep; and he entered into his chamber, and wept there.

## yearned → were kindled — I Kings 3:26
*(from batch_0114_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "her bowels kindled upon her son" is missing the auxiliary needed for the passive sense (rule 2). Geneva1599 renders this "her compassion was kindled toward her sonne"; "were kindled" (kindled: 68 hits) is adopted, keeping the verse's own subject "bowels" with the correct passive auxiliary.
- NEW: Then spake the woman whose the living child was unto the king, for her bowels were kindled upon her son, and she said, O my lord, give her the living child, and in no wise slay it. But the other said, Let it be neither mine nor thine, but divide it.

## yell → howl — Jeremiah 51:38
*(from batch_0114_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "they shall roar as lions' whelps" duplicates "roar" already used earlier in the same verse (rule 12). "howl" is attested (28 hits) and provides a genuinely distinct word for the animal cry, avoiding the duplication while preserving the sense of Geneva1599's "yell as the lyons whelpes."
- NEW: They shall roar together like lions: they shall howl as lions' whelps.

## yelled → gave forth their voice — Jeremiah 2:15
*(from batch_0114_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "roared upon him, and delivered" is nonsensical. "roared" is already used at the start of this same verse, so a plain synonym for roaring would duplicate it (rule 12); instead, Darby and YLT's own construction "gave forth their voice" is adopted, avoiding the duplication entirely.
- NEW: The young lions roared upon him, and gave forth their voice, and they made his land waste: his cities are burned without inhabitant.

## yokefellow → companion — Philippians 4:3
*(from batch_0114_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "companion" is attested (14 hits) and preserves the sense of a close partner in labor found in DRC's "my sincere companion."
- NEW: And I intreat thee also, true companion, help those women which laboured with me in the gospel, with Clement also, and with other my fellowlabourers, whose names are in the book of life.

## youthful → young — II Timothy 2:22
*(from batch_0114_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "fleshly lusts" shifts the verse's sense from age-typical impulsiveness to carnal/sexual sin, a meaningful doctrinal-category change (flesh vs. spirit) rather than a plain synonym swap. "young" is extremely common (297 hits) and preserves the original age-based sense of "youthful" without introducing that theological drift.
- NEW: Flee also young lusts: but follow righteousness, faith, charity, peace, with them that call on the Lord out of a pure heart.

- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "earnestly" is attested (17 hits) and preserves the sense of ardent pursuit; no meaning inversion.
- NEW: They earnestly affect you, but not well; yea, they would exclude you, that ye might affect them.

*(from batch_0114_proposals.md)*
